Ayahs

تَنزِيلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ (١)
The [gradual] sending down of the Book is from Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise. (1)
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ فَٱعْبُدِ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصًا لَّهُ ٱلدِّينَ (٢)
Indeed We have sent down the Book to you with the truth; so worship Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him. (2)
أَلَا لِلَّهِ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْخَالِصُ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ مَا نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونَآ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ زُلْفَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ فِى مَا هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَنْ هُوَ كَـٰذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ (٣)
Indeed, only exclusive faith is worthy of Allah, and those who take other as awliya besides Him [claiming,] ‘We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah,’ Allah will judge between them concerning that about which they differ. Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a liar and an ingrate. (3)

Content of Surah az-Zumar

This Surah was revealed in Mecca, and for this reason, it speaks more than anything else about matters related to Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and the importance of the Quran and the status of the Prophet of Islam's ص prophethood. This is the common characteristic of Meccan Surahs.

The Meccan period was a time for building the Muslims in terms of the foundation of religious beliefs and the pillars of faith; therefore, the most powerful and effective discussions in this field are reflected in the Meccan Surahs. It was this firm foundation that produced its magnificent effects in Medina, in the battles, in confronting the enemy, against the sabotage of the hypocrites, and in the acceptance of the Islamic system. If we wish to know the secret of the Muslims' rapid victory in Medina, we must study the effective teachings of Mecca.

In any case, this Surah consists of several important sections:

1- What is reflected more than anything else throughout this Surah is the issue of the call to pure Monotheism (Tawhid), recommended in all its dimensions and branches: Monotheism in Creation (Khaliqiyyah), Monotheism in Lordship (Rububiyyah), and Monotheism in Worship ('Ibadah). Specifically, the issue of sincerity (Ikhlas) in worship and servitude to Allah is emphasized repeatedly in various verses of this Surah, and its expressions in this regard are so effective that they draw and attract the human heart toward sincerity.

2- Another important issue addressed at various points in this Surah, from almost the beginning to the end, is the issue of "Resurrection" and the great court of Allah's justice; the matter of reward and punishment, the heavenly chambers, and the fiery canopies of Hell; the issue of the fear and terror of the Day of Judgment, the manifestation of the results of deeds, and the appearance of the deeds themselves in that great scene; the issue of the faces of liars and those who fabricated lies against Allah turning black; the driving of the disbelievers toward Hell and the reproach and rebuke of the angels of punishment toward them; and the invitation of the people of Paradise toward Paradise and the congratulations and greetings of the angels of mercy to them! These issues, revolving around the Resurrection, are so intertwined with Monotheistic matters that they seem to form the warp and weft of a single fabric.

3- Another part of this Surah, which occupies only a short section, is the importance of the Glorious Quran; however, this short section contains a beautiful depiction of the Quran and its powerful impact on hearts and souls.

4- Another section, which is also relatively short, describes the fate of past nations and the painful Divine punishment for those who denied the signs of Truth.

5- Finally, a part of this Surah is about the issue of repentance (Tawbah) and the doors of return to Allah being open. The most effective verses regarding repentance and mercy are stated in this section, such that there may not be a more promising verse in the Quran in this regard.

This Surah is famously known as Surah "az-Zumar," a name taken from verses 71 and 73 of this Surah. It is sometimes called Surah "al-Ghuraf" due to verse 20, but this name is not well-known.

"Virtue of Surah az-Zumar"

In Islamic traditions, extraordinary importance has been given to the recitation of this Surah. Among them, we read in a tradition from the Prophet of Islam ص:

من قرء سورة الزمر لم يقطع اللَّه رجاه، و اعطاه ثواب الخائفين الذين خافوا اللَّه تعالى:

"Whoever recites Surah az-Zumar, Allah will not cut off his hope (from His mercy), and He will grant him the reward of those who fear Allah the Almighty." [^1] In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated:

من قرء سورة الزمر اعطاه اللَّه شرف الدنيا و الآخرة، و اعزه بلا مال و لا عشيرة، حتى يهابه من يراه و حرم جسده على النار:

"Whoever recites Surah az-Zumar, Allah will grant him the honor of this world and the Hereafter, and will grant him might and dignity without wealth or tribe, such that whoever sees him will hold him in awe, and He will forbid his body to the fire of Hell." [^2]

Comparing the above virtues with the content of this Surah regarding fear of the Lord, hope in His mercy, sincerity in servitude, and absolute submission to the Pure Essence of Truth, clearly shows that these rewards belong to those who make "recitation" a prelude to "contemplation," and "contemplation" a means for "faith and action." In other words: the content of this Surah is implemented within their souls, and its manifestation becomes apparent in their entire lives. Yes, such individuals are worthy of such a great reward and the vast mercy of the Lord.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, beginning of Surah az-Zumar. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Thawab al-A'mal, and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ (1) إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ فَاعْبُدِ اللَّهَ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ (2) أَلا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخالِصُ وَ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلاَّ لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفى‌ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ فِي ما هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ كاذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ (3)

Commentary

Purify and Sincerize Your Religion from Any Form of Shirk!

This Surah begins with two verses regarding the revelation of the Glorious Quran. In one verse, the "Origin of Revelation" of the Quran—namely the Pure Essence of Allah—is discussed, and in the other verse, the "Content and Goal" of the Quran.

First, it says: "The revelation of the Book is from Allah, the Almighty, the All-Wise" (تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللَّهِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَكِيمِ). [^1] Every book must be known by its sender or author. When we know that this great Heavenly Book originates from the knowledge of a Powerful and Wise God, for whom nothing is difficult against His infinite power and no matter is hidden from His boundless knowledge, we realize the greatness of its content. Without further explanation, we become certain that its content is Truth and entirely wisdom, light, and guidance.

Furthermore, such expressions at the beginning of Quranic Surahs draw the believers' attention to the fact that what they find in this great Book is the Word of Allah, not the word of the Prophet ص, even though his word is also sublime and wise.

[^1]: "تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ" is the predicate (khabar) for an omitted subject (mubtada), and the estimation is: "هذا تنزيل الكتاب" (This is the revelation of the Book). Some commentators have also suggested that "تنزيل الكتاب" is the subject and "من اللَّه" is its predicate, but the first possibility seems more correct. Additionally, "تنزيل" is an infinitive used as a passive participle and is of the type of adding an attribute to the described noun, meaning "This is a Book revealed from Allah...".

Then, addressing the content of this Heavenly Book and its goal, it says: "Indeed, We have sent down the Book to you in truth" (إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ).

There is nothing but "Truth" in it, and you will observe no matter but "Truth" within it. For this reason, seekers of truth follow it, and those thirsty in the wilderness of reality search for its content.

And since the goal of its revelation is to provide pure religion to humanity, it adds at the end of the verse: Now that this is the case, "so worship Allah, making the religion pure for Him" (فَاعْبُدِ اللَّهَ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ).

It is possible that "religion" (din) here refers to the worship of Allah, because before it, the command to worship is given with the phrase "فَاعْبُدِ اللَّهَ". Therefore, the following part, "مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ", states the condition for the validity of worship—namely sincerity, being free from any form of polytheism (shirk), ostentation (riya), or anything other than Allah.

Nevertheless, the breadth of the concept of "religion" and the absence of any restriction or condition in it conveys a broader meaning that includes worship, other actions, and beliefs. In other words, "religion" encompasses the entirety of man's spiritual and material life. The sincere servants of Allah must make all aspects of their lives pure for Him; they must remove everything other than Him from the house of the heart, the scene of the soul, the field of action, and the circle of speech. They should think of Him, love for His sake, speak of Him, act for Him, and always walk the path of His pleasure—for this is the "sincerity of religion."

Therefore, limiting the concept of the verse to the testimony of "La ilaha illa Allah" or specifically to "worship and obedience" is neither necessary nor supported by clear evidence.

In the next verse, it emphasizes the issue of "sincerity" once again, saying: "Unquestionably, for Allah is the pure religion!" (أَلا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخالِصُ).

This expression can carry two meanings:

First: That which Allah accepts is only the pure religion, and only unconditional submission to His command. Any form of polytheism, ostentation, or mixing Divine laws with anything else is rejected and discarded.

Second: Pure religion and creed must be taken only from Allah, because whatever is manufactured and fashioned by human thoughts is inadequate and mixed with error and mistake.

However, considering what was mentioned under the previous verse, the first meaning seems more appropriate, because there, the agents of sincerity are the servants; therefore, the purity in the verse under discussion must also be observed from their side.

Another witness to this is a tradition narrated from the Noble Prophet ص that a man came to him and said:

"O Messenger of Allah! We give our wealth seeking fame; do we have any reward?" The Messenger of Allah ص said, "No." He said, "O Messenger of Allah! We give seeking both reward and fame; do we have any reward?" The Prophet ص said: "Indeed, Allah the Almighty does not accept anything unless it is pure for Him," then the Messenger of Allah ص recited this verse: "أَلا لِلَّهِ الدِّينُ الْخالِصُ" [^1]

[^1]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 23, p. 212 (under the verses discussed).

In any case, this verse is actually a statement of the reason for the previous verse. There it says: Worship Allah with sincerity, and here it adds: Know that Allah accepts only pure action.

In the verses of the Quran and Islamic traditions, the issue of sincerity is heavily emphasized. The beginning of the phrase under discussion with "ألا" (Unquestionably/Behold), which is usually said to draw attention, is another sign of the importance of this subject.

Then, it proceeds to invalidate the weak and hollow logic of the polytheists who abandoned the path of sincerity and wandered in the bypass of polytheism, saying: "And those who take protectors besides Him [say], 'We only worship them that they may bring us nearer to Allah in station.' Indeed, Allah will judge between them concerning that over which they differ" and it is there that the corruption and ruin of their deeds and thoughts will become apparent to everyone (وَ الَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفى‌ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ فِي ما هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ). [^1] This verse is actually a decisive threat to the polytheists that on the Day of Judgment—the day when differences are resolved and realities are revealed—He will judge between them and bring them to the punishment for their deeds, in addition to them being disgraced before everyone in the scene of the Gathering.

Here, the logic of the idolaters is clearly stated.

To explain:

Some believe that one of the origins of idol-worship is that a group, in their own imagination, considered the Pure Essence of Allah too great for our intellect and thought to reach Him.

[^1]: It is clear that in the above verse, before the phrase "ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ", a phrase is implied: "They say: We do not worship them..."

Based on this, they considered Him too sanctified for us to worship Him directly. Therefore, they believed we must turn to those to whom the Lordship and management of this world have been entrusted by Allah, and make them intermediaries between ourselves and Him.

They accepted and worshipped them as "lords" and gods so that they might bring them closer to Allah. These were the angels, the jinn, and generally the sacred beings of the world.

Then, since access to these sacred beings was also not possible, they made images and symbols for them and worshipped them; these were the idols. Because they believed in a kind of unity between these images and the sacred beings, they also considered the idols as their "lords" and gods.

In this way, the gods in their view were those contingent beings created by the God of the universe, and in their imagination, they were those close to the threshold of Truth and the managers of the world's affairs by the command of the Lord. They considered Allah as the "Lord of lords" (God of gods) who is the Creator and Originator of the universe. Otherwise, very few idolaters believed that these stone and wooden idols, or even their imaginary gods like angels and jinn, were the creators and originators of this world. [^1] Of course, idol-worship has other origins as well, including the fact that extraordinary respect for prophets and the righteous sometimes led people to respect their images after their death. Over time, these images took on an independent status, and respect turned into worship. For this reason, the matter of making statues is strictly forbidden in Islam.

[^1]: "Tafsir al-Mizan," Vol. 17, p. 247 (with modifications).

It is also recorded in history that the pre-Islamic Arabs, due to the extraordinary respect they held for the Kaaba and the land of Mecca, would sometimes take pieces of stone from there to other places, which were respected and gradually worshipped.

In any case, these do not contradict what is narrated in the story of "Amr ibn Luhay," that during a trip to Syria he observed scenes of idol-worship and for the first time brought an idol from there to the Hijaz, and the worship of idols became common from that time. Each of what we have mentioned explains one of the roots of idol-worship, and the motivation of the Syrians in worshipping idols also originated from these matters or similar ones.

But in any case, these were all baseless illusions and fantasies emanating from weak minds, diverting people from the authentic path of knowing God.

The Glorious Quran emphasizes this point specifically: that man can contact his God without any intermediary, speak to Him, share secrets and needs, ask for necessities, and request forgiveness and repentance. All of these belong to Him and are within His control and power.

Surah "al-Hamd" expresses this reality, as servants, by reciting this Surah continuously in their daily prayers, establish a direct connection with their Lord, call upon Him, and without any intermediary, request from Him and seek their needs.

The method of seeking forgiveness and repentance in Islamic programs, as well as any request from the Great God—which our narrated supplications are full of—all show that Islam has recognized no intermediary in these matters, and this is the very reality of Monotheism (Tawhid).

Even the issue of intercession (shafa'ah) and seeking recourse (tawassul) to the friends of Allah (Awliya Allah) is conditional upon the permission of the Lord and His leave, and is an emphasis on that same issue of Monotheism.

And such a relationship must exist, for He is closer to us than we are to ourselves, as the Quran says: "And We are closer to him than [his] jugular vein" (Qaf: 16); "And know that Allah intervenes between a man and his heart" (al-Anfal: 24).

Thus, neither is He far from us, nor are we far from Him, such that there would be a need for an intermediary. He is closer to us than anyone else, He is present everywhere, and His place is within our hearts.

Therefore, worshipping intermediaries, whether they be angels, jinn, and the like, or worshipping stone and wooden idols, is a baseless and false act. Furthermore, it is considered ingratitude for the blessings of the Lord, because the Giver of blessings is worthy of worship, not these lifeless beings or those who are entirely in need.

Hence, at the end of the verse, it says: "Indeed, Allah does not guide he who is a liar and [confirmed] disbeliever" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ كاذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ).

Neither guidance to the straight path in this world, nor toward Paradise in the next world, because he himself has provided the means for closing the doors of guidance. For Allah sends His grace of guidance to grounds that are worthy and ready to receive it, not to hearts that have consciously destroyed any readiness within themselves.

Key Point: The Difference Between "Tanzil" and "Inzal"

In the first verse of this Surah, the expression "تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ" (Tanzil of the Book) is used, and in the second verse, the expression "أَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ" (We sent down [Anzalna] the Book to you).

Regarding the difference between "Tanzil" and "Inzal," and the purpose of this variation in expression in these verses: what is derived from some linguistic texts is that "Tanzil" is usually used in cases where something is revealed gradually, while "Inzal" has a general meaning that includes both gradual revelation and instantaneous revelation. [^1] Some have also considered these two as opposites, believing that "Tanzil" is only gradual revelation and "Inzal" is only instantaneous revelation. [^2] Therefore, the variation in expression may be because the Quran has two types of revelation: one is the instantaneous revelation that occurred on the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr) in the holy month of Ramadan, where it was revealed all at once upon the heart of the Noble Prophet of Islam, as the Quran says:

"Indeed, We sent it down [Anzalnahu] on the Night of Power" (al-Qadr: 1); "Indeed, We sent it down [Anzalnahu] on a blessed night" (ad-Dukhan: 3); "The month of Ramadan [is that] in which was revealed [Unzila] the Quran" (al-Baqarah: 185).

In all these cases, the root "Inzal" is used, referring to the instantaneous revelation of the Quran.

The other revelation is that which took place gradually over the 23 years of the Prophet's ص mission, where verses were revealed appropriate to every event and incident, guiding the Muslims step by step through the stages of spiritual, moral, doctrinal, and social perfection.

[^1]: Raghib's Mufradat, root "nzl" (The difference between Inzal and Tanzil in describing the Quran and the angels is that Tanzil is specific to the place that indicates its revelation separately and time after time, while Inzal is general). [^2]: In the Tafsir al-Kabir of Fakhr al-Razi, this difference is narrated from some.

As we read in verse 106 of Surah al-Isra: "And [it is] a Quran which We have divided that you might recite it to the people over a prolonged period. And We have sent it down [Nazzalnahu] gradually."

Interestingly, sometimes both expressions are used in one verse for two purposes, as the Glorious Quran says in verse 20 of Surah Muhammad: "Those who believe say, 'Why has a surah not been sent down [Nuzzilat]?' But when a precise surah is revealed [Unzilat] and fighting is mentioned therein, you see those in whose hearts is disease looking at you with a look of one overcoming by death."

It is as if the believers are requesting the gradual revelation of a Surah to become accustomed to it, but since the gradual revelation of a Surah regarding matters such as Jihad sometimes provided an opportunity for the hypocrites to shirk their responsibility step by step, in such cases, the Surah was revealed all at once.

This is the final point that can be said regarding the difference between these two expressions, and according to it, the verses under discussion refer to both types of revelation, and in this respect, they possess complete comprehensiveness.

However, there are exceptional cases to the above interpretation and difference; for example, in verse 32 of al-Furqan we read: "And those who disbelieve say, 'Why was the Quran not revealed [Nuzzila] to him all at once?' Thus [it is] that We may strengthen thereby your heart. And We have spaced it [Rattalnahu] distinctly."

Of course, each of these two "revelations" has benefits and effects that have been mentioned in their proper place. [^1]

[^1]: Regarding the benefits of the gradual revelation of the Quran, we had a detailed discussion in Vol. 15, p. 81 onwards.

Ayahs

لَّوْ أَرَادَ ٱللَّهُ أَن يَتَّخِذَ وَلَدًا لَّٱصْطَفَىٰ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۖ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٤)
Had Allah intended to take a son, He could have chosen from those He has created whatever He wished. Immaculate is He! He is Allah, the One, the All-paramount. (4)
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ يُكَوِّرُ ٱلَّيْلَ عَلَى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُكَوِّرُ ٱلنَّهَارَ عَلَى ٱلَّيْلِ ۖ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۗ أَلَا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرُ (٥)
He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom. He winds the night over the day, and winds the day over the night, and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term. Indeed, He is the All-mighty, the All-forgiver! (5)

Commentary

He is the Ruler over all things; what need has He for a child?!

In addition to considering idols as intermediaries and intercessors before Allah—which was discussed in the preceding verses—the polytheists held another belief regarding some of their deities, such as the angels, whom they imagined to be the daughters of Allah. The first verse under discussion addresses this ugly delusion, stating: "Had Allah willed to take a son, He could have chosen whatever He willed from what He creates" (لَوْ أَرادَ اللَّهُ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ وَلَداً لَاصْطَفى‌ مِمَّا يَخْلُقُ ما يَشاءُ).

"Glory be to Him! [He is far above] having a son. He is Allah, the One, the All-Subduing" (سُبْحانَهُ هُوَ اللَّهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ).

Commentators have offered various interpretations for the first part of the verse:

Some have said: The meaning is that if Allah had wanted to choose a child, why would He choose daughters, who—according to your own misguided perception—are humans of lesser value? Why would He not choose sons? This is, in fact, a type of argument based on the mindset of the opposing party so that they may realize the baselessness of their own claims.

Others have said the meaning is that if Allah had wanted to have a child, He would have created creatures superior to and better than the angels.

However, considering that the existential value of daughters in the sight of Allah is not less than that of sons, and considering that the angels or Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him)—who, according to the deviant, is the son of God—are very noble and worthy beings, neither of these two interpretations seems appropriate.

It is better to say that the verse intends to convey that a child is typically for "assistance" or "spiritual companionship." Even assuming the impossible—that Allah needed such a thing—a child would not be necessary; rather, He would have selected from among His noble creatures those who could fulfill this purpose. Why would He choose a "child"?

But since He is One, Unique, All-Subduing, and Dominant over all things, Eternal and Everlasting, He neither needs anyone's help, nor can any loneliness be imagined for Him that would be dispelled through companionship with something, nor does He need to continue His lineage. Therefore, He is Glorified and Pure from having a child, whether it be a biological child or an adopted one.

Furthermore, as we have stated before, these mindless individuals—who sometimes imagined angels to be the daughters of Allah, sometimes claimed a kinship between Him and the Jinn, and sometimes introduced the "Messiah" or "Ezra" as the son of Allah—were ignorant of the clear reality that if the intent behind "child" is a literal child, it implies: firstly, being a physical body; secondly, being divisible (since a child is a part of the father's existence that separates from him).

Thirdly, it implies having a likeness and peer (since a child always resembles the father). And fourthly, it implies the need for a spouse. Allah is Pure and Exalted above all these matters.

And if the intent is an "adopted child" (tabanni), that too would be due to a need for physical assistance or emotional companionship and the like, and the All-Powerful, All-Subduing God is independent of all such things. Therefore, the description as "the One" (al-Wahid) and "the All-Subduing" (al-Qahhar) is a concise response to all these possibilities.

In any case, the use of the term "law" (if), which is usually employed for impossible conditions, indicates that it is an impossible assumption for Allah to choose a child. Even if—hypothetically—He had a need, He would not need what they claim; rather, His chosen creatures would fulfill that purpose.

Then, to establish the reality that Allah has no need for His creatures, and also to state signs of His Oneness and Greatness, He says: "He created the heavens and the earth in truth" (خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِ‌).

Their being "in truth" is evidence that a great purpose was at work, which is nothing other than the perfection of beings—with humanity at the forefront—and ultimately leading to the Resurrection.

After mentioning this great creation, He points to a corner of the wondrous governance, the calculated changes, and the magnificent systems governing them, saying: "He wraps the night over the day and wraps the day over the night" (يُكَوِّرُ اللَّيْلَ عَلَى النَّهارِ وَ يُكَوِّرُ النَّهارَ عَلَى اللَّيْلِ‌).

What a fascinating expression! If a person were standing outside the planet Earth, observing the scene of the Earth's axial rotation around itself and the emergence of night and day around it, they would see that, as if continuously, the black ribbon of night is wrapped over the light of day from one side, and the white ribbon of day is wrapped over the darkness of night from the other. Given that "yukawwiru" is derived from "takwir," meaning "to wrap" or "to coil"—and linguists specifically consider the wrapping of a turban around the head as an example of it—the subtle point hidden in this Quranic expression becomes clear. Although many commentators, due to not noticing this point, have mentioned other matters that are not very consistent with the concept of "takwir," the point is that the Earth is spherical and rotates around itself. Due to this rotation, the black ribbon of night and the white ribbon of day constantly revolve around it, as if the white ribbon is being wrapped over the black from one side, and the black over the white from the other.

In any case, the Holy Quran uses various expressions regarding the system of "light" and "darkness" and the emergence of night and day, each pointing to a specific nuance and looking at it from a particular angle:

Sometimes it says: "He causes the night to enter into the day, and He causes the day to enter into the night" (يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ وَ يُكَوِّرُ النَّهارَ فِي اللَّيْلِ‌ - Fatir: 13). Here, the talk is of the stealthy and silent entry of night into day and day into night.

And sometimes it says: "He covers the day with the night" (يُغْشِي اللَّيْلَ النَّهارَ - Al-A'raf: 54). Here, the night is likened to a dark veil that seemingly falls over the light of day and hides it.

In the verses under discussion, the talk is of "takwir" and the wrapping of these two over each other, which also contains a point mentioned above.

Then, He addresses another aspect of the governance and order of this world, saying: "And He subjected the sun and the moon, each running [its course] for a specified term" (وَ سَخَّرَ الشَّمْسَ وَ الْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِي لِأَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى‌).

The sun shows not the slightest disorder in its rotation around itself, or in its movement with the entire solar system toward a specific point in the galaxy; nor does the moon in its movement around the Earth and around itself. In all states, they are submissive to His command, "subjected to the laws of His creation," and they continue their state until the end of their lifespan.

There is also the possibility that the subjection of the sun and moon means "their being subjected for the benefit of man" by the Lord's permission, as stated in Surah Ibrahim, verse 33: "And He subjected for you the sun and the moon, both constant [in their courses]." However, considering the preceding and succeeding sentences in the verse under discussion, and also the fact that the expression "for you" (lakum) is absent in this verse, this meaning seems unlikely.

At the end of the verse, as a warning to the polytheists while simultaneously opening the way for return, grace, and favor, He says: "Unquestionably, He is the All-Mighty, the All-Forgiving" (أَلا هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ).

By virtue of His infinite Might and Power, no sinner or polytheist can escape the grasp of His punishment; and by virtue of His being All-Forgiving, He casts a veil over the faults and sins of the repentant and places them under the shadow of His mercy.

"Ghaffar" is an intensive form derived from "ghufran," which originally means to cover something to protect it from pollution. When used regarding Allah, its meaning is that He covers the faults and sins of His remorseful servants and protects them from punishment and retribution. Yes, He is "Ghaffar" despite His Might and Power, and "Qahhar" (All-Subduing) despite His Mercy and Forgiveness. The mention of these two attributes at the end of the verse is to create a state of fear and hope (khawf and raja) in the servants, which is the primary driver of any evolutionary movement.

Ayahs

خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ ثَمَـٰنِيَةَ أَزْوَٰجٍ ۚ يَخْلُقُكُمْ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ خَلْقًا مِّنۢ بَعْدِ خَلْقٍ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتٍ ثَلَـٰثٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ۖ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُصْرَفُونَ (٦)
He created you from a single soul, then made from it its mate, and He has sent down for you eight mates of the cattle. He creates you in the wombs of your mothers, creation after creation, in a threefold darkness. That is Allah, your Lord! To Him belongs all sovereignty. There is no god except Him. Then where are you being led away? (6)
إِن تَكْفُرُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ عَنكُمْ ۖ وَلَا يَرْضَىٰ لِعِبَادِهِ ٱلْكُفْرَ ۖ وَإِن تَشْكُرُوا۟ يَرْضَهُ لَكُمْ ۗ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُم مَّرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٧)
If you are ungrateful, Allah has indeed no need of you, though He does not approve ingratitude for His servants; and if you give thanks, He approves that for you. No bearer shall bear another’s burden; then your return will be to your Lord, whereat He will inform you concerning what you used to do. Indeed, He knows best what is in the breasts. (7)

Commentary

He Created You All from a Single Soul

Once again, these verses speak of the signs of the greatness of Allah’s creation and describe another part of His various blessings regarding human beings.

First, it speaks of the creation of man and says: "He created you all from a single person, then He created from it its mate" (خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنْها زَوْجَها).

The creation of all humans from a single soul refers to the creation of Adam, our first forefather; that all these human individuals, with their diversity of creation, different temperaments, and various talents and tastes, all return to one root, which is "Adam."

The expression "ثُمَّ جَعَلَ مِنْها زَوْجَها" (then He made from it its mate) actually refers to the fact that Allah created Adam, then created his spouse from the remainder of his clay [^1]. Based on this, the creation of "Eve" (Hawwa) was after the creation of "Adam" and before the creation of Adam's children.

The term "ثُمَّ" (then) does not always denote a chronological delay; rather, it sometimes indicates a delay in mention or description. For example, we might say: "We saw your work of today, then we also observed your work of yesterday," while the actions of yesterday certainly occurred before today's actions, but attention was drawn to them in the next stage.

The idea held by some that the above expression refers to the "World of Dharr" (Alam al-Dharr) and the creation of Adam's children after his creation and before the creation of Eve in the form of tiny ants is an incorrect notion, which we explained in the commentary and clarification of the "World of Dharr" under verse 172 of Surah Al-A'raf [^1]. It is also necessary to remind that the creation of Adam's spouse was not from the physical parts of Adam himself, but rather from the remainder of his clay, as explicitly stated in Islamic narrations. As for the narration stating that "Eve" was created from Adam's last left rib, it is a baseless claim taken from certain Israelite narrations (Isra'iliyyat) and is consistent with what appears in the second chapter of "Genesis" in the current distorted Torah. Furthermore, it contradicts observation and sense, because according to this narration, one of Adam's ribs was removed and Eve was created from it, and therefore men should have one fewer rib on the left side, whereas we know there is no difference between the number of ribs in men and women, and this difference is nothing more than a myth.

After that, it refers to the creation of livestock, which are among the important means of human life—providing clothing from skin on one hand, and being used for nutrition through their milk and meat on the other; furthermore, humans make clothes and various life essentials from their skin and wool, and thirdly, they utilize them as mounts and means of transportation. It says: "And He sent down for you of the livestock eight pairs" (وَ أَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ‌).

The "eight pairs" refers to the male and female sheep, male and female goat, camel, and male and female cow.

[^1]: In fact, the above sentence has an omitted part and is estimated as: "He created you from a single soul which He created, then He made from it its mate." [^1]: Please refer to Volume 7, page 3 onwards.

Since the word "زوج" (pair/mate) is applied to each of the two sexes (male and female), they total 8 pairs (although in everyday Persian usage "زوج" refers to the set of two sexes, it is not so in Arabic terminology; hence, at the beginning of this very verse, Adam's spouse is referred to as "زوج").

The expression "أَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ‌" (He sent down for you) regarding livestock—as we have said before—does not mean sending from a high physical place, but in such cases, it means "descent in status" (nuzul maqami) and a blessing given from a higher authority to a lower one.

It has also been suggested that "انزال" (sending down) here is from the root "نزل" (nuzul) meaning to entertain a guest or the first thing brought to welcome a guest, similar to what appears in Surah Al-Imran, verse 198, regarding the people of Paradise:

"خالِدِينَ فِيها نُزُلًا مِنْ عِنْدِ اللَّهِ‌": "Abiding eternally therein, an accommodation from Allah."

Some commentators have also said that although livestock did not descend from a high place, the prerequisites for their life and growth—which are the life-giving drops of rain and the life-sustaining rays of the sun—come to the earth from above.

A fourth interpretation has also been given for this expression: that all beings were initially in the treasury of the Lord's knowledge and power in the Unseen world (Alam al-Ghayb), then they reached the stage of "witnessing" (shuhud), manifestation, and emergence from the stage of "Ghayb"; thus, it is expressed as "sending down," as we read in verse 21 of Surah Al-Hijr: وَ إِنْ مِنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَ ما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ‌: "And there is not a thing but with Us are its depositories, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" [^1].

[^1]: "Tafsir al-Mizan" and "Ruh al-Ma'ani" under the verses being discussed.

However, the first interpretation seems most appropriate, although there is no contradiction between these interpretations and all may be included in the concept of the verse.

In a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read that he said:

"انزاله ذلك خلقه اياه": "His sending down of those eight pairs is His creating them."

This narration also apparently refers to the first interpretation, because Allah's creation is a creation from a higher authority.

In any case, although livestock are used less for transportation today, their other important benefits have not only not decreased compared to the past but have expanded further. Even today, a major part of human nutrition comes from the milk and meat products of livestock, besides the clothing and other life essentials prepared from their wool and skin. For this reason, one of the important sources of income for the world's major countries is through the breeding of these animals.

Then, it addresses another link in the chain of the Lord's creation, which is the developments in the creation of the embryo, saying: "He creates you in the wombs of your mothers, creation after creation, in three darknesses" (يَخْلُقُكُمْ فِي بُطُونِ أُمَّهاتِكُمْ خَلْقاً مِنْ بَعْدِ خَلْقٍ فِي ظُلُماتٍ ثَلاثٍ‌).

It goes without saying that "خَلْقاً مِنْ بَعْدِ خَلْقٍ‌" (creation after creation) refers to repeated and successive creations, not just two creations.

It is also clear that "يخلقكم" (He creates you), being a present/future tense verb (mudari'), denotes continuity and is a short yet meaningful reference to the amazing transformations and the wonderful, different forms of the embryo during various stages in the mother's womb. According to embryologists, these are among the most wondrous and delicate aspects of the Lord's creation, to the extent that the science of embryology is considered a complete course in Monotheism (Tawhid) and the knowledge of God. Few people can study the intricacies of these matters without opening their mouths in praise and glorification of its Creator.

The expression "ظُلُماتٍ ثَلاثٍ‌" (three darknesses) refers to the darkness of the mother's belly, the darkness of the womb, and the placenta (the special sac in which the embryo is placed), which are in fact three thick layers covering the embryo.

Ordinary artists must work in the presence of full light and brightness, but the Creator of man, in that strange place of darkness, paints such a design upon the water and fashions such a form that everyone is captivated by its sight. In a place where there is no access for anyone, He continuously provides its sustenance, which it desperately needs for its rapid development and growth.

Sayyid al-Shuhada, Imam Hussain عليه السلام, in the famous "Dua of Arafah"—which is a complete and sublime course in the lesson of Tawhid—while enumerating the blessings and power of Allah, addresses His presence thus:

"و ابتدعت خلقى من منى يمنى، ثم اسكنتنى فى ظلمات ثلاث: بين لحم و جلد و دم، لم تشهر بخلقى، و لم تجعل الى شيئا من امرى، ثم اخرجتنى الى الدنيا تاما سويا!": "You initiated my creation from a drop of semen emitted, then You settled me in three darknesses: between flesh, skin, and blood. You did not make my creation public, and You continued the developments of my creation in that hiding place, and You did not entrust any of my vital affairs to me. Then You brought me out into the world complete and sound!" [^1] (Regarding the wonders of creation during the embryonic period and its various stages, we have discussed this in Volume 2, page 316 onwards under verse 6 of Surah Al-Imran, and in Volume 14, page 22 onwards under verse 5 of Surah Al-Hajj).

[^1]: Dua of "Arafah" (Misbah al-Za'ir by Ibn Tawus).

At the end of the verse, after mentioning the three monotheistic links regarding the creation of man, livestock, and embryonic developments, it says: "That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs the sovereignty throughout the entire universe. There is no deity except Him. How then are you turned away?" (ذلِكُمُ اللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ الْمُلْكُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ فَأَنَّى تُصْرَفُونَ‌).

It is as if, after observing these great monotheistic signs, man is brought to the station of witnessing the Essence of the Lord, then He points to His Holy Essence, saying: "That is Allah, your Deity and your Lord." Truly, if there is a seeing eye, it can clearly behold Him behind these signs; the physical eye sees the effects, and the eye of the heart sees the Creator of the effects!

With a hundred thousand manifestations You came forth so that I, With a hundred thousand eyes, may behold You!

The expression "ربكم" (your Lord) and also "له الملك" (to Him belongs the sovereignty) are in fact evidence for the exclusivity of the Deity in the Pure Essence of Allah, which is stated in the phrase "لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ" (There is no deity except Him) (take note).

When He is the Creator, He is also the Owner and the Sustainer; sovereignty throughout existence also belongs only to Him. So, what role does anyone other than Him have in this world to be worthy of worship?! It is here that He cries out to a group who are asleep or oblivious to everything: "فَأَنَّى تُصْرَفُونَ‌" (How then are you turned away?)—despite all this, how have you been deceived and diverted from the path of Tawhid?! [^1]

[^1]: Note that "انى" (anna) sometimes means "where" and sometimes means "how."

After mentioning these great blessings of the Lord, in the next verse, it addresses the issue of "gratitude and ingratitude" (shukr and kufr) and examines its various aspects.

First, it says: The result of your ingratitude or gratitude returns to yourselves, and "if you are ungrateful, then indeed Allah is independent of you" (and likewise, if you give thanks for His blessing, He has no need for it) (إِنْ تَكْفُرُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَنِيٌّ عَنْكُمْ‌).

Then it adds that this richness and independence of the Lord does not prevent Him from charging you with the duty of gratitude and forbidding you from ingratitude, because "duty" (taklif) itself is another grace and blessing. Yes, "He does not approve of ingratitude for His servants, and if you are grateful, He approves it for you" (وَ لا يَرْضى‌ لِعِبادِهِ الْكُفْرَ وَ إِنْ تَشْكُرُوا يَرْضَهُ لَكُمْ‌) [^1]. After stating these two points, it addresses a third matter in this regard, which is "the responsibility of each person for their own actions," because the concept of "duty" is not complete without this meaning. It says: "And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another" (وَ لا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى‌).

And since duty without punishment and reward is meaningless, in the fourth stage it refers to the issue of the Resurrection, saying: "Then to your Lord is your return, and He will inform you of what you used to do" (ثُمَّ إِلى‌ رَبُّكُمْ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ‌).

[^1]: "يرضه" (yardahu) is read with a dammah on the 'ha' in the famous recitation without saturation of the pronoun, because it was originally "يرضاه" and the 'alif' dropped due to the jussive state, becoming "يرضه". Additionally, it should be noted that this pronoun refers back to "gratitude" (shukr), and although the word 'shukr' is not explicitly mentioned in the preceding phrase, the clause "إِنْ تَشْكُرُوا" (if you are grateful) indicates it, similar to the pronoun in "اعْدِلُوا هُوَ أَقْرَبُ لِلتَّقْوى‌" which refers back to justice.

And because the matter of accounting and recompense is not possible without knowledge and awareness of hidden secrets, the verse ends with this sentence: "Indeed, He is Knowing of that within the breasts" (إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ).

In this way, it expresses a collection of the philosophy of "duty" and its characteristics, as well as "human responsibility" and the issue of "recompense, reward, and punishment" in short and cohesive sentences.

Furthermore, this verse is a crushing response to the proponents of the school of determinism (Jabr), who unfortunately were not few among the Islamic sects, because it explicitly says: He never approves of His servants being ungrateful. This itself is clear evidence that He never willed disbelief even for the disbelievers (as the followers of the school of determinism claim), because when He does not approve of something, He certainly will not will it. Is it possible for His Will to be separate from His Approval?

And strange are the biased ones who, to cover up this clear expression, have tried to restrict the word "عباد" (servants) to the believers or the infallible, while this word is absolute and clearly includes all servants. Yes, Allah does not approve of disbelief or ingratitude for any of His servants, just as He approves of gratitude for all of them without exception [^1]. It is also noteworthy that the principle of each person's responsibility for their own actions is among the logical and established principles in all heavenly religions [^2].

[^1]: Regarding "gratitude" (shukr) and its importance, philosophy, real concept, and dimensions, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 10, page 278 onwards—under verse 5 of Surah Ibrahim. [^2]: This matter has been discussed in Volume 12, page 56 (under verse 15 of Surah Al-Isra).

Of course, sometimes a person may be an accomplice in another's crime, but this is only if they were involved in some way in creating the prerequisites or the act itself, such as those who establish an evil innovation (bid'ah) or a bad and wrong tradition; the sin of whoever acts upon it is recorded for the "original cause" without anything being diminished from the sin of those who perform it [^1].

[^1]: This matter has also been discussed in Volume 6, page 64—(under verse 164 of Surah Al-An'am).

Ayahs

۞ وَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ ضُرٌّ دَعَا رَبَّهُۥ مُنِيبًا إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ إِذَا خَوَّلَهُۥ نِعْمَةً مِّنْهُ نَسِىَ مَا كَانَ يَدْعُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ مِن قَبْلُ وَجَعَلَ لِلَّهِ أَندَادًا لِّيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ ۚ قُلْ تَمَتَّعْ بِكُفْرِكَ قَلِيلًا ۖ إِنَّكَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلنَّارِ (٨)
When distress befalls man, he supplicates his Lord, turning to Him penitently. Then, when He grants him a blessing from Himself, he forgets that for which he had supplicated Him earlier and sets up equals to Allah, that he may lead [people] astray from His way. Say, ‘Revel in your ingratitude for a while. Indeed you are among the inmates of the Fire.’ (8)
أَمَّنْ هُوَ قَـٰنِتٌ ءَانَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ سَاجِدًا وَقَآئِمًا يَحْذَرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ وَيَرْجُوا۟ رَحْمَةَ رَبِّهِۦ ۗ قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِى ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ۗ إِنَّمَا يَتَذَكَّرُ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٩)
Is he who supplicates in the watches of the night, prostrating and standing, apprehensive of the Hereafter and expecting the mercy of his Lord...? Say, ‘Are those who know equal to those who do not know?’ Only those who possess intellect take admonition. (9)

Commentary

Are the Learned and the Ignorant Equal?!

In the preceding verses, the discussion focused on ratiocinative monotheism (Tawhid) and the recognition of the Lord through the study of His signs of greatness in the horizons and within the selves. The verses under discussion first address primordial monotheism (Tawhid al-Fitri), making it clear that what man perceives through reason, intellect, and the study of the system of creation exists primordially in the depths of his soul. This manifests itself in the theater of hardships and the storms of incidents; however, this forgetful human, once the storm of events subsides, again falls prey to negligence and arrogance.

It says: "And when harm touches man, (the light of monotheism begins to shine in his heart) he calls upon his Lord, turning to Him in repentance, regretful of his sin and negligence" (وَ إِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ ضُرٌّ دَعا رَبَّهُ مُنِيباً إِلَيْهِ‌).

"But when He bestows a favor upon him from Himself, he forgets that for which he called upon Him before" [^1] (ثُمَّ إِذا خَوَّلَهُ نِعْمَةً مِنْهُ نَسِيَ ما كانَ يَدْعُوا إِلَيْهِ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌). "He sets up rivals and partners for Allah to worship them, so that in addition to his own straying, he may mislead people from the path of Allah" (وَ جَعَلَ لِلَّهِ أَنْداداً لِيُضِلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ‌).

[^1]: There is a discussion among commentators regarding the meaning of "ما" (what/that) in the phrase "نَسِيَ ما كانَ يَدْعُوا إِلَيْهِ‌". A group believes it is a relative pronoun (ma al-mawsulah) referring to the "harm" (durr)—this meaning seems the most appropriate of all, and we have chosen it above. Some consider it to refer to "Allah," while others take it as an infinitive (ma al-masdariyah) meaning "supplication" (du'a). Careful reflection on Verse 12 of Surah Yunus: (وَ إِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ الضُّرُّ دَعانا لِجَنْبِهِ أَوْ قاعِداً أَوْ قائِماً فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُ مَرَّ كَأَنْ لَمْ يَدْعُنا إِلى‌ ضُرٍّ مَسَّهُ‌) serves as a clear witness for the first meaning.

The term "man" (al-insan) here refers to ordinary individuals who have not been nurtured under the light of the teachings of the Prophets. Otherwise, the students of the men of Truth, like their mentors, are always mindful of Him in "ease and hardship," in distress and comfort, in failure and success, and they cling to the mantle of His grace.

The term "harm" (durr) here refers to any kind of injury, loss, or distress, whether physical or spiritual.

"Khawwalahu" is derived from the root khawl, meaning to oversee and constantly monitor something. Since such special attention necessitates granting and bestowing, this root is used to mean "to bestow."

Some have also said it comes from khawl meaning "servant"; thus, "khawwalahu" would mean "He granted him servants," and was subsequently used for any granting of a blessing.

Others have considered this root to mean pride and boasting; thus, the above sentence means to make someone proud by granting a blessing. [^1] Overall, this phrase reflects Allah's special attention and favor in addition to the act of granting.

The expression "turning to Him" (muniban ilayh) shows that in difficult states, when all veils of arrogance and negligence are cast aside, man leaves everything other than Allah and returns to Him. The concept of "inabah" (turning/returning) also implies the reality that man's original station, his origin, and his destination is Allah.

"Andad" is the plural of "nidd," meaning an equal or a peer, with the difference that "mithl" (like/example) has a broad concept, while "nidd" specifically means similarity in the essence and core of a thing.

The expression "sets up" (ja'ala) indicates that man, through his crude imagination and fancy, carves out and fabricates an equal for Allah—something that in no way corresponds to reality.

The phrase "to mislead from His path" (li-yudilla 'an sabilih) shows that the arrogant misguided are not content with their own straying alone, but strive to drag others into this wilderness as well.

In any case, the Quranic verses repeatedly point to the relationship between "primordial monotheism" and the "harsh events of life" which serve as its manifestation. They reflect the volatility and lack of capacity of this arrogant human who takes on a pure divine and monotheistic color when the storms blow, but changes color when the storm subsides, stubbornly treading the path of polytheism.

And how numerous are these fickle individuals, and how few are those whose "Pacific Ocean" of existence is not disturbed by victories, blessings, calms, or storms.

Indeed, a bowl of water or a small pool is stirred by a breeze, but the Great Ocean remains calm in the face of severe storms because of its greatness, and for this reason, it is called "Pacific" (Calm).

At the end of the verse, it addresses such individuals with a direct, decisive, and cutting threat, saying: "Say: Enjoy your disbelief for a little while; indeed, you are of the companions of the Fire" (قُلْ تَمَتَّعْ بِكُفْرِكَ قَلِيلًا إِنَّكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ النَّارِ).

Can such a narrow-minded, misguided, and misguiding human have any fate other than this?

[^1]: Refer to Lisan al-Arab, Mufradat al-Raghib, and the commentary Ruh al-Ma'ani.

In the next verse, using the method of comparison—which is a well-known Quranic method for explaining various issues—it says: "Is such a person [worthy and valuable], or one who is devoutly obedient during the hours of the night, prostrating and standing, [supplicating his Lord], fearing the Hereafter and hoping for the mercy of his Lord?" (أَمَّنْ هُوَ قانِتٌ آناءَ اللَّيْلِ ساجِداً وَ قائِماً يَحْذَرُ الْآخِرَةَ وَ يَرْجُوا رَحْمَةَ رَبِّهِ‌) [^1]. Where is that polytheistic, forgetful, fickle, misguided, and misguiding human, and where is this awakened, luminous, and pure-hearted human who, in the heart of the night when the eyes of the negligent are asleep, places his forehead at the threshold of the Beloved and calls upon Him with fear and hope?! They neither feel secure from His punishment and retribution during times of blessing, nor do they lose hope in His mercy during times of calamity. These two factors always drive their existence in a continuous movement toward the Beloved, coupled with vigilance and caution.

"Qanit" is from the root qunut, meaning the persistence of obedience coupled with humility.

"Ana'" is the plural of "ina," meaning an hour or a portion of time.

The emphasis on the hours of the night is because during those hours, the presence of the heart is greater and the pollution of ostentation (riya) is less than at any other time.

Placing "prostrating" (sajidan) before "standing" (qa'iman) is because prostration is a higher stage of worship. The absolute nature of "mercy," not being restricted to the Hereafter, is evidence of the vastness of divine mercy and its inclusion of both this world and the Hereafter.

[^1]: This sentence has an omitted part, and its estimation is: "Is this one whom We mentioned better, or he who is devoutly obedient during the hours of the night..."

In a tradition narrated in Ilal al-Shara'i from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, and also in the book al-Kafi from him, we read that the above verse (أَمَّنْ هُوَ قانِتٌ آناءَ اللَّيْلِ‌) is interpreted as the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl). [^1] It is clear that this interpretation, like many other interpretations mentioned under various verses of the Quran, is an explanation of a clear instance (misdaq) and does not limit the concept of the verse to the Night Prayer.

Following the verse, it addresses the Prophet, saying: "Say: Are those who know equal to those who do not know?" (قُلْ هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

No, they are not equal. "Only the people of understanding will take heed" (إِنَّما يَتَذَكَّرُ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ‌).

Although the above question is broad and expansive, and is an obvious comparison between the aware and the unaware, the learned and the ignorant, given that before this question another question was raised regarding the inequality of the polytheists with the night-vigilant believers, the second question refers more to this very issue. That is: Are those who know that those stubborn and blind-hearted polytheists are unequal to these pure, enlightened, and sincere believers, equal to those who are unaware of this clear reality?

In any case, this sentence, which begins with a rhetorical question and is part of the fundamental slogans of Islam, clarifies the greatness of the status of knowledge and the learned over the ignorant. Since this inequality is mentioned in an absolute manner, it becomes clear that these two groups are neither equal in the presence of Allah, nor in the view of the aware creation; they do not stand in the same rank in this world, nor in the Hereafter; they are neither equal in appearance nor in essence.

[^1]: Ilal al-Shara'i and al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 479.

Key Points

In these two verses, subtle references are made to interesting points that become clear with a little reflection:

  1. In the first verse, one of the important philosophies of bitter and unpleasant events is mentioned as the removal of the veils of arrogance and negligence from before the eye of the heart, the igniting of the light of faith, and the return and repentance toward the Lord. This is an answer to those who consider the existence of bitter life events as an objection to the system of creation or the justice of the Lord.

  2. The second verse begins with action and self-purification and ends with knowledge and recognition, because as long as there is no self-purification, the light of recognition will not shine upon the heart; fundamentally, these two are inseparable.

  3. The expression "قانت آناء الليل" (devoutly obedient during the hours of the night), which appears as an active participle (ism al-fa'il), combined with the absolute nature of the word "night" (al-layl), is evidence of the continuity and permanence of their servitude and humility before Allah, for if an action is not continuous, its effect is negligible.

  4. The forced knowledge and awareness that occurs during the descent of calamity and connects man to the Origin of creation is a true instance of knowledge only if it continues after the storm of the event subsides. Therefore, the above verses place those who wake up at the moment of calamity and then fall into forgetfulness in the rank of the ignorant. Thus, the truly learned are those who are mindful of Him at all times.

  5. Interestingly, at the end of the latter verse, it says: even the difference between knowledge and ignorance is understood by the "possessors" of intellect! For the ignorant person does not even know the value of knowledge! In reality, every stage of knowledge is a prelude to another stage.

  6. Knowledge in this verse and other verses of the Quran does not mean knowing a handful of terms or material relationships between things—so-called "formal sciences"—but rather it refers to a specific recognition and awareness that invites man to "qunut," meaning obedience to the Lord, fear of His court, and hope for Allah's mercy. This is the reality of knowledge. Formal sciences are also knowledge if they serve such recognition; but if they become a source of arrogance, negligence, oppression, and corruption on earth, and no "quality or state" results from them, they are nothing more than "empty talk."

  7. Contrary to what the uninformed imagine—viewing religion as an opiate—the most important call of the Prophets was toward science and knowledge, and they declared their estrangement from ignorance everywhere. In addition to the verses of the Quran which use every opportunity to state this reality, expressions are seen in Islamic traditions such that no greater importance for knowledge can be imagined. In a tradition from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"There is no good in life except for two men: a learned man who is obeyed, or an attentive listener who retains knowledge." [^1]

[^1]: al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Chapter on the Description of Knowledge and its Virtue, Hadith 7.

In another tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Indeed, the scholars are the heirs of the Prophets. That is because the Prophets did not leave behind dirhams or dinars as inheritance; rather, they left behind traditions from their traditions. Whoever takes anything from them has taken an abundant share. So look at this knowledge of yours, from whom do you take it? For indeed, among us, the Ahl al-Bayt, in every generation there are just individuals who repel from it the distortion of the extremists, the plagiarism of the falsifiers, and the interpretation of the ignorant." [^1]

  1. In the latter verse, three groups are mentioned: the learned, the ignorant, and the people of understanding (ulu al-albab). In a tradition from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of these three groups, we read:

"We are those who know, our enemies are those who do not know, and our Shias are the people of understanding." [^2] It is clear that this interpretation is meant to state the most obvious instances of the verse and does not negate the generality of the verse's concept.

  1. It is narrated in a tradition: One night, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, moved from the Mosque of Kufa toward his home while Kumayl ibn Ziyad, who was one of his special companions, accompanied him. On the way, they passed by the house of a man whose voice was loud while reciting the Quran, and he was reading this verse: (أَمَّنْ هُوَ قانِتٌ آناءَ اللَّيْلِ ...) with a pleasant and sorrowful voice. In his heart, Kumayl took great pleasure in the state of this man and was happy with his spirituality, without saying anything out loud. The Imam عليه السلام turned to him and said: "Do not let the noise of this man impress you; he is of the people of Hell! And soon I will inform you of it." Kumayl fell into wonder at this matter: first, that the Imam عليه السلام quickly became aware of his thought and intention, and second, that he testified to the hellish nature of this man who appeared righteous. Some time passed until the affair of the Kharijites reached the point where they stood against the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام and the Hazrat fought them, while they had memorized the Quran as it was revealed. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, turned to Kumayl while a sword was in his hand and the heads of those rebellious disbelievers had fallen on the ground. With the tip of the sword, he pointed to one of those heads and said: "O Kumayl! (أَمَّنْ هُوَ قانِتٌ آناءَ اللَّيْلِ‌): meaning, this is the same person who was reciting the Quran that night, and whose state impressed you." Kumayl kissed the Hazrat and sought forgiveness. [^3]

[^1]: al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Chapter on the Description of Knowledge and its Virtue, Hadith 2. [^2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [^3]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 496, regarding the states of Kumayl.

Ayahs

قُلْ يَـٰعِبَادِ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةٌ ۗ وَأَرْضُ ٱللَّهِ وَٰسِعَةٌ ۗ إِنَّمَا يُوَفَّى ٱلصَّـٰبِرُونَ أَجْرَهُم بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (١٠)
Say, ‘[Allah declares:] “O My servants who have faith! Be wary of your Lord. For those who do good in this world there will be a good [reward], and Allah’s earth is vast. Indeed the patient will be paid in full their reward without any reckoning.” ’ (10)

وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (١٢)
and I have been commanded to be the foremost of those who submit [to Him].’ (12)
قُلْ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّى عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٣)
Say, ‘Indeed, should I disobey my Lord, I fear the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (13)

You worship whatever you wish besides Him.’ Say, ‘The losers are those who ruin themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection.’ Indeed, that is a manifest loss! (15)
