There will be canopies of fire above them, and [similar] canopies beneath them. With that Allah deters His servants. So, My servants, be wary of Me! (16)

Commentary

The Main Outlines of the Program for Sincere Servants

Following the previous verses, which compared arrogant polytheists with believers obedient to Allah's command, as well as the learned with the ignorant, the verses under discussion outline the main features of the programs for true and sincere servants through "seven instructions." These are presented across several verses, each beginning with the command "Say" (قُلْ).

First, it begins with Taqwa (piety/God-wariness) and commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say: O My servants who have believed, fear your Lord and practice Taqwa" (قُلْ يا عِبادِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا رَبَّكُمْ) [^1]. Indeed, Taqwa—which is self-restraint against sin and a sense of responsibility and commitment before the Truth—is the primary program for the believing servants of Allah. Taqwa is a shield against the Fire and a deterrent against deviation; it is the great capital in the market of Resurrection and the criterion for human personality and dignity in the presence of the Lord.

In the second instruction, it addresses the issue of "Ihsan" (excellence and doing good) in this world, which is the abode of action. By stating the result of goodness, it encourages and incites people toward it, saying: "For those who do good in this world is a good reward (Hasanah)" (لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةٌ) [^2]. Indeed, doing good in an absolute sense in this world—in speech, in action, and in one's way of thinking toward friends and strangers alike—results in enjoying a magnificent reward in both worlds, for goodness shall yield nothing but a good result.

In reality, Taqwa is a deterrent factor, while Ihsan is a motivating factor; together, they encompass "abandoning sin" and "performing obligatory and recommended deeds."

The third instruction encourages "Hijrah" (migration) from centers of polytheism, disbelief, and those tainted by sin, stating: "Allah's earth is spacious" (وَ أَرْضُ اللَّهِ واسِعَةٌ).

This is, in fact, a response to the weak-willed excuse-seekers who used to say: "We are unable to perform our divine duties in the land of Mecca due to the dominance of the polytheists' rule." The Quran says: Allah's land is not limited to Mecca; "if not Mecca, then Medina." The world is vast; stir yourselves and move from centers polluted by polytheism, disbelief, and oppression—which hinder your freedom and the fulfillment of your duties—to another place.

The issue of Hijrah is one of the most important matters that not only played the most fundamental role in the victory of the Islamic government at the dawn of Islam—and for this reason became the foundation and starting point of the Islamic calendar—but also holds extraordinary importance at any other time. On one hand, it prevents believers from surrendering to the pressure and suffocation of their environment, and on the other, it serves as a factor for exporting Islam to various parts of the world.

The Holy Quran says: "When the angels take the souls of those who wronged themselves, they ask: 'In what state were you?' They reply: 'We were oppressed and under pressure in our land.' But the angels answer them: 'Was not Allah's earth spacious so that you might migrate therein?' Their abode is Hell, and what an evil destination it is!" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ قالُوا فِيمَ كُنْتُمْ قالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ قالُوا أَ لَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللَّهِ واسِعَةً فَتُهاجِرُوا فِيها فَأُولئِكَ مَأْواهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ وَ ساءَتْ مَصِيراً) (An-Nisa: 97).

This clearly shows that the pressure and suffocation of an environment is by no means an excuse before Allah when the possibility of migration exists.

(Regarding the importance of Hijrah in Islam and its various dimensions, diverse discussions have been provided in Volume 4, page 89, under verse 100 of Surah An-Nisa, and in Volume 7, page 261, under verse 72 of Surah Al-Anfal).

Since migration is usually accompanied by numerous difficulties in various aspects of life, the fourth instruction regarding patience and steadfastness is stated as follows:

"The patient and steadfast shall receive their reward without account" (إِنَّما يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ) [^1]. The expression "Yuwaffa," derived from the root "Wafa" meaning to give in full, on one hand, and the expression "without account" (bi-ghayri hisab) on the other, indicates that the steadfast patient ones hold the highest reward in the presence of Allah, and the importance of no action reaches the level of patience and steadfastness.

A witness to this statement is the famous Hadith narrated by Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله:

"When the ledgers of deeds are unfolded and the scales of justice are set up, no scale shall be set up for the people of affliction (those who endured hardships), nor shall any ledger be unfolded for them." Then he recited this verse: (إِنَّما يُوَفَّى الصَّابِرُونَ أَجْرَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ) "Indeed, the patient will be given their reward without account." [^2]

Some believe that this verse was revealed regarding the first migration of Muslims—that is, the migration of a large group led by Ja'far ibn Abi Talib to the land of Abyssinia. We have repeatedly stated that while the occasions of revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) clarify the concepts of the verses, they do not limit them.

[^1]: It is obvious that the address "O My servants" is from Allah, and if He tells the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say this word," the meaning is "address them on My behalf."

[^2]: Most commentators consider "in this world" (فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا) to be related to "those who do good" (أحسنوا); therefore, "good reward" (حسنه) will be absolute, encompassing every kind of good reward in this world and the next. Given that "Tanwin" (nunation) in such cases indicates "exaltation and greatness," the magnitude of this reward is highlighted.

[^1]: "Without account" (بِغَيْرِ حِسابٍ) may be related to "shall be given in full" (يوفى), or it may be a state (Hal) for "their reward" (اجرهم), but the first possibility is more appropriate.

[^2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed—and the same meaning with a slight variation is narrated in Tafsir al-Qurtubi from Husayn ibn Ali عليه السلام from his grandfather the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله.

In the fifth instruction, the issue of sincerity (Ikhlas) and monotheism (Tawhid)—pure from any taint of polytheism—is mentioned. However, here the tone of the discourse changes, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله speaks of his own duties and responsibilities, saying: "Say: I have been commanded to worship Allah, making my religion sincere for Him" (قُلْ إِنِّي أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ اللَّهَ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ الدِّينَ).

Then he adds: "And I have been commanded to be the first of the Muslims!" (وَ أُمِرْتُ لِأَنْ أَكُونَ أَوَّلَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ). Here, the sixth instruction—namely, taking precedence over everyone in Islam and complete submission to Allah's command—is presented.

The seventh and final instruction, which is the issue of fearing the Lord's punishment on the Day of Resurrection, is also presented in this same tone, saying: "Say: I fear, if I should disobey my Lord, the punishment of a Great Day" (قُلْ إِنِّي أَخافُ إِنْ عَصَيْتُ رَبِّي عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ).

This is so that this reality becomes clear: the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is also a servant among the servants of Allah; he too is commanded to worship sincerely; he too fears divine punishment; he too is commanded to submit to the command of the Truth, and he even has a heavier mission in that he must be the foremost of all! He never claimed the status of "divinity" nor did he step outside the path of "servitude"; rather, he takes pride and glory in this status, and for this reason, he is a model and an example in everything.

He does not claim any privilege for himself over others in these regards, and this itself is a clear sign of his greatness and truthfulness. He is not like the false claimants who invited people to worship them, presenting themselves as superhuman and of a higher essence, and sometimes inviting their followers to give them their weight in gold and jewels every year! In reality, he says: I am not like the tyrannical kings who impose duties on people while considering themselves "above duty and obligation." This is, in fact, an allusion to an important educational point: every educator and leader must be the foremost in performing the instructions of his own school. He must be the first believer in his own creed, the most diligent individual, and the most self-sacrificing person, so that people find faith in his sincerity and recognize him as their "guide" and "model" in everything.

From this, it becomes clear that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله being the "first Muslim" is not only in terms of time, but that he was the first Muslim in all aspects: in faith, in sincerity, in action and sacrifice, and in Jihad, steadfastness, and resistance. The entire history of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life also confirms this reality well.

After mentioning the seven-point program of the above verses (Taqwa, Ihsan, Hijrah, patience, sincerity, submission, and fear), since the issue of sincerity—especially against various motives of polytheism—has a special significance, the text returns to it once more for emphasis. With the same tone, it says: "Say: Allah [alone] do I worship, making my religion sincere for Him" (قُلِ اللَّهَ أَعْبُدُ مُخْلِصاً لَهُ دِينِي) [^1].

[^1]: The precedence of "Allah," which is the object (Maf'ul) of "I worship" (اعبد), is for the purpose of "restriction" (Hasr), meaning "I worship Him alone." Therefore, the phrase "making my religion sincere for Him" (مُخْلِصاً لَهُ دِينِي), which is a state (Hal), is a renewed emphasis on this meaning.

"But you, worship whatever you will besides Him" (فَاعْبُدُوا ما شِئْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ). Then it adds: "Say: Indeed, the losers are those who lose themselves and their families on the Day of Resurrection!" (قُلْ إِنَّ الْخاسرِينَ الَّذِينَ خَسِرُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ وَ أَهْلِيهِمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ). They neither benefited from their own existence nor gained any result from the capital of their lives; neither their families nor their children are a means of their salvation, nor are they a source of honor or intercession in the presence of the Truth.

"Unquestionably, that is the manifest loss!" (أَلا ذلِكَ هُوَ الْخُسْرانُ الْمُبِينُ).

In the last verse under discussion, it describes one of the aspects of "manifest loss" and their clear deficit as follows: "For them, there are canopies of fire above them, and beneath them are canopies [of fire]!" (لَهُمْ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ مِنَ النَّارِ وَ مِنْ تَحْتِهِمْ ظُلَلٌ). In this way, they are surrounded by flames of fire from every side. What loss is greater than this? And what punishment is more painful than this? "Zulal" is the plural of "Zullah," meaning a curtain or covering fixed on the upper side. Therefore, applying it to a carpet spread beneath the feet is a type of metaphorical usage and an expansion of the word's concept.

Some commentators have said that because the inhabitants of Hell are trapped between the layers of Hell, the curtains of fire are both above their heads and beneath their feet, and thus applying the word "Zulal" to the lower curtains is not even metaphorical.

Similar to this verse is verse 55 of Surah Al-Ankabut, which says: "On the Day the punishment covers them from above them and from beneath their feet and He says: Taste what you used to do!" (يَوْمَ يَغْشاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ وَ مِنْ تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِهِمْ وَ يَقُولُ ذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ). This is, in fact, an embodiment of their states in this world, where ignorance, disbelief, and injustice had encompassed their entire being and covered them from every side.

Then, for emphasis and as a lesson, it adds: "That is what Allah warns His servants with. So, O My servants, fear Me!" (ذلِكَ يُخَوِّفُ اللَّهُ بِهِ عِبادَهُ يا عِبادِ فَاتَّقُونِ).

The use of the term "servants" (عباد) and its attribution to "Allah," repeatedly in this verse, indicates that if Allah threatens with punishment, it is out of His grace and mercy, so that the servants of the Truth do not fall into such a grim fate. From this, it becomes clear that it is not necessary to interpret "servants" in this verse as specifically the believers; rather, it includes everyone, for no one should consider themselves safe from divine punishment.

Key Points

The Reality of Loss (Khusran)?

Khusran—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—originally means the loss of capital or its reduction. Sometimes it is attributed to the person, and it is said "so-and-so suffered a loss," and sometimes it is attributed to the action itself, and it is said "his trade suffered a loss."

On the other hand, sometimes Khusran is used regarding outward capital, such as worldly wealth and status, and sometimes regarding spiritual capital, such as health, sanity, intellect, faith, and reward. This is exactly what Allah has named "manifest loss" (Khusran Mubin)... and every loss that Allah has mentioned in the Quran refers to the second meaning, not what pertains to worldly capital and ordinary trades. [^1] The Quran, in fact, likens human beings to traders who enter the marketplace of this world with heavy capital; some make a massive profit, and a group suffers a severe loss.

There are many verses in the Holy Quran where this expression and metaphor are reflected, and in reality, they express the truth that for salvation on the Day of Resurrection, one should not sit waiting for this person or that; the only way is to utilize existing capital and to strive and endeavor in this great trade where "everything is given for a price, not for an excuse!" As for why the loss of the polytheists and sinners is described as "manifest loss"? Because, firstly, they have lost the greatest capital—namely the capital of life, intellect, reason, emotions, and existence—without gaining anything in return.

Secondly: If they had only lost this capital without purchasing punishment and penalty, it would be one thing; the misfortune is that in exchange for losing these great capitals, they have prepared for themselves the harshest and most painful punishment.

Thirdly: This is a loss that cannot be compensated, and this is the most painful of all. Yes, this is "manifest loss."

2- The phrase "worship whatever you will" (فَاعْبُدُوا ما شِئْتُمْ) is what is termed a command given for the purpose of threat. This is said in a situation where advice and admonition do not affect the criminal and sinner. The last word said to him is: "Do whatever you want, but wait for the punishment," meaning you have reached a point where you no longer have the worthiness for obligation, advice, or admonition, and there is no fate or remedy for you other than painful punishment.

Who is meant by "Ahl" (Family)?

The above verses say: These losers not only lose the capitals of their own existence but will also lose the capital of the existence of their "Ahl" (family/people).

Some commentators have said that "Ahl" here refers to a person's followers and those who aligned with his school of thought and programs.

Some have interpreted it as the heavenly spouses whom the polytheists and criminals lose.

And some interpret it as the family and relatives in this world. The latter meaning seems most appropriate given the original concept of this word, for faithless individuals will lose them in the Hereafter; if they are believers, they will be separated from them, and if they are disbelievers like themselves, they will not only be of no benefit to them but will be a cause of even more painful punishment.

[^1]: Al-Mufradat, root "Khasara".

Ayahs

وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱجْتَنَبُوا۟ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ أَن يَعْبُدُوهَا وَأَنَابُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ لَهُمُ ٱلْبُشْرَىٰ ۚ فَبَشِّرْ عِبَادِ (١٧)
As for those who stay clear of the worship of fake deities and turn penitently to Allah, there is good news for them. So give good news to My servants (17)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ ٱلْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُۥٓ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ هَدَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (١٨)

Commentary

The True Servants of Allah

In these verses, the Quran once again employs the method of comparison. In contrast to the prejudiced and stubborn polytheists who have no destiny other than the fire of Hell, it speaks of the special and truth-seeking servants of the Lord, saying: "Give glad tidings to those who avoided the worship of 'Taghut' and turned back to Allah" (وَ الَّذِينَ اجْتَنَبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ أَنْ يَعْبُدُوها وَ أَنابُوا إِلَى اللَّهِ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى‌).

Given that "glad tidings" (bushra) is mentioned here in an absolute sense, it encompasses all kinds of tidings regarding Divine blessings, whether material or spiritual. However, this vast and extensive glad tiding is reserved for those who avoid the worship of Taghut and return to Allah; indeed, the sum of faith and righteous deeds is gathered in this single sentence.

This is because "Taghut" is originally derived from the root "tughyan," meaning transgression and exceeding the limits. Therefore, this word is applied to every transgressor and every deity worshipped besides Allah, such as Satan and tyrannical rulers (this word is used for both singular and plural) [1]. Consequently, "avoiding Taghut" in this broad sense includes distancing oneself from every kind of polytheism, idol-worship, worship of whims, worship of Satan, and submission to

[^1]: Some commentators, such as al-Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf, believe that "Taghut" was originally "Tughyut" on the weight of fa‘lut (like malakut), then underwent transposition where the lam of the root preceded the ‘ayn, becoming "Tughut," and after the conversion of the waw to alif, it became "Taghut." It conveys emphasis from several aspects: the intensive form, the infinitive meaning, and the transposition (Tafsir al-Kashshaf, vol. 4, p. 120).

tyrannical rulers and oppressive hegemons. "Turning to Allah" (inaba) encapsulates the spirit of piety, God-wariness, and faith; certainly, such people are worthy of glad tidings.

It is also noteworthy that the worship of Taghut does not only mean bowing and prostrating; rather, it includes every form of obedience. As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"Whoever obeys a tyrant has indeed worshipped him!" [1]

Then, to introduce these special servants, He says: "So give glad tidings to My servants" (فَبَشِّرْ عِبادِ) [2].

"Those who listen to the word and follow the best of it" (الَّذِينَ يَسْتَمِعُونَ الْقَوْلَ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ أَحْسَنَهُ‌). "Those are the ones whom Allah has guided, and those are the people of understanding" (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَداهُمُ اللَّهُ وَ أُولئِكَ هُمْ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ‌).

These two verses, which have become an Islamic slogan, clearly demonstrate the free-thinking of Muslims and their selectivity in various matters.

First, He says: "Give glad tidings to My servants," and then He introduces these special servants by stating that "they listen to the words of this person and that person without considering the speaker or other characteristics, and with the power of intellect and reason, they choose the best of them." There is no prejudice or stubbornness in their conduct, and no limitation in their thought or reflection. They are seekers of truth and thirsty for reality; wherever they find it, they welcome it with their whole being and drink freely from its pure spring

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed, vol. 7, p. 493. [^2]: "‘Ibad" was originally "‘ibadi"; the "ya" was omitted and the kasra remains in its place.

until they are quenched.

They are not only seekers of truth and thirsty for good speech, but among "good" and "better," and "fine" and "finest," they choose the latter. In short, they desire the best and the most superior.

Yes, this is the sign of a true Muslim and a truth-seeking believer.

Regarding what is meant by "the word" (al-qawl) in the phrase "they listen to the word," commentators have various interpretations:

Some have interpreted it as the "Quran," and what it contains of acts of obedience and permissible things, considering "following the best" to mean following the acts of obedience.

Others have interpreted it as all Divine commands, whether in the Quran or elsewhere.

However, there is no evidence for these limited interpretations; rather, the literal meaning of the verse includes every kind of word and speech. The believing servants of Allah choose from among all words that which is "best" (ahsan), follow it, and implement it in their actions.

Interestingly, in the above verse, the Quran restricts those who possess "Divine guidance" to this group, just as it restricts the "people of understanding" to them. This indicates that this group is subject to both outward and inward guidance: outward guidance through intellect and reason, and inward guidance through Divine light and unseen aid. These two great honors belong to these free-thinking truth-seekers.

Since the Prophet of Allah was intensely eager for the guidance of the lost and the polytheists, and suffered from the deviation of those who had no listening ear for the truths, the next verse comforts him by

stating the reality that this world is a world of freedom and trial, and that a group is ultimately deserving of the Fire. It says: "Is he, then, against whom the word of punishment has become due [to be saved by you]? Can you rescue him who is in the Fire?" (أَ فَمَنْ حَقَّ عَلَيْهِ كَلِمَةُ الْعَذابِ أَ فَأَنْتَ تُنْقِذُ مَنْ فِي النَّارِ) [1]. The phrase "the word of punishment has become due against him" refers to verses such as verse 85 of Surah Sad, which says regarding Satan and his followers: "I will surely fill Hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together!" It is obvious that the finalization of the decree of punishment for this group is not a matter of compulsion; rather, it is because of the deeds they have committed and their persistence in oppression, corruption, and sin, such that the spirit of faith and discernment has died within them forever, and their very existence has become entirely hellish! From here, it becomes clear that the phrase "Can you rescue him who is in the Fire?" is a subtle allusion to the fact that their being destined for Hell is so certain that it is as if they are already in the heart of the Fire. We know that such people, who have severed all their lines of communication with Allah, have no way of salvation; even the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, despite being a "mercy to the worlds," cannot deliver them from the punishment.

[^1]: This sentence actually has an omitted part, and its estimation is: "Is he against whom the word of punishment has become due [saved by you]? Can you rescue him who is in the Fire?" where the phrase "can you save him" is omitted and the second sentence serves as evidence for it. Some have said the estimation is: "Is he against whom the word of punishment has become due, saved from it?"

However, to gladden the heart of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and to give hope to the believers, the final verse says: "But those who fear their Lord, for them are chambers, above which are built [other] chambers" (لكِنِ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ لَهُمْ غُرَفٌ مِنْ فَوْقِها غُرَفٌ‌).

If the people of Hell are surrounded by layers of fire—and in the words of the previous verses, "They shall have canopies of fire above them and canopies beneath them"—the people of Paradise have chambers above chambers, and palaces above palaces. This is because viewing the scenery of flowers, water, rivers, and gardens from elevated chambers is more delightful and pleasant.

"Ghuraf" is the plural of "ghurfah," from the root "gharf" (on the weight of harf), meaning to pick up something. Therefore, the water one scoops up from a spring with the palm to drink is called a "ghurfah." Subsequently, it was applied to the upper parts of a building and the higher floors of houses.

These beautiful heavenly chambers are adorned with rivers flowing beneath them; thus, the verse continues: "beneath which rivers flow" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ).

Yes, "This is the promise of Allah, and Allah does not fail in His promise" (وَعْدَ اللَّهِ لا يُخْلِفُ اللَّهُ الْمِيعادَ) [1].

Key Points

1- The Logic of Islamic Free-thinking

Many religions forbid their followers from studying and examining the words of others,

[^1]: Al-Zamakhshari says in Al-Kashshaf: "Wa‘da Allah" is in the accusative case as an emphatic absolute object, because the sentence "for them are chambers" implies "Allah promised them chambers."

because due to the weakness of logic they suffer from, they fear that the logic of others might prevail and take their followers away from them! However, as we read in the above verses, Islam implements an "open-door" policy in this regard. it considers the true servants of Allah to be those who are people of research—they are neither terrified of hearing the words of others, nor do they submit unconditionally, nor do they accept every temptation.

Islam gives glad tidings to those who hear the words and choose the best of them. Not only do they prefer the good over the bad, but among the good, they pick whichever flower is the finest.

The Quran severely rebukes the ignorant who, upon hearing the message of truth, put their fingers in their ears and wrap themselves in their garments, as mentioned in the words of Noah عليه السلام when complaining to the Lord: "And indeed, every time I invited them that You might forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and were arrogant with [great] arrogance" (Noah: 7).

Fundamentally, a school of thought that possesses powerful logic has no reason to fear the words of others or to dread the presentation of their issues. Those who are weak and lack logic are the ones who should fear.

At the same time, this verse excludes from the ranks of the "people of understanding" and the "guided" those blind followers who accept every word unconditionally and do not even investigate their food as much as sheep going to a pasture. It considers these two descriptions exclusive to those who are neither trapped in the excess of unconditional submission

nor the deficiency of dry and ignorant prejudices.

2- Answer to Several Questions

[The Prohibition of Studying Books of Misguidance]

1- A question may arise here: why is the buying and selling of "books of misguidance" (kutub al-dalal) forbidden in Islam?

2- Why is giving the Quran into the hands of disbelievers considered forbidden?

3- How can someone who does not know a subject be selective in it and separate the good from the bad? Does this not imply a circular argument?

The answer to the first question is clear, because the discussion in the above verses concerns words in which there is hope for guidance. Whenever it is proven after investigation and scrutiny that a certain book is misleading, it falls outside the scope of this instruction. Islam never allows people to walk a path whose incorrectness has been established.

Of course, as long as this matter is not proven to someone—and they are, so to speak, in the state of researching different religions to accept the correct faith—they can examine all these books. But after the matter is established, it must be removed from reach as a poisonous substance.

As for the second question, it is not permissible to give the Quran to a non-Muslim if it leads to desecration and disrespect. However, if we know that a non-Muslim is truly thinking of researching Islam and wants to examine the Quran for this purpose and goal, not only is placing the Quran at their disposal permissible, but it may even be obligatory. Those who have forbidden this intended cases other than this.

Therefore, major Islamic centers insist on translating the Quran into the living languages of the world and placing it at the disposal of truth-seekers who are thirsty for

realities to spread the Islamic call.

Regarding the third question, it should be noted that it often happens that a person cannot perform a task personally, but when another performs it, they can distinguish the good from the bad and, with the power of intellect and the capital of conscience, choose the best of them.

For example, there may be people who are not knowledgeable in the art of architecture and masonry, and cannot even place two bricks correctly on top of each other, yet they can distinguish a good building of high quality from an ugly, shapeless, and disproportionate one.

We know many people who are not poets themselves but recognize the value of the poems of great poets and distinguish them from the worthless poems of pretenders. There are those who are not athletes but judge and select well among athletes.

3- Examples of Islamic Narrations Regarding Free-thinking

In Islamic hadiths mentioned in the commentary of the above verses, or independently, much emphasis is seen on this subject:

Among them, we read in a hadith from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام that he said to his learned companion named "Hisham ibn al-Hakam":

those are the ones whom Allah has guided, and those are the people of intellect and thought.'" [1]

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned regarding the interpretation of the above verse:

"It refers to the person who hears a hadith and narrates it exactly as he heard it, neither adding to it nor subtracting from it." [2]

Of course, the purpose of this hadith is to interpret "following the best of it," because one of the signs of following the best words is that a person does not add anything of his own to it and places it exactly at the disposal of others.

In Nahj al-Balagha, among the short aphorisms of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read:

"Wisdom is the lost property of the believer, so take wisdom even if it comes from the people of hypocrisy!" [3]

They were "Salman al-Farsi," "Abu Dharr al-Ghifari," and "Zayd ibn 'Amr." [4]

Have you not seen that Allah sends down water from the sky, then He conducts it through the ground as springs. Then He brings forth with it crops of diverse hues. أَفَمَن شَرَحَ ٱللَّهُ صَدْرَهُۥ لِلْإِسْلَـٰمِ فَهُوَ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ ۚ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلْقَـٰسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُم مِّن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٢٢)

Commentary

Those Who Ride Upon a Mount of Light

In these verses, the Quran once again returns to the proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection (Ma'ad), completing the discussions regarding disbelief and faith found in the preceding verses.

From among the manifestations of the Lord’s greatness and His Rububiyyah (Lordship) in the system of the universe, He points to the issue of "the descent of rain" from the sky. He then describes the cultivation of "thousands of colors" of plants from this "colorless water," tracing their stages of life until they reach their final phase.

Addressing the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) as a model for all believers, He says: "Have you not seen that Allah sends down water from the sky and then causes it to penetrate the earth as springs?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسَلَكَهُ يَنابِيعَ فِي الْأَرْضِ) [^1]. The life-giving drops of rain descend from the sky; the "permeable" layer of the earth accepts them into its depths until they reach an "impermeable" layer, which stops and stores them. Subsequently, He sends them forth in the form of springs, subterranean canals (qanats), and wells.

The phrase "salakahu" (He caused it to enter the channels of the earth) is a concise reference to what we mentioned above.

"Yanabi‘" is the plural of "yanbu‘," derived from the root "nab‘," meaning the gushing of water from the ground.

If the earth possessed only one impermeable layer, it would not store a single drop of rain, and all of it would flow into the seas after descending from the sky; no spring would exist, nor any canal or well. Conversely, if it had only a permeable layer, all the water would sink into the depths of the earth such that it would be inaccessible. The arrangement of the earth's crust into these two layers—permeable and impermeable—at such calculated intervals is among the signs of His power. Interestingly, there are sometimes multiple alternating layers of permeable and impermeable strata, which are utilized in the digging of "shallow," "semi-deep," and "deep" wells.

He then adds: "Then He produces thereby crops of varying colors" (ثُمَّ يُخْرِجُ بِهِ زَرْعاً مُخْتَلِفاً أَلْوانُهُ).

Their types are diverse, such as wheat, barley, rice, and corn; their qualities differ, as do their outward colors—some are dark green, some light green, some have broad and expansive leaves, while others have narrow and delicate leaves. Furthermore, considering that "zar‘" (crop) refers to plants without a strong trunk—as opposed to "shajar" (tree), which is generally applied to plants with sturdy trunks—"zar‘" has a broad meaning that includes non-food plants as well. This encompasses various flowers, ornamental plants, and medicinal herbs that are extraordinarily diverse and possess various colors and appearances. Sometimes, even on a single branch, or rather within a single flower, these different colors are placed side-by-side in a most beautiful and delicate manner, singing the song of Monotheism and the glorification of God in a silent language.

He then proceeds to the other stages of this plant's life, saying: "Then it withers so that you see it turned yellow [and lifeless]" (ثُمَّ يَهِيجُ فَتَراهُ مُصْفَرًّا) [^1].

[^1]: Grammatically, "Yanabi‘" is mansub bi-naz‘ al-khafid (accusative due to the removal of a preposition), originally being "fi yanabi‘" (Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani and Ruh al-Bayan). [^1]: "Yahiju" is derived from "hayajan," which linguistically has two meanings: first, the drying and yellowing of a plant; and second, to be set in motion or to surge. It is possible these two meanings return to a single root, for when a plant dries, it becomes ready for separation, scattering, movement, and agitation.

A gale blows from every direction and uproots that which has become frail. "Then He makes it [scattered] debris" (ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُ حُطاماً).

"Indeed, in that is a reminder for those of understanding" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَذِكْرى‌ لِأُولِي الْأَلْبابِ).

It is a reminder of the calculated and magnificent system of the universe and the Lordship of the Creator in this vast scene. It is also a reminder of the end of life, the extinguishing of the flames of existence, and subsequently, the issue of the Resurrection and the restoration of life to the dead.

Though this scene takes place in the world of plants, it warns human beings that the same cycle is repeated in your own lives. The duration may differ, but the principles are the same: birth, vitality and youth, then withering and old age, and finally, death!

Following this great lesson on Monotheism and the Resurrection, He proceeds to a comparison between the believer and the disbeliever to clarify the truth that the Quran and heavenly revelation are like raindrops descending upon the land of hearts. Just as only prepared lands benefit from the life-giving drops of rain, only those hearts benefit from the divine verses that have gained readiness and expansion under the shadow of His grace and self-purification. He says: "Is one whose breast Allah has opened to Islam and who is upon a light from his Lord [like one whose heart is hardened]?" (أَ فَمَنْ شَرَحَ اللَّهُ صَدْرَهُ لِلْإِسْلامِ فَهُوَ عَلى‌ نُورٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ) [^1]. He then adds: "So woe to those whose hearts are hardened against the remembrance of Allah" (فَوَيْلٌ لِلْقاسِيَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ مِنْ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ).

Neither beneficial exhortations affect them, nor warnings or glad tidings; neither the soul-shaking verses of the Quran set them in motion, nor does the life-giving rain of revelation grow the flowers of piety and virtue within them. In short, "they have neither freshness, nor leaves, nor flowers, nor shade!"

Indeed, "Those are in manifest error" (أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ).

"Qasiyah" is derived from "qaswah," meaning harshness, hardness, and impermeability. Thus, rough stones are called "qasi." For this reason, hearts that show no flexibility toward the light of truth and guidance, do not become soft or submissive, and do not allow the light of guidance to penetrate them, are called "hardened hearts" (qulub qasiyah). In Persian, we refer to this as "sang-deli" (stone-heartedness).

In any case, this expression stands in contrast to "sharh al-sadr" (expansion of the breast) and the spaciousness of the soul, for expansion is a metaphor for readiness to receive. A vast desert or a spacious house is ready to accommodate more people, and an expansive breast and an open soul are ready to accept more truths.

In a narration, we read that Ibn Mas'ud said: We asked the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family) about the interpretation of this verse: "Is one whose breast Allah has opened to Islam and who is upon a light from his Lord...":

How does a person attain expansion of the breast?

He replied: "When the light enters the heart, it expands and opens!" We asked: O Messenger of Allah, what is its sign?

He said: "Turning toward the eternal abode, detaching from the abode of deception, and preparing for death before its arrival." [^1] In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, we read that the phrase "Is one whose breast Allah has opened to Islam" was revealed concerning the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), and in some commentaries, it is stated that the phrase "So woe to those whose hearts are hardened" is about "Abu Lahab and his children." [^2] It is clear that these instances of revelation are, in fact, applications of a general concept to its most obvious examples.

It is noteworthy that in the phrase "and he is upon a light from his Lord," light is mentioned as a mount upon which the believers ride; its speed of travel is wondrous, its path is clear, and its power of movement encompasses the entire world.

[^1]: This verse contains an omitted part (ellipsis) which becomes clear by the context of the following sentence; it is implied as: "Is one whose breast Allah has opened to Islam and who is upon a light from his Lord like one who is hard-hearted and not guided by light?" [^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 8, p. 5691. This hadith is also narrated with slight variations from Rawdat al-Wa'izin by Sheikh al-Mufid. [^2]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verses in question.

Key Points Factors of "Expansion of the Breast" and "Hardness of Heart"

Human beings are not identical in their acceptance of truth, their understanding of matters, and their inner initiative. Some, with a subtle hint or a short word, understand the truth well; a single reminder awakens them, and a single sermon or piece of advice creates a storm in their soul.

Meanwhile, for others, the most intense orations, the most eloquent proofs, and the most powerful advice and exhortations leave not the slightest effect on their being. This is not a simple matter.

What a beautiful expression the Quran uses in this regard, introducing some as possessing "sharh al-sadr" (expansion of the breast) and vastness of soul, and others as having "diq al-sadr" (constriction of the breast). As it says in Surah Al-An'am, verse 125: "Whomever Allah wills to guide, He expands his breast to Islam; and whomever He wills to leave astray, He makes his breast tight and constricted as though he were climbing into the sky." This is a subject that is completely evident when studying the states of individuals. Some have souls so open and expansive that no matter how many truths enter, they are easily accepted. But others, conversely, have souls and thoughts so limited that it is as if there is no room for any truth within them—as if their brains have been placed in a container with powerful iron walls.

Of course, each of these has its factors:

Persistent and continuous study, constant association with scholars and righteous sages, self-purification and refinement of the soul, avoiding sin—especially forbidden food—and the remembrance of Allah are factors for the expansion of the breast.

Conversely, ignorance, sin, stubbornness, disputation, arguing, keeping company with the wicked, the profligate, and criminals, worldliness, and following whims cause the constriction of the soul and hardness of the heart.

And when the Quran says: "Whomever Allah wills to guide, He expands his breast," or if He wills to lead astray, He gives constriction of the breast—this "willing" and "not willing" is not without reason; its sources begin with ourselves.

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), we read: "Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, revealed to Moses: O Moses! Do not rejoice in the abundance of wealth, and do not abandon My remembrance in any state, for the abundance of wealth often causes one to forget sins, and abandoning My remembrance hardens the hearts!" [^1]

In another hadith from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), it is stated: "Tears do not dry up except due to the hardness of hearts, and hearts do not become hard except due to the abundance of sins!" [^2]

In another hadith, it is mentioned that among the messages of the Lord to Moses (peace be upon him) was: "O Moses! Do not lengthen your hopes in the world, for your heart will become hard and inflexible, and the hard-hearted are far from Me!" [^3]

And finally, in another hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (peace be upon him), it is said: "There are two types of inspirations: an inspiration from the Shaytan and an inspiration from the Angel. The inspiration of the Angel results in tenderness and understanding, while the inspiration of the Shaytan results in heedlessness and hardness." [^4]

In any case, to obtain expansion of the breast and to be delivered from hardness of heart, one must turn to the threshold of Allah so that the divine light promised by the Prophet may shine into the heart. One must polish the mirror of the heart from the rust of sin and clear the dwelling of the soul from the refuse of whims and desires so that it becomes ready to host the Beloved. Shedding tears out of the fear of Allah and out of love for that peerless Beloved has a wondrous effect on the tenderness of the heart and the softening and expansion of the soul, while the dryness of the eyes is among the signs of stone-heartedness.

[^1] and [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 55 (Hadiths 23 and 24). [^3]: Al-Kafi, Vol. 2, "Chapter on Hardness," Hadith 1. [^4]: Ibid, Hadith 3.

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْحَدِيثِ كِتَـٰبًا مُّتَشَـٰبِهًا مَّثَانِىَ تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ ثُمَّ تَلِينُ جُلُودُهُمْ وَقُلُوبُهُمْ إِلَىٰ ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ يَهْدِى بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٢٣)
Allah has sent down the best of discourses, a scripture [composed] of similar motifs, whereat shiver the skins of those who fear their Lord, then their skins and hearts relax at Allah’s remembrance. That is Allah’s guidance, by which He guides whomever He wishes; and whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide. (23)
أَفَمَن يَتَّقِى بِوَجْهِهِۦ سُوٓءَ ٱلْعَذَابِ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ وَقِيلَ لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ ذُوقُوا۟ مَا كُنتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ (٢٤)
What! Is someone who fends off with his face the terrible punishment [meted out to him] on the Day of Resurrection…? And the wrongdoers will be told, ‘Taste what you used to earn.’ (24)
كَذَّبَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٥)
Those who were before them impugned [the apostles], whereat the punishment overtook them whence they were not aware. (25)
فَأَذَاقَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخِزْىَ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَعَذَابُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ ۚ لَوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٦)
So Allah made them taste disgrace in the life of the world, and the punishment of the Hereafter will surely be greater, if they knew. (26)

Circumstances of Revelation

Some commentators have narrated from "Abdullah ibn Masud" that one day a group of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) companions, who were feeling a sense of weariness and boredom, said: "O Messenger of Allah! Would that you relate a discourse (hadith) to us so that the rust of weariness might be polished away from our hearts?"

At this point, the first of the aforementioned verses was revealed, introducing the Quran as the "Best of Discourses" (Ahsan al-Hadith) [^1].

Commentary

In the preceding verses, there was mention of those servants who listen to statements and follow the best of them, as well as mention of the "expansion of the breast" (sharh al-sadr) and open hearts that are ready to accept the word of Truth.

In the verses under discussion, the Quran is mentioned in this same context so that, while completing the previous discussions, it may perfect the cycles of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Hereafter by mentioning the proofs of "Prophethood."

First, it says: "Allah has sent down the best of discourses" (اللَّهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ).

Then, it proceeds to describe the merits of the Quran, explaining the great distinctions of this Heavenly Book through three descriptions. First, it says:

"A Book whose verses are consistent and harmonious, and are similar to one another in terms of grace, beauty, and depth of expression" (كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً).

The meaning of "mutashabih" (consistent/similar) here refers to a discourse whose various parts are of the same color and harmony; there is no contradiction or discrepancy within it. It does not contain "good" and "bad" parts; rather, each part is better than the other.

This is exactly contrary to human words; no matter how much care is taken, when they become extensive and vast, discrepancies, contradictions, and inconsistencies inevitably appear in them. Some parts reach the peak of beauty, while others are completely ordinary and mundane. Examining the works of famous and great writers, whether in prose or poetry, is a living testament to this fact.

However, the Word of God, the Holy Quran, is not like this. The extraordinary cohesion, the interconnectedness of concepts, and the peerless eloquence and rhetoric that govern all its verses testify that it is not from the speech of humans.

Then it adds that another characteristic of this Book is that it is "oft-repeated" (مَثانِيَ).

This expression may refer to the repetition of various topics—stories, chronicles, exhortations, and advice—but a repetition that is never wearisome; rather, it is longing-inducing and invigorating. This is one of the important principles of eloquence: that when necessary, one repeats something to create a deep impact, but each time in a fresh style and a new form that does not breed boredom.

Furthermore, the repeated subjects of the Quran interpret one another, and many difficulties are resolved through this method. Some have also considered it a reference to the repeated recitation of the Quran and its not becoming old despite repeated reading.

Others suggest it refers to the repeated revelation of the Quran: once revealed as a whole upon the heart of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) on the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr), and once revealed gradually over 23 years.

There is also the possibility that it refers to the repetition of the Quran's reality in every era and a fresh manifestation from the Unseen with the passing of years and months.

Among these interpretations, the first seems more appropriate, although there is no contradiction between them, and a combination of all of them is possible [^1]. After this description, it addresses the final characteristic of the Quran in this discussion—namely, the issue of its deep and extraordinary influence—saying: "The skins of those who fear their Lord shiver from it (and their hair stands on end); then their skins and their hearts—their exterior and interior—become soft and ready for the remembrance of Allah, and become calm and reassured" (تَقْشَعِرُّ مِنْهُ جُلُودُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ ثُمَّ تَلِينُ جُلُودُهُمْ وَ قُلُوبُهُمْ إِلى‌ ذِكْرِ اللَّهِ).

What a fascinating and beautiful depiction of the wonderful influence of the Quranic verses on ready hearts! First, it creates a fear and awe within them—a fear that is the source of awakening and the start of movement, and a fear that makes a person mindful of his various responsibilities.

In the next stage, it grants him a state of softness and acceptance of the word of Truth, followed by tranquility.

This dual state, which shows the different stages and stations of "the journey towards Allah" (suluk ila Allah), is completely understandable. The verses of wrath and the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) station of warning make hearts tremble, then the verses of mercy grant them peace.

Contemplating the Essence of Truth and the issue of eternity, pre-eternity, and the infinity of His Pure Essence plunges man into awe as to how He can be known; but studying the signs and proofs of that Holy Essence in the horizons and within souls grants him softness and tranquility [^1]. Islamic history is full of signs of the Quran's extraordinary influence on the hearts of believers and even non-believers who had receptive hearts, and this extraordinary influence and attraction is a clear proof that this Book was revealed through revelation.

In a tradition narrated from "Asma," she says: "The companions of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) were such that when the Quran was recited to them—just as Allah described them—their eyes would overflow with tears and their bodies would shiver!" [^2] and [^3].

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), has described this reality regarding the pious in the most sublime manner where he says:

"As for the night, they stand on their feet, reciting the parts of the Quran, chanting it with measured recitation, saddening their souls with it and seeking through it the cure for their ailment. When they come across a verse containing encouragement, they lean towards it out of desire and their souls gaze upon it with longing, thinking it is before their very eyes. And when they come across a verse containing fear, they turn the ears of their hearts to it, as if the moaning and roaring of the flames of Hell are echoing in the depths of their ears."

At the end of the verse, after stating these descriptions, it says: "That is the guidance of Allah; He guides therewith whom He wills" (ذلِكَ هُدَى اللَّهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ).

It is true that the Quran was revealed for the guidance of all, but only the seekers of Truth, the searchers for reality, and the pious benefit from its light of guidance. Those who have deliberately closed the windows of their hearts to it, and upon whose souls the darkness of prejudice and stubbornness reigns, not only do they not benefit from it, but their deviation increases due to their enmity and hostility. Therefore, following this statement, He says: "And whomsoever Allah leaves to stray, for him there will be no guide" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ).

This is a straying whose foundations were laid by the person himself, and whose infrastructure was strengthened by his own wrong actions; for this reason, it has the slightest conflict with the principle of human choice and free will.

In the next verse, He compares the group of oppressors and criminals with the group of believers whose state was previously described, so that in this comparison, realities may become clearer. He says: "Is he, then, who wards off the painful punishment of Allah with his face like one who spends that day in ultimate security and the fire of Hell never reaches him?" (أَ فَمَنْ يَتَّقِي بِوَجْهِهِ سُوءَ الْعَذابِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ) [^1]. A point that is necessary to note here is that it says:

"He wards off the punishment from himself with his face." This expression is because the "face" (wajh) is the most noble of human organs, containing the important senses (eyes, ears, nose, and tongue), and fundamentally, the identification of humans is done through the face. For these reasons, when a danger approaches it, a person places his hands, arms, and other limbs as a shield in front of it to ward off the danger.

But the state of the oppressive dwellers of Hell on that day is such that they must defend themselves with their faces, because their hands and feet are in fetters and chains, as we read in Surah Ya-Sin, verse 8: "We have put yokes round their necks (in which their hands are also placed) right up to their chins, so that their heads are forced up."

Some have also said this expression is because they are cast into the Fire on their faces; thus, the first organ of theirs to reach the Fire is the face, as stated in Surah An-Naml, verse 90: "And whoever brings an evil deed, their faces will be cast down into the Fire."

It has also been said that this expression is merely a metaphor for their inability to defend themselves against the Fire of Hell.

These three interpretations are not contradictory and may be combined in the concept of the verse.

Then, at the end of the verse, it adds: "On that day it will be said to the oppressors: 'Taste what you used to earn!'" (وَ قِيلَ لِلظَّالِمِينَ ذُوقُوا ما كُنْتُمْ تَكْسِبُونَ).

Yes, the angels of punishment express this painful reality to them: that these are your very deeds placed beside you, tormenting you. This statement itself is another psychological torture for them.

It is noteworthy that it does not say: "Taste the punishment for your deeds," but rather says: "Taste your deeds," and this is another witness to the issue of the "embodiment of deeds" (tajassum al-a'mal).

What has been said so far was a brief reference to their painful punishments in the Hereafter. The next verse speaks of their worldly punishment, lest they imagine they will be safe in this worldly life. It says: "Those before them denied [the truth], so the punishment came upon them from where they did not perceive" (كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ).

If a person receives a blow from where he expects it, it will not be very painful; more painful than that is to receive a blow from where he never expects it—for example, from his closest friends, from his most beloved means of life, from the water that is the source of his life, from the breeze that is the source of his vigor, or from the calm earth which is considered his place of rest, security, and peace.

Yes, the descent of divine punishment through these ways is very painful, and this is exactly what we read regarding the people of Noah, 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Lot, Pharaoh, Korah, and their like—that each was afflicted with punishment through one of these ways they never expected.

In the last verse under discussion, it shows that their worldly punishment was not only physical but was also a psychological penalty. It says: "So Allah made them taste disgrace in the life of this world" (فَأَذاقَهُمُ اللَّهُ الْخِزْيَ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا) [^1]. Yes, if a person is afflicted with a calamity but dies with honor and his head held high, it is not as significant; what is significant is to die with humiliation and abasement, and to be caught in the clutches of punishment with dishonor and scandal.

"But the punishment of the Hereafter is surely greater, if they but knew" (وَ لَعَذابُ الْآخِرَةِ أَكْبَرُ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ).

The expression "greater" (akbar) is a metaphor for the intensity and severity of the punishment.

Key Point

Regarding these verses, traditions have been narrated that manifest wider horizons of the verses' concepts before us.

In a tradition, "Abbas," the uncle of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), narrates from him that he said:

"When the skin of a servant shivers from the fear of Allah, his sins fall away just as the leaves fall from a dry tree" [^1]. It is clear that one who is so affected by divine fear possesses a state of repentance and turning back (inabah), and such a person will certainly be forgiven by the Lord.

In another tradition narrated from "Asma," which we mentioned in the commentary of the verses, we read: when she was asked about the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), she said:

"When they recited the Quran—just as Allah described them—their eyes would overflow with tears and their bodies would shiver." Then the narrator says: I asked Asma, "There are people among us who, when they hear the verses of the Quran, fall into a faint and become intoxicated and unconscious." Asma said: "I seek refuge in Allah the Almighty from Satan (this is a satanic act!)" [^2]. This tradition is, in fact, an answer to those who claim Sufism and form sessions and circles, reciting verses and litanies, then making movements, coming into a state of so-called ecstasy and joy, shouting, screaming, and throwing themselves into a faint, and perhaps some even pass out. Such matters were never narrated regarding the states of the Prophet's companions and are among the innovations (bid'ah) of the Sufis.

Of course, it is possible for a person to sometimes become unconscious from the intensity of fear, but this is very different from the practices of the Sufis who form sessions for litanies and incantations in the manner mentioned above.
