[^2]: Alusi narrated this tradition in Ruh al-Ma'ani (Vol. 23, p. 235); other commentators have also narrated it under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ ضَرَبْنَا لِلنَّاسِ فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٢٧)
We have drawn for mankind in this Quran every [kind of] example, so that they may take admonition: (27)
قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِى عِوَجٍ لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (٢٨)
an Arabic Quran, without any deviousness, so that they may be Godwary. (28)
ضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَّجُلًا فِيهِ شُرَكَآءُ مُتَشَـٰكِسُونَ وَرَجُلًا سَلَمًا لِّرَجُلٍ هَلْ يَسْتَوِيَانِ مَثَلًا ۚ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٢٩)
Allah draws an example: a man jointly owned by several contending masters, and a man belonging entirely to one man: are the two equal in comparison? All praise belongs to Allah! But most of them do not know. (29)
إِنَّكَ مَيِّتٌ وَإِنَّهُم مَّيِّتُونَ (٣٠)
You will die, and they [too] will die. (30)
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ تَخْتَصِمُونَ (٣١)
Then on the Day of Resurrection you will contend before your Lord. (31)

Commentary

The Quran in Which There Is No Crookedness

In these verses, the discussion continues regarding the Holy Quran and its characteristics, completing the previous discussions on this subject.

First, it speaks of the comprehensiveness of the Quran as follows: "And We have certainly presented for the people in this Quran from every [kind of] example" (وَلَقَدْ ضَرَبْنَا لِلنَّاسِ فِي هَذَا الْقُرْآنِ مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ).

From the painful stories of past oppressors and rebels, the terrifying consequences of sin, various types of advice and exhortations, the secrets of creation and the system of the universe, to firm laws and regulations—in short, whatever was necessary for the guidance of humanity, We have explained to them in the form of parables and examples.

"That perhaps they will remember" and return from the path of error to the Straight Path (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ).

Considering that "mathal" (example/parable) in the Arabic language refers to any speech that personifies a truth, describes something, or likens one thing to another, this expression encompasses all the truths and subjects of the Quran and highlights its comprehensiveness.

Then, it proceeds to another description of the Quran, saying: "[It is] an Arabic Quran, without any crookedness, deviation, contradiction, or inconsistency" (قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا غَيْرَ ذِي عِوَجٍ) [1]. In fact, three descriptions for the Quran are mentioned here:

First, the term "Quran," which refers to the fact that these verses are repeatedly recited—in prayer and outside of it, in private and in public, throughout the entire history of Islam, and until the end of the world. Thus, it is a light of guidance that shines perpetually.

Second is the issue of the eloquence, sweetness, and attractiveness of this Divine speech, which is expressed as "Arabiyyan" (Arabic), because one of the meanings of "Arabi" is "eloquent," and this is the intended meaning here.

Third is that no crookedness or deviation finds its way into it; its verses are harmonious, its expressions are lucid, and its phrases interpret one another [2]. Many linguists and commentators have stated that "iwaj" (with a kasra on the ayn) refers to spiritual or abstract deviations, while "awaj" (with a fatha on the ayn) is used for physical or apparent crookedness. (Of course, the first term has rarely been used for physical crookedness as well, such as in verse 107 of Surah Ta-Ha: "لَا تَرَىٰ فِيهَا عِوَجًا وَلَا أَمْتًا" — "You will not see therein any crookedness or any curve." Therefore, some linguists consider the first term to be more general) [3]. In any case, the purpose of revealing the Quran with all these attributes was "that perhaps they will become righteous" (لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ).

[1]: From the perspective of grammar, "قُرْآنًا عَرَبِيًّا" is a hal (circumstantial adverb) for "al-Quran" mentioned previously. However, since "Quranan" does not have a descriptive aspect, some consider it a preparation for the hal which is "Arabiyyan," some take it to mean "maqru'an" (that which is recited) which has a descriptive meaning, and some consider it mansub (accusative) due to a hidden verb.

[2]: Considering that "iwaj" is used as an indefinite noun in a negative context, it denotes generality and negates every type of deviation and crookedness from the Quran.

[3]: Refer to "Mufradat al-Raghib," "Lisan al-Arab," and various commentaries.

It is noteworthy that at the end of the previous verse, it said "لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ" (perhaps they will remember), and here it says "لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ" (perhaps they will be righteous), because "remembrance" (tadhakkur) is always a prelude to "piety" (taqwa), and righteousness is the fruit of the tree of "remembrance."

Then, the Quran proceeds to mention one of these examples and portrays the fate of the monotheist and the polytheist in the form of an eloquent and beautiful parable: "Allah presents an example: a man owned by [quarreling] partners who are constantly in dispute over him" (ضَرَبَ اللَّهُ مَثَلًا رَجُلًا فِيهِ شُرَكاءُ مُتَشاكِسُونَ) [1]. He is a slave with several masters, each of whom commands him to do something; one says: "Perform such a task," and the other forbids it. He is bewildered and confused in the meantime, left helpless amidst these contradictory orders, not knowing whose tune to harmonize with! Worse still, to provide for his life's needs, this one refers him to that one, and that one to this one; from this perspective, he is also deprived, wretched, destitute, and wandering. "And [He mentions] a man belonging entirely to one man" (وَ رَجُلًا سَلَماً لِرَجُلٍ). His path and program are clear, his master is known; he is neither caught in doubt nor confusion, neither contradiction nor inconsistency. He walks with a calm spirit and moves forward with confidence, being under the guardianship of someone who supports him in everything, at all times, and everywhere.

[1]: "Mutashakisun" is from the root "shakasah," meaning ill-temper, quarreling, and hostility. Therefore, "mutashakis" refers to those who are engaged in arguing and fighting with anger and bad temper.

"Are the two equal in comparison?" (هَلْ يَسْتَوِيانِ مَثَلًا).

And such is the state of the "polytheist" and the "monotheist": polytheists are submerged in various contradictions and inconsistencies; every day they set their hearts on a deity, and at every moment they turn to a master—no peace, no confidence, and no clear path.

But the monotheists have their hearts bound to the love of Allah; they have chosen Him from the entire world, and in all circumstances, they take refuge in the shadow of His grace, which is above all things. They have turned their eyes away from everything other than Allah and fixed their gaze upon Him; their path and program are clear, and their destiny and end are bright.

In a narration from Ali عليه السلام, it is reported that he said: "I am that man who was entirely submissive (salam) to the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله." [1] In another hadith, it is mentioned: "The man who was truly submissive to the man was Ali عليه السلام and his Shia." [2] And at the end of the verse, He says: "Praise be to Allah" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ).

Allah, who by mentioning these clear examples has shown you the way and provided you with clear proofs to distinguish truth from falsehood; Allah, who invites everyone to sincerity (ikhlas) and grants peace in the shadow of sincerity. What blessing is greater than this? And what gratitude and praise are more necessary than this?

"But most of them do not know," and despite these clear proofs, they do not find the way to the truth due to the love of the world and rebellious desires (بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding monotheism and polytheism, the next verse speaks of the results of monotheism and polytheism on the stage of Resurrection.

First, it begins with the issue of "death," which is the gateway to the Resurrection, and clarifying the universality of the law of death for all human beings, it says: "Indeed, you are to die, and indeed they are to die"! (إِنَّكَ مَيِّتٌ وَإِنَّهُمْ مَيِّتُونَ) [1]. Yes, "death" is one of the matters in which all humans are equal; there is no exception or difference in it. It is a path that everyone must eventually traverse, and as the saying goes, it is a camel that kneels at everyone's door.

Some commentators have said that the enemies of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were waiting for his death and were happy that he would eventually die! In this verse, the Quran responds to them: if the Prophet dies, will you remain alive?

In verse 34 of Surah Al-Anbiya, it is also stated: "Then if you die, are they to live forever?" (أَفَإِنْ مِتَّ فَهُمُ الْخَالِدُونَ).

Then, it takes the discussion to the court of the Resurrection, portraying the dispute of the servants on the scene of the Gathering, and says: "Then indeed, on the Day of Resurrection, before your Lord, you will dispute" (ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ عِنْدَ رَبِّكُمْ تَخْتَصِمُونَ). "Takhtasimun" is from the root "ikhtisam," meaning a quarrel or dispute between two people or two groups, where each wants to invalidate the words of the other. Sometimes one is in the right and the other in the wrong, and sometimes both may be in the wrong, such as the dispute of the people of falsehood with one another.

[1]: Of course, the sentence "إِنَّكَ مَيِّتٌ وَإِنَّهُمْ مَيِّتُونَ" apparently informs of everyone being dead at the present moment, but it is of the type called "the present tense of certain occurrence," which is sometimes presented in the present or past tense.

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether this ruling is general or not:

Some have imagined that this "dispute" is between the Muslims and the disbelievers.

Some have said that a dispute may also exist between Muslims and the People of the Qibla. Here, a hadith is narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri: "In the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we never thought there would be a dispute among us Muslims. We said: our Lord is one, our Prophet is one, and our religion and creed are one; despite this, how is hostility possible? Until the day of Siffin arrived, and two groups, both apparently Muslim (though one was a true Muslim and the other a claimant of Islam), drew swords against each other. We said: Yes, the verse includes us too!" [1] However, the subsequent verses show that this dispute will be between the prophets and the believers on one side, and the polytheists and deniers on the other.

It is well-known in the history of Islam that Umar, after the passing of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, denied his death and said: "It is impossible for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to die; he has gone to his Lord just as Musa ibn Imran was absent from his people for forty nights, then returned to them. By Allah, the Messenger of Allah shall return just as Musa returned. Those who imagine the Prophet has died should have their hands and feet cut off!" This news reached Abu Bakr; he came to him and recited some verses that indicated the death of the Prophet. Umar became silent and said: "By Allah, this was the first time I heard this verse." [2]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 8, p. 497.

[2]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 4, pp. 305-306 (summarized). This incident is also narrated in Al-Kamil by Ibn al-Athir, Vol. 2, pp. 323-324.

Part 24 of the Holy Quran

Ayahs

۞ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن كَذَبَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وَكَذَّبَ بِٱلصِّدْقِ إِذْ جَآءَهُۥٓ ۚ أَلَيْسَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوًى لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٢)
So who is a greater wrongdoer than him who attributes a falsehood to Allah, and denies the truth when it reaches him? Is not the [final] abode of the faithless in hell? (32)
وَٱلَّذِى جَآءَ بِٱلصِّدْقِ وَصَدَّقَ بِهِۦٓ ۙ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُتَّقُونَ (٣٣)
He who brings the truth and he who confirms it—it is they who are the Godwary. (33)
لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣٤)
They will have what they wish with their Lord—that is the reward of the virtuous— (34)
لِيُكَفِّرَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ أَسْوَأَ ٱلَّذِى عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَجْزِيَهُمْ أَجْرَهُم بِأَحْسَنِ ٱلَّذِى كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٣٥)
that Allah may absolve them of the worst of what they did, and pay them their reward by the best of what they used to do. (35)

Commentary

Those Who Attest to the Word of Allah

The discussion previously centered on the presence of people at the scene of Resurrection and the disputes in that great court. These verses continue that same discussion, dividing people into two groups: "the deniers" and "the affirmers."

The first group is described with two characteristics, as He says:

"Who, then, is more unjust than he who lies against Allah and denies the truth when it comes to him?" (فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ كَذَبَ عَلَى اللَّهِ وَ كَذَّبَ بِالصِّدْقِ إِذْ جاءَهُ‌).

The faithless and the polytheists frequently fabricated lies against Allah; sometimes they called the angels His daughters, sometimes they claimed Jesus was His son, sometimes they considered idols as intercessors before Him, and sometimes they forged false laws regarding the lawful and the prohibited and attributed them to Him, and the like.

As for the "truth" (صدق) that came to them and which they denied, it was the heavenly revelation of the Glorious Quran.

At the end of the verse, in a short sentence, He describes the punishment for such individuals:

"Is there not in Hell an abode for the disbelievers?" (أَ لَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْكافِرِينَ‌) [^1]. When the name "Hell" is mentioned, all other painful punishments are summarized within it.

Regarding the second group, He also mentions two descriptions, saying: "And he who brings the truth and he who confirms it—those are the righteous" (وَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِالصِّدْقِ وَ صَدَّقَ بِهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُتَّقُونَ‌).

In some narrations transmitted from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, "he who brings the truth" (وَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِالصِّدْقِ‌) is interpreted as the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the phrase "and he who confirms it" (وَ صَدَّقَ بِهِ‌) is interpreted as Ali عليه السلام [^1]. However, this is intended to highlight the most prominent examples, as the phrase "those are the righteous" (أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُتَّقُونَ‌) indicates the general applicability of the verse.

From this, it becomes clear that interpreting the above verse as referring to the person of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله—who was both the bringer of revelation and its confirmer—must also be considered an instance of identifying a prime example rather than defining the entire scope of the verse's meaning.

Therefore, a group of commentators has interpreted the phrase "he who brings the truth" as referring to all the Prophets, and the phrase "confirms it" as referring to their true followers, who together constitute the righteous of the world.

There is another interesting interpretation of the verse which is the most expansive and comprehensive, though it has received less attention from commentators, yet it is more consistent with the literal wording of the verses. It suggests that "he who brings the truth" is not limited to the messengers of revelation; rather, all those who have been proclaimers of their school of thought and promoters of the words of truth and sincerity are placed in this rank. In this case, there is no obstacle to both phrases applying to a single group (as the literal expression of the verse suggests, since "the one who" [الذى] is mentioned only once).

In this way, the discussion is about those who are both bringers of truth and practitioners of it; it speaks of those who have spread the school of revelation and the true word of the Lord in the world, and who themselves believe in it and act upon it—including the Prophets, the Infallible Imams عليه السلام, and the explainers of their school.

It is noteworthy that instead of "revelation," the term "truth" (صدق) is used, indicating that the only word in which there is no possibility of falsehood or error is the word revealed from the Lord through revelation. Furthermore, piety and righteousness only flourish under the shadow of the teachings of the Prophets' school and the internal affirmation of it within souls.

In the following verse, He mentions three great rewards for this group: First, He says: "They shall have whatever they desire with their Lord. That is the reward of the doers of good" (لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌).

The scope of this verse's meaning is so vast that it encompasses all spiritual blessings and material bounties—whatever can be imagined or conceived, and even what cannot.

Some have raised a question here: if they request the stations of the Prophets and Saints who are higher than them, will those also be granted?

They overlook that the inhabitants of Paradise, possessing the eye of truth-seeing, never think of anything that contradicts truth, justice, or the principle of balance between "merit" and "reward."

In other words, it is impossible for individuals who differ in degrees of faith and action to have identical rewards. How could the inhabitants of Paradise wish for the impossible? At the same time, they are spiritually such that they are satisfied with what they have, and no envy or jealousy governs their existence.

We know that the rewards of the Hereafter, and even Divine favors, are based on the merits that a person acquires in this world. One who knows that his faith and actions in this world were not at the level of others' faith and actions will never desire their station, for it would be an illogical wish.

The expression "with their Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌) expresses the ultimate Divine grace toward them, as if they are always His guests and have whatever they desire in His presence.

The expression "That is the reward of the doers of good" (ذلِكَ جَزاءُ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌)—using a noun instead of a pronoun—indicates that the primary cause of these rewards is their very "Ihsan" (excellence/good-doing).

He describes the second and third rewards for them as follows: "That Allah may remit from them the worst of what they did and reward them their due for the best of what they used to do" (لِيُكَفِّرَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ أَسْوَأَ الَّذِي عَمِلُوا وَ يَجْزِيَهُمْ أَجْرَهُمْ بِأَحْسَنِ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌) [^1]. What a beautiful expression! On one hand, they have the request that their worst deeds be covered by Divine grace and that these stains be washed from their record with the water of repentance. On the other hand, their request is that Allah makes their best deeds the criterion for their reward and accepts all their actions based on that standard! And Allah, with the expression used in these verses, has accepted their request: He forgives the worst and makes the best the criterion for reward.

Naturally, when the greater lapses are covered by Divine forgiveness, the rest will be covered all the more so. The main point is that a person's anxiety stems mostly from major lapses, and for this reason, believers are more concerned about them.

Here, the question arises: were the previous verses not speaking of the Prophets and their followers? How can they have "worst deeds" or major lapses?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: when an action is attributed to a group, it does not mean that every single one of them committed it; rather, it is sufficient that a group among them performed it. For example, we say: "The Umayyads sat unjustly upon the throne of the Prophet's caliphate." This does not mean every one of them became a caliph, but rather that a group of them did so.

In the above verse as well, among the collective of the messengers of revelation and the followers of their school, some had lapses which Allah overlooks because of their good deeds.

In any case, the mention of "forgiveness and remission" before the reward is because they must first be washed and purified, and then step upon the carpet of proximity to God. They must first be at peace regarding Divine punishment so that the blessings of Paradise may be pleasant for them.

Key Point: Who Was the First "Siddiq" (Truthful Confirmer)?

Many Muslim commentators, both Shi'a and Sunni, have narrated this tradition in the interpretation of the verse "And he who brings the truth and he who confirms it" (وَ الَّذِي جاءَ بِالصِّدْقِ وَ صَدَّقَ بِهِ‌), stating that "he who brings the truth" refers to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and "he who confirms it" refers to Ali عليه السلام.

The great scholar of Islam, Tabarsi, in Majma' al-Bayan, and Abu al-Futuh Razi in the commentary Ruh al-Jinan, have narrated this from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام.

However, a group of Sunni scholars and commentators have also narrated it from Abu Hurayrah from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله or through other chains, including: Allamah Ibn al-Maghazili in Al-Manaqib, Allamah Ganji in Kifayat al-Talib, the famous commentator Qurtubi in his Tafsir, Allamah Suyuti in Al-Durr al-Manthur, and also Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani [^1]. As we mentioned before, such interpretations are to highlight the clearest examples, and undoubtedly Ali عليه السلام stands in the foremost rank among the followers and confirmers of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله and is his first "Siddiq."

No scholar of Islam denies the fact that Ali عليه السلام was the first among men to confirm the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

The only criticism raised by some is their claim that he embraced Islam when he was 10 or 12 years old and his Islam at that time was not "official"! But this statement seems very strange, for how could such a thing be correct when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله accepted his Islam, addressed him as his "Vizier" and "Successor," and repeatedly referred to him in his words as "the first of the believers" (Awwal al-Mu'minin) or "the first of you in Islam" (Awwalukum Islaman)? We have provided the documentation for this from the books of Sunni scholars in the eighth volume of this Tafsir under the commentary of Surah At-Tawbah, verse 10 (page 103 onwards) in detail.

[^1]: "Mathwa" (abode) is from the root "thawa," meaning residence accompanied by continuity. Therefore, "mathwa" here means a permanent and eternal place. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed. [^1]: Regarding the grammatical connection of "That Allah may remit from them" (لِيُكَفِّرَ اللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ‌), many possibilities have been suggested. However, what seems most appropriate in terms of meaning is that it is connected to the verb "they did good" (ahsanū) understood from the word "the doers of good" (al-muhsinīn). The implied meaning is: "That is the reward of the doers of good; they did good so that Allah might remit their lapses...". Yes, they pursued good deeds so that Allah would forgive their slips and give them the best of rewards. [^1]: For further explanation, refer to Ihqaq al-Haqq, vol. 3, p. 177 onwards, and Al-Muraja'at, p. 64 (Letter 12).

Ayahs

أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِكَافٍ عَبْدَهُۥ ۖ وَيُخَوِّفُونَكَ بِٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يُضْلِلِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِنْ هَادٍ (٣٦)
Does not Allah suffice [to defend] His servant? They would frighten you of others than Him. Yet whomever Allah leads astray, has no guide, (36)
وَمَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن مُّضِلٍّ ۗ أَلَيْسَ ٱللَّهُ بِعَزِيزٍ ذِى ٱنتِقَامٍ (٣٧)
and whomever Allah guides, there is no one who can lead him astray. Is not Allah an all-mighty avenger? (37)

Circumstances of Revelation

Many commentators have narrated that the idolaters of Mecca would warn the Prophet ص from the wrath and anger of the idols, saying: "Do not speak ill of them, and do not act against them, for they will strike you with madness and cause you harm!" (The above verse was revealed and responded to them) [1]. Some have also narrated that when Khalid, by the command of the Prophet ص, was commissioned to break the famous idol "Uzza," the polytheists said: "O Khalid! Beware, for the wrath of this idol is severe! (And it will bring you to ruin)." Khalid, with the axe he held in his hand, struck the nose of that idol firmly, shattered it, and said: Kufran laki ya 'Uzza, la subhanaki—subhana man ahanaki, inni ra'aytu Allaha qad ahanaki!: "Ungratefulness be to you, O Uzza! You are never sanctified; sanctified is He who has humiliated you! I have seen that Allah has humiliated you!" [1] However, the story of Khalid, which naturally occurred after the Conquest of Mecca, cannot be considered the primary circumstance of revelation, as the entire Surah Az-Zumar is Meccan; therefore, it may be a case of application (tatbiq).

Commentary

"Allah" is Sufficient!

Following the threats that Allah expressed in the previous verses toward the polytheists, and the promises He gave to His Prophet, the first verse under discussion addresses the threats of the disbelievers, saying: "Is Allah not sufficient for His servant [to save and protect him] against the enemies? Yet they threaten you with those [idols] besides Him and frighten you with other than Him" (أَ لَيْسَ اللَّهُ بِكافٍ عَبْدَهُ وَ يُخَوِّفُونَكَ بِالَّذِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِ).

How is it possible for Allah—whose power is superior to all powers, who is well aware of the needs and difficulties of His servants, and who possesses the ultimate grace and mercy toward them—to leave His faithful servants alone against the storm of events and the waves of enemy hostility? When He is the supporter of His servant: If the sword of the world moves from its place, It shall not cut a vein if God does not will so! And when He wishes to help someone:

[1]: Tafsir "Kashshaf," "Majma' al-Bayan," "Abu al-Futuh Razi," and "Fi Zilal" (with variations in expression). [1]: "Majma' al-Bayan" under the verses discussed (this narration also appears more briefly in "Kashshaf" and "Qurtubi").

If a thousand enemies intend my destruction, If You are my friend, I have no fear of enemies! Let alone the idols, which are worthless and useless entities.

Although the circumstance of revelation for the verse, according to the narrated report, concerns the intimidation and threats regarding the wrath of idols, the concept of the verse is so vast and expansive that it includes every type of threat from other than Allah. In any case, this verse is a glad tiding for all seekers of the path of truth and true believers, especially in environments where they are in the minority and threatened from every side.

This verse grants them encouragement and steadfastness, fills their souls with vitality, firms their steps, and neutralizes the harmful psychological effects of the enemies' threats. Indeed, when Allah is with us, we have no fear of anyone else, and if we become estranged and separated from Him, everything becomes terrifying for us.

In the continuation of this verse and the following one, it refers to the issue of "guidance" and "misguidance" and the division of people into two groups—the "astray" and the "guided"—stating that all of this is from Allah, so it may become clear that all servants are in need of His threshold, and nothing happens in the universe without His will. It says: "And whomever Allah leaves to stray, for him there is no guide" (وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ هادٍ).

"And whomever Allah guides, for him there is no misguider" (وَ مَنْ يَهْدِ اللَّهُ فَما لَهُ مِنْ مُضِلٍ).

It is obvious that neither is that misguidance without reason, nor is this guidance without calculation; rather, each is a continuation of the human's own will and effort. He who steps onto the path of misguidance and strives with all his might to extinguish the light of truth, missing no opportunity to deceive others, and is immersed from head to toe in sin and rebellion—it is natural that Allah leaves him to stray. Not only does He withdraw His success (tawfiq) from him, but He also disables his power of perception and discernment, sets a seal upon his heart, and casts a veil over his eyes, which is the result of the deeds he performs. But those who, with sincerity of intention, aim for the "journey toward Allah" (sayr ila Allah), have provided its means, and have taken the first steps, the light of Divine guidance rushes to their aid. The angels of truth come to their assistance, cleansing the whispers of devils from their hearts, strengthening their will, and firming their steps; and at the points of slipping, the hand of Divine grace supports them.

These are matters to which numerous verses of the Holy Quran bear witness. How unaware are those who have severed the connection of such verses from other Quranic verses and taken them as evidence for the school of fatalism (jabr), as if they do not know that the verses of the Quran interpret one another.

In fact, the end of this very verse under discussion is a clear witness to this meaning, for it says: "Is Allah not Exalted in Might and Owner of Retribution?" (أَ لَيْسَ اللَّهُ بِعَزِيزٍ ذِي انْتِقامٍ).

We know that retribution from Allah means punishment for the wrong deeds that have been committed. This shows that His "misguiding" has a punitive aspect and is a reaction to the actions of the humans themselves; naturally, His "guidance" also has an aspect of reward and is a reaction to sincere and pure deeds and striving in the way of "Allah" [1].

[1]: Raghib in Al-Mufradat says that "niqmah" means "uqubah" (punishment) and penalty.

Key Point

Guidance and Misguidance are from Allah

Guidance (hidayah) linguistically means direction and guidance accompanied by grace and precision [1]. It has been divided into two branches: "showing the way" (ira'at al-tariq) and "delivering to the goal" (isal ila al-matlub); in other words, "legislative guidance" (hidayah tashri'i) and "existential guidance" (hidayah takwini) [2]. To explain: sometimes a person shows the way with total precision, grace, and favor to one who seeks it, but traversing the path and reaching the destination is the responsibility of the seeker himself.

But sometimes He takes the hands of the seekers and, in addition to showing the way, delivers them to the destination.

In other words, in the first stage, He only explains the law and states the conditions for traversing the path and reaching the destination; but in the second stage, in addition to this, He provides the means of travel, removes obstacles, solves problems, and accompanies, supports, and protects the travelers of this path until the destination.

Of course, the opposite of this is "misguidance" (idlal).

A brief look at the verses of the Quran clearly reveals that the Quran considers guidance and misguidance to be the act of Allah and attributes both to Him. If we were to count all the verses that speak on this subject, the discussion would become lengthy. It is sufficient that in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 213, we read:

[1]: Al-Mufradat, root "h-d-y". [2]: Note that "existential guidance" here is taken in a broad sense that includes any kind of guidance except through the explanation of laws and showing the way.

وَ اللَّهُ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ: "And Allah guides whom He wills to a straight path."

And in Surah An-Nahl, verse 93, it says: وَ لكِنْ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ: "But He causes to stray whom He wills and guides whom He wills."

Similar expressions regarding guidance and misguidance, or one of the two, are found in many verses of the Holy Quran [1]. Beyond this, in some verses, guidance is explicitly negated from the Prophet of Islam ص and attributed to Allah, as we read in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 56: إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ: "Indeed, [O Muhammad], you do not guide whom you like, but Allah guides whom He wills!" And in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 272, we read: لَيْسَ عَلَيْكَ هُداهُمْ وَ لكِنَّ اللَّهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ: "Not upon you is their guidance, but Allah guides whom He wills."

A superficial study of these verses and a failure to understand their deep meaning has caused a group to fall into "misguidance" in their interpretation, deviating from the path of "guidance" and becoming buried under the debris of the school of "fatalism" (jabr). Even some famous commentators have not remained immune to this pest and have fallen into this terrifying precipice, to the extent that they considered guidance and misguidance to be fatalistic in all stages. Surprisingly, because the contradiction of this belief with the principle of Allah's Justice and Wisdom was clear, they preferred to deny the principle of Justice rather than correct their error. Fundamentally, if we believe in the principle of fatalism, no meaning remains for duty (taklif), responsibility, the sending of messengers, and the revelation of Heavenly Books.

[1]: As examples, one can name verses: Fatir 8, Az-Zumar 23, Al-Muddaththir 31, Al-Baqarah 272, Al-An'am 88, Yunus 25, Ar-Ra'd 27, Ibrahim 4.

However, those who support the school of free will (ikhtiyar) and believe that no sound intellect can accept that God forces a group to traverse the path of misguidance and then punishes them for this forced act, or forces a group to be guided and then grants them reward without reason—granting them an advantage for an act they did not perform themselves—have chosen other ways to interpret these verses, the most important of which are:

  1. The meaning of Divine guidance is "legislative guidance," which takes place through revelation, Heavenly Books, the sending of Prophets and their successors, as well as the perception of intellect and conscience. However, traversing the path in all stages is the responsibility of the human himself. Of course, this interpretation is consistent with many verses of guidance, but many others cannot be interpreted this way, as they are explicit regarding "existential guidance" and "delivering to the goal," such as verse 56 of Surah Al-Qasas which says: "You do not guide whom you like, but Allah guides whom He wills," for we know that legislative guidance and showing the way is the primary duty of the Prophets.

  2. Another group of commentators has interpreted guidance and misguidance, where they have an existential aspect, as the matter of reward and punishment, and delivering to the path of Paradise or Hell. They said Allah guides the righteous to the path of Paradise and misguides the wicked from it. Of course, this meaning is correct, but only for some verses; for other verses, it is inconsistent with the absolute nature of the words "guidance" and "misguidance" and the lack of conditions or restrictions in them.

  3. Another group has said that guidance means providing the means and preliminaries for reaching the goal, and misguidance means not providing them or removing them, which some have expressed as "success" (tawfiq) and "withdrawal of success" (salb al-tawfiq). This is because tawfiq is the provision of preliminaries to reach the goal, and salb al-tawfiq is their removal. Therefore, Divine guidance is not that Allah forcibly brings humans to the destination, but that He places the means at their disposal. For example, the existence of a good mentor, a healthy educational environment, righteous friends and associates, and the like are all preliminaries. Yet, despite all these, the human is not forced to traverse the path of guidance; rather, he can turn his back on all of them and take the path of misguidance. However, the question remains in this interpretation: why do these successes include one group while another group is deprived of them? Supporters of this interpretation, considering that Allah's actions are wise, must mention reasons for this difference—for example, saying that performing a good deed leads to Divine success, and performing evil deeds strips the human of success. In any case, this is a good interpretation, but the matter is even deeper than that.

  4. The most precise interpretation that is consistent with all verses of guidance and misguidance and interprets them all well without the slightest deviation from the literal meaning is to say: Legislative guidance, in the sense of "showing the way," has a general and universal aspect and has no conditions or restrictions, as stated in Surah Al-Insan, verse 3: إِنَّا هَدَيْناهُ السَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شاكِراً وَ إِمَّا كَفُوراً: "Indeed, We guided him to the way, be he grateful or ungrateful." And in Surah Ash-Shura, verse 52, we read: وَ إِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ: "And indeed, you guide to a straight path." It is obvious that the Prophet's invitation is the manifestation of Allah's invitation, for whatever he has is from Allah. And regarding a group of deviants and polytheists, it says in Surah An-Najm, verse 23: وَ لَقَدْ جاءَهُمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمُ الْهُدى‌: "And there has already come to them from their Lord guidance."

However, existential guidance, in the sense of "delivering to the goal," taking the hands of the servants and passing them through all the twists and turns of the path, and protecting and supporting them until reaching the shore of salvation—which is the subject of many other Quranic verses—is never unconditional. This guidance is specific to a group whose attributes are described in the Quran, and misguidance, which is its opposite, is also specific to a group whose attributes are also described. Although some verses are absolute, many other verses precisely state the conditions and restrictions. When we place these "absolute" and "restricted" verses together, the matter becomes completely clear, and no ambiguity or doubt remains in the meaning of the verses. Not only is it not contrary to the issue of human choice and free will, but it precisely emphasizes it.

Now consider the following explanation

The Holy Quran says in one place: يُضِلُّ بِهِ كَثِيراً وَ يَهْدِي بِهِ كَثِيراً وَ ما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ: "By it He misleads many and many He guides; but He misleads not except the defiantly disobedient (fasiqun)" (Al-Baqarah 26). Here, the source of misguidance is identified as fisq (disobedience) and exiting from Divine obedience and command.

In another place, it says: وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ: "And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people" (Al-Baqarah 258). Here, the emphasis is on the issue of injustice (zulm), introducing it as the groundwork for misguidance.

In another place, we read: وَ اللَّهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْكافِرِينَ: "And Allah does not guide the disbelieving people" (Al-Baqarah 264). Here, disbelief (kufr) is mentioned as the groundwork for straying.

Again, in another verse, we read: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ كاذِبٌ كَفَّارٌ: "Indeed, Allah does not guide him who is a liar and [confirmed] disbeliever" (Az-Zumar 3). Here too, lying and ingratitude are considered the prelude to misguidance.

And in another place, it says: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ: "Indeed, Allah does not guide one who is a transgressor and a liar" (Ghafir 28). That is, extravagance and lying are factors of misguidance.

Of course, what we have brought here is only a portion of the Quranic verses in this field; some of these verses with these same concepts appear repeatedly in different Surahs. The result is that the Quran considers Divine misguidance to be specific to those who possess these attributes: "disbelief," "injustice," "disobedience," "lying," "extravagance," and "ingratitude." Are those who possess these attributes not deserving of misguidance and straying?! In other words, does darkness and a veil not cover the heart of one who commits these things?! To put it even more clearly, these actions and traits have effects that inevitably affect the human, casting a veil over his eyes, ears, and intellect, and leading him to misguidance. Since the property of all things and the influence of all causes is by the command of Allah, one can attribute misguidance in all these cases to Allah, but this attribution is the very essence of the servants' choice and their free will.

This was regarding the issue of misguidance; as for "guidance," conditions and attributes are also stated in the Quran which show that it too is not without cause or contrary to Divine wisdom. Some of the attributes that bring merit for guidance and attract Divine grace are mentioned in the following verses:

In one place, we read: يَهْدِي بِهِ اللَّهُ مَنِ اتَّبَعَ رِضْوانَهُ سُبُلَ السَّلامِ وَ يُخرِجُهُمْ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِهِ وَ يَهْدِيهِمْ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ: "By which Allah guides those who pursue His pleasure to the ways of peace and brings them out from darknesses into the light by His permission and guides them to a straight path" (Al-Ma'idah 16). Here, following Allah's command and seeking His pleasure are considered the groundwork for Divine guidance.

In another place, we read: إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ أَنابَ: "Indeed, Allah leaves to stray whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns back [to Him]" (Ar-Ra'd 27). Here too, "repentance and turning back" (inabah) are considered factors for deserving guidance.

In another verse, He says: وَ الَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا: "And those who strive for Us—We will surely guide them to Our ways" (Al-Ankabut 69). Here, "striving" (jihad), and specifically "sincere striving in the way of Allah," is mentioned as the primary condition for guidance.

And finally, in another verse, we read: وَ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً: "And those who are guided, He increases them in guidance" (Surah Muhammad ص 17). Here, traversing a portion of the path of guidance is mentioned as a condition for the continuation of this path by the grace of Allah.

The result is that until there is repentance and turning back from the servants, until they follow His command, until striving and effort take place, and until they take the first steps in the path of truth, Divine grace does not include them, does not take their hands, and does not deliver them to the goal. Is the inclusion of guidance for those who possess these attributes without calculation, or is it evidence of guidance being fatalistic? You see that the verses of the Quran in this field are very clear and expressive; however, those who could not or did not want to make a correct synthesis of the verses of guidance and misguidance fell into such a dangerous mistake, and "because they did not see the truth, they took the path of myth." It must be said that they themselves provided the groundwork for this "misguidance"! In any case, the Divine Will (mashiyyah) emphasized in the verses of guidance and misguidance never means a will without reason or void of wisdom; rather, in every case, it has specific conditions that make it harmonious with His being Wise.

Relying on Allah's Grace

The human is like a straw before the gale of events, thrown in a different direction at every moment. This straw might attach itself to a leaf or a broken branch, but the gale takes both with it; even if it clings to a tree, sometimes the storm uproots the tree itself. But if it joins a massive mountain, no storm can move it from its place. This mountain is faith in Allah, and the rest is reliance on other than Him. For this reason, in the above verses, it says: أَ لَيْسَ اللَّهُ بِكافٍ عَبْدَهُ: "Is Allah not sufficient for His servant?" Attention to and faith in the content of this verse grants the human extraordinary courage and self-confidence, making his heart calm and assured so that he resists hard events like a mountain, does not fear the multitude of enemies, is not terrified by the fewness of companions, and severe crises do not disturb the peace of his soul. As it has come in the Hadith: Al-mu'minu kal-jabali al-rasikh, la tuharrikuhu al-'awasif "The believer is like a firm and rooted mountain; storms do not move him."

Ayahs

وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلْ أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ إِنْ أَرَادَنِىَ ٱللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ هَلْ هُنَّ كَـٰشِفَـٰتُ ضُرِّهِۦٓ أَوْ أَرَادَنِى بِرَحْمَةٍ هَلْ هُنَّ مُمْسِكَـٰتُ رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ قُلْ حَسْبِىَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ عَلَيْهِ يَتَوَكَّلُ ٱلْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ (٣٨)
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘Allah.’ Say, ‘Have you considered what you invoke besides Allah? Should Allah desire some distress for me, can they remove the distress visited by Him? Or should He desire some mercy for me, can they withhold His mercy?’ Say, ‘Allah is sufficient for me. In Him let all the trusting put their trust.’ (38)
قُلْ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَكَانَتِكُمْ إِنِّى عَـٰمِلٌ ۖ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
Say, ‘O my people! Act according to your ability. I too am acting. Soon you will know (39)
مَن يَأْتِيهِ عَذَابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَيَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذَابٌ مُّقِيمٌ (٤٠)
who will be overtaken by a punishment that will disgrace him, and on whom a lasting punishment will descend.’ (40)

Commentary

Do Your Deities Have the Power to Solve Any Problem?

Since the preceding verses spoke of the deviant beliefs of the polytheists and their sinister consequences, the verses under discussion speak of the evidences of Monotheism (Tawhid) to complete the previous discussion by providing proof. Furthermore, the previous verses mentioned that Allah’s support alone is sufficient; this matter is also pursued with supporting evidence in the verses under discussion.

First, He says: "And if you ask them, 'Who created the heavens and the earth?' they will surely say, 'Allah'" (وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ‌).

This is because no conscience or intellect accepts that this vast and expansive universe, with all its grandeur, is the creation of a terrestrial being, let alone lifeless idols devoid of reason and perception. In this way, the Quran brings them before the judgment of intellect, conscience, and primordial nature (fitrah), so that the first foundation of Monotheism—which is the issue of the Creator of the heavens and the earth—is solidified in their hearts.

In the next stage, it addresses the issue of profit and loss and the influence over human interests and harms, to prove that idols have no role in this regard. It adds:

"Say, 'Have you then considered those you invoke besides Allah? If Allah intended me some harm, could they remove His harm? Or if He intended me some mercy, could they withhold His mercy?'" (قُلْ أَ فَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ إِنْ أَرادَنِيَ اللَّهُ بِضُرٍّ هَلْ هُنَّ كاشِفاتُ ضُرِّهِ أَوْ أَرادَنِي بِرَحْمَةٍ هَلْ هُنَّ مُمْسِكاتُ رَحْمَتِهِ‌) [1]

[1] Commentators and lexicographers usually interpret the phrase "أفرأيتم" (Have you seen/considered) as "خبرونى" (Tell me), while there is no obstacle to interpreting "vision" here in its original sense, meaning seeing with the eye or the heart. Therefore, it can be translated as "Have you observed" or "Have you realized."

Now that neither "creatorship" belongs to them nor do they have "power over profit or loss," what is the meaning of worshipping them? Why abandon the Origin of the creation of the world and the Owner of every profit and loss, and cling to these useless and senseless beings? Even if the deities possessed consciousness—such as the Jinn and angels who were worshipped by a group of polytheists—they are still neither creators nor the owners of profit and loss.

It is here that, as a general and final conclusion, He says: "Say: 'Allah is sufficient for me; in Him let those who trust put their trust'" (قُلْ حَسْبِيَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ يَتَوَكَّلُ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ‌).

The fact that the polytheists considered the creation of the heavens and the earth to be exclusive to Allah is mentioned repeatedly in the verses of the Quran [1]. This indicates that the matter was completely certain to them, and this itself is the best evidence for the invalidity of polytheism (shirk); for "Monotheism in Creatorship, Ownership, and Lordship" of the universe is the best proof for "Monotheism in Worship," and its result is trusting in the Pure Essence of Allah and turning one's eyes away from others.

And if we see that in the encounter of Ibrahim عليه السلام, the breaker of idols, with the tyrant Nimrod, the latter claims lordship over the universe and considers the life and death of the people to be in his own hands, and then becomes bewildered and silenced by Ibrahim's عليه السلام proposal that "if you are truthful, bring the sun from the west," this type of belief is rare among idolaters. It could only arise from a weak, arrogant, and senseless brain like that of Nimrod.

It is noteworthy that the pronouns referring to their false deities in this verse, as well as the plural forms, are all in the feminine plural (هُنَّ - كاشِفاتُ - مُمْسِكاتُ).

[1] Al-Ankabut 61 and 63, Luqman 31, Az-Zukhruf 9 and 87.

This is because: "Firstly," the famous great idols of the Arabs had feminine names (Lat, Manat, and Uzza). "Secondly," since they believed in the weakness and inability of the female gender, Allah intends to personify the inability of the idols according to their own beliefs. "Thirdly," because there were many lifeless objects among the idols, and the feminine plural form is sometimes used for inanimate objects; hence it was used in the verse under discussion.

It is also noteworthy that the phrase "عَلَيْهِ يَتَوَكَّلُ الْمُتَوَكِّلُونَ‌" conveys the meaning of exclusivity (hasr) because "عَلَيْهِ" (in Him) precedes the verb, meaning those who trust rely "only" on Him.

In the next verse, He addresses those who do not submit to the logic of intellect and conscience with an effective divine threat, saying: "Say, 'O my people! Act according to your position; indeed, I am acting [according to mine]. And soon you will know'" (قُلْ يا قَوْمِ اعْمَلُوا عَلى‌ مَكانَتِكُمْ إِنِّي عامِلٌ فَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ‌) [1]

[1] Regarding what root "مكانة" (position/status) is derived from and what it means, most commentators and lexicographers have considered it to mean place, position, and status, derived from the root "كون" (to be). However, they state that because the word "مكان" (place) has been used in this form so much, it was imagined that the "m" is original, and thus its broken plural is given as "امكنه". However, in Lisan al-Arab, the possibility is also given that it is from the root "مكنة" and "تمكن", meaning ability and power. In any case, in the first scenario, the concept of the verse becomes "stay in your position," and in the second scenario, the meaning becomes "do whatever is in your power."

"You will know to whom will come a punishment that will disgrace him [in this world], and upon whom will descend an everlasting punishment [in the Hereafter]" (مَنْ يَأْتِيهِ عَذابٌ يُخْزِيهِ وَ يَحِلُّ عَلَيْهِ عَذابٌ مُقِيمٌ‌).

In this way, He speaks the final word to them: either submit to the logic of intellect and reason and listen to the call of conscience, or wait for two painful punishments: a punishment in this world that causes humiliation and disgrace, and a punishment in the Hereafter that is eternal and everlasting. These are the very punishments you have prepared with your own hands, and the fire for which you yourselves have gathered and lit the fuel.

Commentary

Allah Takes the Souls at the Time of Death and Sleep

After mentioning the proofs of Monotheism and describing the fate of the polytheists and the monotheists, the first verse under discussion explains the reality that the benefit or harm of your acceptance or rejection of faith returns to yourselves. If the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) insists on this matter, it is not for any benefit that might reach him, but merely to fulfill a Divine duty. It says:

"Indeed, We have sent down to you the Book for the people in truth" (إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ لِلنَّاسِ بِالْحَقِ‌) ^1. "So whoever is guided, it is for [the benefit of] his own soul; and whoever goes astray, he only goes astray against it" (فَمَنِ اهْتَدى‌ فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَ مَنْ ضَلَّ فَإِنَّما يَضِلُّ عَلَيْها).

And in any case, "you are not a disposer of affairs over them" to forcibly instill the truth into their hearts; your duty is only to convey the message and to warn (وَ ما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوكِيلٍ‌).

This statement—that whoever takes the path of truth benefits himself, and whoever treads the path of error harms himself—is repeated frequently in the verses of the Quran. It is an emphasis on the reality that neither does Allah need the faith of His servants nor does He fear their disbelief, nor does His Prophet. He did not design this program to gain a profit, "but rather to bestow His generosity upon His servants."

The expression "وَ ما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوَكِيلٍ‌" (considering that wakil here means one who is responsible for making the misguided believe) is repeated several times in the Quran with this exact phrasing or similar to it. it expresses the reality that the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is not responsible for the people's faith. Fundamentally, faith cannot be achieved through compulsion, and he is only duty-bound not to neglect or falter for a single moment in conveying the Divine command to the people, whether they accept it or turn away.

Then, to clarify that everything regarding human beings, including their life and death, is in the hands of Allah, it says: "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death" (اللَّهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها) ^1 "and those that do not die [He takes] during their sleep" (وَ الَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنامِها) [^2]. Thus, "sleep" is the brother of "death" and a weak form of it,

[^2]: "Manam" has an infinitive meaning and refers to "nawm" (sleep).

because the connection between the soul and the body reaches its minimum during sleep, and many of the bonds between the two are severed.

Then it adds: "He keeps those for which He has decreed death" (such that they never wake up from sleep) "and releases the others for a specified term" to return to their bodies to continue their lives (فَيُمْسِكُ الَّتِي قَضى‌ عَلَيْهَا الْمَوْتَ وَ يُرْسِلُ الْأُخْرى‌ إِلى‌ أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى‌).

Indeed, "in this are signs for a people who give thought" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ‌).

From this verse, the following points are clearly derived:

  1. Man is a combination of soul and body; the soul is a non-material essence whose connection with the body is the source of its light and life.
  2. At the time of death, Allah severs this connection and takes the soul to the world of spirits. During sleep, He also takes this soul, but not in a way that the connection is entirely severed. Therefore, the soul has three states relative to the body: full connection (the state of life and wakefulness), partial connection (the state of sleep), and complete severance of connection (the state of death).
  3. "Sleep" is a weak version of "death," and "death" is a complete example of "sleep"!
  4. Sleep is among the proofs of the independence and originality of the soul, especially when accompanied by "visions" (ru'ya), particularly true dreams; this meaning becomes clearer.
  5. When the connection of some souls with the body becomes weak during sleep, it sometimes leads to the complete severance of this connection so that their owners never wake up. Other souls, however, fluctuate between sleep and wakefulness until the Divine command arrives.
  6. Paying attention to the reality that every night during "sleep" a person stands on the threshold of death is a lesson of admonition; if one reflects upon it, it is sufficient for his "awakening."
  7. All these matters take place by the power of Allah. If other verses speak of the seizing of the soul by the "Angel of Death" and the angels of death, it is because they are the subordinates of the Truth and the executors of His commands; there is no contradiction between the two.

In any case, the fact that the end of the verse says: "In this are signs for a people who give thought" refers to signs of Allah's power, the issue of the Origin and the Resurrection, and the weakness and helplessness of man before His will.

Since the previous verse established the sovereignty of "Allah" over the existence of man and His governance through the system of death, life, sleep, and wakefulness, the next verse mentions the deviation of the polytheists regarding the issue of intercession (shafa'ah) to prove to them that the Owner of intercession is the same Owner of man's death and life, not the mindless idols. It says: "Or have they taken other than Allah as intercessors?" (أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ شُفَعاءَ) ^1. We know that one of the famous excuses of the idolaters for worshipping idols was their claim: "We worship them only so that they may be our intercessors with Allah," as we read in the beginning of this very Surah: "We do not worship them except that they may bring us closer to Allah in station" (Az-Zumar, 3). This was either because they considered the idols to be images and manifestations of angels and holy spirits, or they believed in a mysterious power for these lifeless stones and wood.

In any case, since intercession is, firstly, dependent on perception, understanding, and intellect, and secondly, dependent on power, ownership, and sovereignty, the continuation of the verse responds to them as follows:

"Say: Even if they do not possess anything, nor do they understand?" (قُلْ أَ وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يَمْلِكُونَ شَيْئاً وَ لا يَعْقِلُونَ‌) ^1. If you consider your intercessors to be angels and holy spirits, they possess nothing of their own; whatever they have is from Allah. And if you seek intercession from stone and wooden idols, they, in addition to lacking ownership, do not possess the slightest intellect or perception. Abandon these excuses and turn toward the One to whom belongs the ownership and sovereignty of the entire universe, and to whom all lines lead.

Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "Say: To Allah belongs intercession entirely" (قُلْ لِلَّهِ الشَّفاعَةُ جَمِيعاً). "For to Him belongs the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth, then to Him you will be returned" (لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ‌).

In this way, He completely disarms them, because the Monotheism that governs the entire world dictates that intercession is also impossible except by the permission of the Lord: "Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission?" (Al-Baqarah, 255).

Or, as some commentators have said, the reality of intercession is fundamentally the same as seeking recourse (tawassul) to the Beautiful Names of Allah—recourse to His Mercy, His Forgiveness, and His Concealment of faults. Therefore, every kind of intercession ultimately returns to His Pure Essence. That being the case, how can one seek intercession from other than Him without His permission? ^1 Regarding the connection of the phrase "then to Him you will be returned" (ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ‌) to what preceded it, various explanations have been offered by commentators:

  1. This phrase indicates that not only is intercession in this world in the hands of Allah—and one should not, like the polytheists, seek the solution to problems and the removal of calamities from other than Allah—but in the Hereafter as well, intercession and salvation belong to Him and are from Him.
  2. This phrase is a second proof for the exclusivity of intercession to Allah, because the first proof relied on Allah's "ownership," and here it relies on "the return of all things to Him."
  3. This phrase is a threat to the polytheists, telling them: You shall return to Allah and you will see the result of your sinister and ugly thoughts and deeds.

All these interpretations are appropriate, though the first and second interpretations seem more correct.

^1: "Am" here is munqati'ah (disjunctive) and means "bal" (rather). If it were muttasilah (conjunctive), a counterpart would have to be assumed, which is contrary to the apparent meaning.

^1: The sentence "أَ وَ لَوْ كانُوا لا يَمْلِكُونَ شَيْئاً" has something implied, and its meaning is: "Will they intercede for you even if they do not possess anything?"

^1: Al-Mizan, Vol. 17, p. 286.

Key Points

1. The Mysterious World of Sleep

What is the reality of sleep? And what happens that causes a person to fall asleep? Scientists have many discussions on this matter:

Some consider it the result of the transfer of a major part of the blood from the brain to other parts of the body, thus attributing a "physical factor" to it.

Others believe that excessive physical activity causes the accumulation of specific toxic substances in the body, and this very matter affects the nervous system, causing the state of sleep. This state continues until these toxins are decomposed and absorbed by the body; thus, they have attributed a "chemical factor" to it.

Another group attributes a type of "nervous factor" to sleep, saying: The specific active nervous system inside the human brain, which is the source of the continuous movements of the limbs, stops working and shuts down due to extreme fatigue.

However, none of these theories has been able to provide a convincing answer to the issue of sleep, although the influence of these factors cannot be denied in general.

We believe that what has caused modern scientists to remain unable to provide a clear explanation for the issue of sleep is their materialistic thinking. They want to explain this issue without accepting the originality and independence of the soul, whereas sleep, before being a physical phenomenon, is a spiritual phenomenon that is impossible to explain without a correct understanding of the soul.

The Holy Quran, in the above verses, has expressed the most precise explanation for the issue of sleep, for it says sleep is a type of "seizing of the soul" and a separation of the soul from the body, but not a complete separation.

Thus, when by the command of Allah the radiance of the soul is withdrawn from the body and only a faint ray of it shines upon this body, the apparatus of perception and intellect stops working, and man is deprived of sensation and movement, although some of the activities necessary for the continuation of his life—such as the heartbeat, blood circulation, and the activity of the respiratory and digestive systems—continue.

In a tradition from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), we read:

"There is no one who sleeps except that his nafs (soul) ascends to the heaven and his ruh (spirit) remains in his body, and between the two there is a connection like the ray of the sun. Whenever Allah issues the command to seize the souls, the ruh answers the nafs and flies toward it. And when Allah permits the return of the soul, the nafs answers the ruh and returns to the body. This is the meaning of the words of Allah, the Sublime: 'Allah takes the souls at the time of their death...'" ^1 [^2]

Incidentally, from here another important issue—the issue of "visions" (dreaming)—is also solved, for there are many dreams that occur in reality exactly or with a slight change.

Materialistic interpretations are unable to explain or justify such dreams, whereas spiritual interpretations can clearly illuminate this matter. For the human soul, during its separation from the body and its connection with the world of spirits, perceives more realities related to the past and the future, and this is what forms the basis of true dreams.

^1: Majma' al-Bayan under the verse discussed, and Tafsir al-Safi.

[^2]: Note that "ruh" in this narration refers to the animal spirit and the functioning of the body's main systems, and "nafs" refers to the human soul.

(For more explanation, please refer to Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 312, under verse 4 of Surah Yusuf, where this matter is discussed in detail).

2. "Sleep" in Islamic Traditions

From the traditions that commentators have mentioned under the above verses, it is also clearly evident that sleep in Islam is considered a movement of the soul toward the world of spirits, and wakefulness is the return of the soul to the body and a type of renewed life.

In a tradition from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), we read that he taught his companions:

"A Muslim should not sleep while in a state of janabah (ritual impurity); he should not sleep except in a state of purity (taharah). If he does not find water, let him perform tayammum with clean earth. For the soul of the believer is raised to Allah the Exalted; He accepts it and blesses it. If its appointed time has arrived, He places it in the treasures of His mercy, and if its time has not arrived, He sends it back with His trusted ones among His angels, and they return it to its body." ^1

In another tradition from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام), we read:

"When you rise at night from your sleep, say: 'Praise be to Allah who returned my soul to me so that I may praise Him and worship Him.'" [^2]

And traditions in this regard are many.

^1: Al-Khisal by al-Saduq (as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 488).

[^2]: Usul al-Kafi (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 488).

Commentary

Those Who Dread the Name of Allah!

Once again, these verses speak of Monotheism (Tawhid) and## Commentary

Those Who Dread the Name of Allah!

Once again, these verses speak of Monotheism (Tawhid) and polytheism (Shirk). In the first verse under discussion, one of the most hideous and repulsive traits of the polytheists and deniers of the Hereafter is reflected in their encounter with Monotheism; He says: "When Allah alone is mentioned, the hearts of those who do not believe in the Hereafter shrink with aversion, but when those [deities] other than Him are mentioned, they are filled with joy!" (وَ إِذا ذُكِرَ اللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ اشْمَأَزَّتْ قُلُوبُ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ وَ إِذا ذُكِرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِذا هُمْ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ) [^1]. Sometimes, a person becomes so accustomed to ugliness and so alienated from purity and goodness that they become distressed by hearing the name of Truth and delighted by hearing falsehood. They do not bow their head in submission before Allah, the Creator of the universe, yet they kneel and show reverence before a piece of stone or wood they have fashioned themselves, or before humans and creatures like themselves.

[^1]: "Ishma’azzat" (اشمأزت) is derived from the root "Ishmi’zaz," meaning contraction and aversion toward something. "Wahdahu" (وحده) is in the accusative case as a circumstantial adverb (Hal) or an absolute object (Maf'ul Mutlaq).

A similar meaning appears in Surah Al-Isra, verse 46: "وَ إِذا ذَكَرْتَ رَبَّكَ فِي الْقُرْآنِ وَحْدَهُ وَلَّوْا عَلى‌ أَدْبارِهِمْ نُفُوراً" (And when you mention your Lord alone in the Quran, they turn their backs in aversion). The great Prophet of God, Nuh (peace be upon him), complains to Allah about such crooked-minded people, saying: "وَ إِنِّي كُلَّما دَعَوْتُهُمْ لِتَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ جَعَلُوا أَصابِعَهُمْ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَ استَغْشَوْا ثِيابَهُمْ وَ أَصَرُّوا وَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا اسْتِكْباراً" (And indeed, every time I invited them that You might forgive them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, persisted, and were arrogant with [great] arrogance) (Nuh: 7).

Indeed, such is the state of stubborn bigots and conceited ignoramuses.

Furthermore, it is clearly understood from this verse that the source of this group's misery lies in two things: the denial of the principle of Monotheism and the lack of faith in the Hereafter.

In contrast to them are the believers, who, upon hearing the name of the One God, become so captivated by His holy name that they are willing to sacrifice everything they possess in His path. The name of the Beloved sweetens their palate, perfumes the fragrance of their soul, and illuminates their entire heart. Not only His name, but the name of anything associated or connected with Him brings them joy.

One should not imagine that this trait was exclusive to the polytheists of the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) era. In every age and time, there are dark-hearted deviants who rejoice at hearing the names of the enemies of God and atheistic ideologies or the victories of oppressors, while the names of the virtuous and pure, and their programs and victories, are painful to them. Therefore, in some narrations, this verse has been interpreted as referring to those who are distressed by hearing the virtues of the Ahl al-Bayt of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) or by following their school of thought [^1].

When the discourse reaches the point where this stubborn group and these conceited ignoramuses are even repulsed by hearing the name of the One God, He commands His Prophet to turn away from them and turn toward the threshold of Allah. He is to speak to Him in a tone reflecting deep faith and overflowing with love, bringing the complaint against this group to His presence, both to soothe his own heart—which is filled with sorrow—and to give a jolt to those heedless, sleeping souls. He says: "Say: O Allah! Creator of the heavens and the earth, Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, You will judge between Your servants concerning that over which they used to differ" (قُلِ اللَّهُمَّ فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ عالِمَ الْغَيْبِ وَ الشَّهادَةِ أَنْتَ تَحْكُمُ بَيْنَ عِبادِكَ فِي ما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ) [^2]. Yes, on the Day of Resurrection, which is the day when all differences are settled and the day when hidden realities are revealed, You are the absolute Judge and Sovereign, for You are both the Creator of all things and aware of their secrets. It is there that, by Your judgment, disputes will end, and these stubborn, misguided ones will realize their mistake. It is then that they will think of making amends, but to what avail?!

As the next verse states: "And if those who did wrong possessed all that is on earth and the like of it with it, they would [seek to] ransom themselves with it from the worst of the punishment on the Day of Resurrection (but such a thing is not possible)."

[^1]: "Usul al-Kafi" and "Rawdah al-Kafi" (according to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 490).

[^2]: "Fatira al-Samawat" (فاطِرَ السَّماواتِ) is in the accusative case as a vocative in a possessive construction (Munada Mudaf).

(وَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً وَ مِثْلَهُ مَعَهُ لَافْتَدَوْا بِهِ مِنْ سُوءِ الْعَذابِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ).

"Wrongdoing" (Zulm) here has a broad meaning that encompasses both polytheism and other forms of injustice.

Then He adds: "And there will appear to them from Allah that which they had never expected" (وَ بَدا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ ما لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ).

They will see with their own eyes punishments that never even crossed their minds! Moreover, they were only deluded by the grace of Allah while being heedless of His wrath, anger, and overwhelming power.

Occasionally, they performed deeds they considered to be good, while they were sometimes among the major sins. In any case, matters will appear to them in these regards that they never believed possible.

This is the exact opposite of the promise of goodness given to the believers, where He said: "فَلا تَعْلَمُ نَفْسٌ ما أُخْفِيَ لَهُمْ مِنْ قُرَّةِ أَعْيُنٍ" (No soul knows what has been hidden for them of comfort for the eyes) (As-Sajdah: 17).

It is narrated that one of the Muslims was in great distress and agitation on the verge of death. When asked the reason, he said: "I thought of this verse where Allah says:

'وَ بَدا لَهُمْ مِنَ اللَّهِ ما لَمْ يَكُونُوا يَحْتَسِبُونَ' (And there will appear to them from Allah that which they had never expected). Terror has gripped my being, and I fear lest matters appear to me from Allah that I never imagined" [^1].

The next verse is an explanation or completion of what was mentioned in the previous verse; it says: "On that day, the evils of what they earned will appear to them" (وَ بَدا لَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا).

"And that which they used to mock will envelop them" (وَ حاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ).

In reality, four subjects regarding the polytheists and wrongdoers are expressed in these verses:

First, the horror and terror of the divine punishment on that day is so great that if they possessed twice all the wealth and property on earth, they would give it all to be saved from the punishment, but no such transaction will take place there.

Second, types of divine punishment that never crossed their minds will appear before them.

Third, the evils of their deeds will be present before them and will be personified.

Fourth, what they considered a joke regarding the Hereafter, they will see as an objective reality, and all doors of salvation will be closed to them.

Given that it says "the evils of their deeds will appear," the above verse serves as another proof for the issue of the "personification of deeds" (Tajassum al-A'mal), as there is no need to assume the word "punishment" or "retribution" is implied.

[^1]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan" and "Tafsir al-Qurtubi" under the verse in question.

Ayahs

فَإِذَا مَسَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ ضُرٌّ دَعَانَا ثُمَّ إِذَا خَوَّلْنَـٰهُ نِعْمَةً مِّنَّا قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أُوتِيتُهُۥ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍۭ ۚ بَلْ هِىَ فِتْنَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٩)
When distress befalls man, he supplicates Us. Then, when We grant him a blessing from Us, he says, ‘I was given it by virtue of [my] knowledge.’ Indeed, it is a test, but most of them do not know. (49)
قَدْ قَالَهَا ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَمَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ (٥٠)
Those who were before them [also] said that, but what they used to earn did not avail them. (50)
فَأَصَابَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ مِنْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ سَيُصِيبُهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا كَسَبُوا۟ وَمَا هُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ (٥١)
So the evils of what they had earned visited them, and as for the wrongdoers among these, the evils of what they earn shall be visited on them and they will not frustrate [Allah]. (51)
أَوَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٥٢)
Do they not know that Allah expands the provision for whomever He wishes and tightens it [for whomever He wishes]? There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (52)

Commentary

In Hardships They Remember Allah, But...

Here again, the subject of discussion is the faithless and the oppressors, reflecting another of their unsightly characteristics.

First, It states: "When adversity (and pain, suffering, or poverty) touches man, he calls upon Us" to solve his problem (فَإِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ ضُرٌّ دَعانا).

The very same human who, according to the previous verses, was disgusted by the mention of the Name of the One God—yes, that same human, when trapped in the straits of events, seeks refuge in the shadow of Divine Grace.

But even that is temporary: "Then when We bestow a favor upon him from Us" and remove his pain and suffering, he consigns Our grace and gift to oblivion and says: "I have obtained this [wealth/blessing] only through my own efforts, and it was due to my own merit and skill" (ثُمَّ إِذا خَوَّلْناهُ نِعْمَةً مِنَّا قالَ إِنَّما أُوتِیتُهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ) ^1. An example of this speech is what the Quran narrates in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 78, from the tongue of Qarun (Korah).

The Quran narrates that in response to the scholars of the Children of Israel who advised him: "Use these God-given blessings in the way of His pleasure," he said: "إِنَّما أُوتِیتُهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ عِنْدِي" ("I was only given it because of knowledge I have")! These heedless individuals do not think for a moment that even that knowledge and skill are gifts from Allah. Did they give themselves this knowledge and skill that allows them to manage their livelihood and earn vast incomes? Or was it part of their essence from eternity?!

Some commentators have suggested another possibility in interpreting this phrase: that they say Allah gave us these favors because He was aware of our merit and worthiness.

While this possibility is plausible in the verse under discussion, it is not possible in the verse of Surah Al-Qasas regarding Qarun, given the word "عِنْدِي" ("with me/in my possession"). This can serve as a contextual clue for the verse under discussion and a reason to prefer the first interpretation.

Then, the Quran, in response to these self-centered and shallow individuals who quickly lose themselves when they reach a blessing, says: "Rather, it is a trial, but most of them do not know" (بَلْ هِيَ فِتْنَةٌ وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ).

The goal is that through the occurrence of hardships, followed by the attainment of great blessings, what is hidden within them becomes manifest.

Do they become despondent at the time of calamity and arrogant at the time of blessing?

In these transitions, do they think of Allah more, or do they become drowned in the world?

Do they forget their own selves, or, by paying attention to their weaknesses, will they remember Allah more than before?

But alas, most people are forgetful and are not aware of these truths.

This reality has been repeated many times in the verses of the Quran: that the All-Wise Allah sometimes places man in the straits of difficulties and sometimes in prosperity, ease, and blessing, to test him through these means, to elevate his existential value, and to acquaint him with the truth that everything is from Him.

Fundamentally, hardships provide the ground for the blossoming of the primordial nature (Fitrah), just as blessings are a prelude to recognition (Ma'rifah). (In this regard, we have provided another discussion in Volume 16, page 343, under verse 65 of Surah Al-Ankabut).

It is noteworthy that in this verse, emphasis is placed on the word "Man" (al-Insan), identifying him as forgetful and arrogant. This refers to humans who have not been placed under the cultivation of Divine schools and have had no mentor or guide; their desires were free, they were submerged in whims, and they grew like wild weeds. Yes, it is they who come toward Allah whenever they are afflicted by pain and suffering, and when the storm of events subsides and they are granted blessings, they consign Allah to oblivion. (For more explanation on this subject, please read under the title "Man in the Holy Quran" in Volume 8, page 239, under verse 12 of Surah Yunus).

In the next verse, it adds: "Those before them said it [as well]" (they also claimed that our blessings are the product of our knowledge and merit), "but what they used to earn did not avail them" (قَدْ قالَهَا الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ) ^1. Yes, the arrogant Qaruns imagined their wealth was the result of their own merit and had forgotten the Divine favors upon them. They became heedless of the Original Source of the blessing and fixed their eyes only on the apparent means. But history shows that when Allah caused the earth to swallow them and their treasures, there was no one to rise to their aid, and their wealth was of the least benefit to them, as the Quran says: "فَخَسَفْنا بِهِ وَ بِدارِهِ الْأَرْضَ فَما كانَ لَهُ مِنْ فِئَةٍ يَنْصُرُونَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ" ("So We caused the earth to swallow him and his home. And there was for him no company to aid him other than Allah") (Al-Qasas: 81). Not only Qarun, but peoples such as 'Ad, Thamud, the people of Saba, and their like, were afflicted by this same fate.
