^1: The pronoun in "قَدْ قالَهَا" refers to the "word" or "statement" inferred from the previous sentence, meaning the phrase "إِنَّما أُوتِیتُهُ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ".

Then He says: "So the evil consequences of what they earned struck them" (فَأَصَابَهُمْ سَيِّئَاتُ مَا كَسَبُوا). And each was afflicted by a type of Divine punishment—storms, floods, earthquakes, and heavenly blasts—and they perished.

And He adds that this fate was not exclusive to them: "And those who have wronged among these [people of Makkah] will also be struck by the evil consequences of what they earned, and they cannot escape [Allah's punishment]" (وَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْ هؤُلاءِ سَيُصِيبُهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا وَ ما هُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ).

Rather, it goes beyond them and includes all arrogant oppressors who are heedless of Allah in all ages and centuries.

Regarding whether the phrase "سَيُصِيبُهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما كَسَبُوا" refers to worldly or otherworldly punishment, two possibilities have been given. However, by the context of "فَأَصَابَهُمْ سَيِّئَاتُ مَا كَسَبُوا" (their predecessors were struck by the evil of their deeds), the first interpretation seems more appropriate.

In response to the statement that "our blessings are the product of our own awareness and ability," the Quran reminded them to take a look at the history of those who came before and see that others said the same thing, and what calamities and punishments they suffered—this is a historical answer.

Then, in the next verse, it proceeds to a rational answer, saying: "Do they not know that Allah extends provision for whom He wills or restricts it?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَقْدِرُ).

How many worthy individuals are there who are deprived and marginalized in life, and how many weak and incapable individuals are there who are blessed in every respect? If material victories were all achieved in the shadow of the efforts and merits of the individuals themselves, we should not witness such scenes.

This itself shows that behind the world of causes, there is another powerful Hand that manages it according to a calculated plan.

It is true that man must strive and effort in life; it is true that struggle and effort are the keys to solving many problems. But it is a great mistake for us to forget the "Causer of Causes" (Musabbib al-Asbab), fix our eyes only on the means, and consider ourselves the real effectors.

One of the secrets behind the failure of a group of capable, knowledgeable people and the success of a group of incompetent, ignorant people is to serve as a warning to all people so they do not get lost in the world of causes and do not rely solely on their personal power.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 19, p. 496

At the end of the verse, it adds: "Indeed in that are signs for a people who believe" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ).

Signs for the Pure Essence of Allah, just as the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said:

"عرفت اللَّه بفسخ العزائم و حل العقود و نقض الهمم"

"I recognized Allah through the breaking of determinations, the undoing of knots (contracts), and the shattering of wills." ^1 And there are signs of the weakness and inability of man, so that he does not lose himself and become afflicted with pride and self-centeredness.

^1: Nahj al-Balagha, Short Sayings, Saying 250.

Ayahs

۞ قُلْ يَـٰعِبَادِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَا تَقْنَطُوا۟ مِن رَّحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥٣)
Say [that Allah declares,] ‘O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah will forgive all sins. Indeed, He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. (53)
وَأَنِيبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا۟ لَهُۥ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ (٥٤)
Turn penitently to Him and submit to Him before the punishment overtakes you, whereupon you will not be helped. (54)
وَٱتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَحْسَنَ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ (٥٥)
Follow the best of what has been sent down to you from your Lord, before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware.’ (55)

Commentary

Allah Forgives All Sins

Following the repeated threats mentioned in the previous verses regarding the polytheists and the oppressors, these verses open the way of return, coupled with hope, for all sinners. This is because the primary goal of all these matters is education and guidance, not vengeance or violence. With a tone filled with ultimate grace and affection, He opens His arms of mercy to everyone and issues the decree of their pardon, saying: "Say to them: O My servants who have transgressed against yourselves! Despair not of the mercy of Allah; indeed, Allah forgives all sins," for He is the Forgiving, the Merciful (قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً).

A careful examination of the expressions in this verse shows that it is among the most hope-inspiring verses of the Holy Quran for all sinners. Its inclusiveness and breadth are to such an extent that, according to a narration, Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام said: "In the entire Quran, there is no verse more vast [in mercy] than this verse: (يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا ...)" [^1]. The reason for this is clear because:

  1. The expression "O My servants!" (يا عبادى) is the initiator of grace from the Lord.
  2. The use of the term "transgression" (اسراف) instead of "oppression, sin, or crime" is another act of grace.
  3. The expression "against yourselves" (عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌), which shows that man's sins all return to haunt himself, is another sign of the Lord's affection—just as a compassionate father says to his child, "Do not wrong yourself so much!"
  4. The command "Despair not" (لا تقنطوا), considering that "qunut" originally means despairing of good, is by itself proof that sinners must not lose hope in "Divine Grace."
  5. The phrase "of the mercy of Allah" (مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللَّهِ‌) after the command "Despair not" places further emphasis on this goodness and love.
  6. When we reach the sentence "Indeed, Allah forgives the sins" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ‌), which begins with a particle of emphasis (inna) and where the word "sins" (al-dhunub—plural with the definite article) encompasses all sins without exception, the discourse reaches its peak and the ocean of mercy surges.
  7. When "all together" (جميعا) is added as another emphasis, hope reaches its final stage.
  8. and 9. Describing Allah as "Forgiving" (غفور) and "Merciful" (رحيم), which are two of the most hope-inspiring attributes of the Lord, leaves no room for the slightest despair at the end of the verse.

Indeed, for this reason, the above verse is the most expansive of the Quranic verses, covering every kind of sin, and is considered among the most hope-inspiring verses of the Holy Quran. Truly, nothing else can be expected from One whose ocean of grace is boundless and whose ray of bounty is unlimited. From One whose "mercy precedes His wrath" and who created His servants for mercy, not for anger and punishment, no other expectation exists. What a Merciful and Compassionate God! What a loving and affectionate Lord!!

Here, two issues have occupied the minds of commentators, the solutions to which are incidentally hidden within the verse itself and the subsequent verses.

First: Does the generality of the verse include all sins, even polytheism (shirk) and other major sins? If so, then why in Surah An-Nisa, verse 48, is polytheism excluded from the sins that can be forgiven? "Indeed, Allah does not forgive that partners be associated with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills" (إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَ يَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ).

Second: Is this promise of forgiveness mentioned in the verse under discussion absolute, or is it conditional upon repentance and the like?

Of course, these two questions are related. The answer can be clearly found in the following verses, as three commands are given that clarify everything: 1. "Turn to your Lord" (أَنِيبُوا إِلى‌ رَبِّكُمْ‌) 2. "And submit to Him" (وَ أَسْلِمُوا لَهُ‌) 3. "And follow the best of what was revealed to you from your Lord" (وَ اتَّبِعُوا أَحْسَنَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ‌).

These three commands state that the doors of forgiveness and mercy are open to all servants without exception, provided that after committing a sin, they come to their senses, change their course, turn toward the threshold of God, submit to His command, and demonstrate their sincerity in this repentance and turning (inabah) through action. In this way, neither polytheism is excluded nor anything else; furthermore, the fact that this general pardon and vast mercy are conditional upon certain requirements is undeniable.

And if we see that in Surah An-Nisa, verse 48, the forgiveness of polytheists is excluded, it refers to polytheists who die in a state of polytheism, not those who wake up and take the path of truth—for the vast majority of Muslims at the dawn of Islam were formerly such.

If we consider the state of many criminals who, after committing major sins, become so upset and regretful that they do not believe a way of return is open to them, and they consider themselves so stained that they cannot be washed by any water, they ask: "Are our sins truly forgivable? Is there a way toward God for us? Is there a bridge behind us that has not been destroyed?" We then understand the meaning of the verse well, for they are ready for any kind of repentance but do not consider their sin forgivable, especially if they have repented many times and broken it.

This verse gives them all the glad tidings that the way is open to all of you.

Therefore, "Wahshi," the famous criminal in Islamic history and the killer of "Hamza, the Master of Martyrs," when he wanted to become a Muslim, feared that his repentance would not be accepted because his sin was truly very heavy. A group of commentators say: the above verse was revealed and opened the doors of Divine mercy to Wahshi and the repentant "Wahshis" of the world! Although this Surah is among the Meccan Surahs, and on the day these verses were revealed, neither the Battle of Uhud had occurred nor the story of Hamza's martyrdom and Wahshi's repentance; therefore, this incident cannot be the "occasion of revelation" (shan al-nuzul) for the verse. Rather, it is of the nature of applying a general law to one of its instances. However, in any case, it can clarify the breadth of the verse's meaning.

From what we have said, it becomes clear that the insistence of some commentators like "Alusi" in Ruh al-Ma'ani that the promise of forgiveness in the above verse is not conditional on anything—even mentioning seventeen reasons for it!—is incorrect. This is because it clearly contradicts the subsequent verses, and his seventeen reasons (many of which can be merged) prove nothing more than that God's mercy is vast and expansive, covering all sinners, which does not contradict this Divine promise being conditional by the context of the following verses.

There are other matters regarding this verse which, God willing, will come under the discussion of "Key Points."

In the next verse, He shows the way to enter this boundless ocean of Divine mercy to all criminals and sinners, saying: "Turn to your Lord" and reform the path of your life (وَ أَنِيبُوا إِلى‌ رَبِّكُمْ‌). "And submit to Him," and listen to His command with the ear of the soul and accept it "before the Divine punishment overtakes you, then no one will be able to rise to help you" (وَ أَسْلِمُوا لَهُ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ ثُمَّ لا تُنْصَرُونَ‌).

After traversing these two stages (the stage of "turning" and "submission"), the discourse mentions the third stage, which is the stage of "action," adding: "And follow the best of what was revealed to you from your Lord, before the Divine punishment comes upon you suddenly while you are unaware" (وَ اتَّبِعُوا أَحْسَنَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذابُ بَغْتَةً وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تَشْعُرُونَ‌).

Thus, the path to reaching God's mercy consists of no more than three steps: Step one is repentance and regret for sin and turning toward God. Step two is faith and submission to His command. Step three is righteous action.

After these three steps, entry into the boundless ocean of His mercy, according to the promise He has made, is certain, no matter how heavy the burden of a person's sins may be.

Regarding what is meant by "follow the best of what was revealed to you from your Lord" (اتَّبِعُوا أَحْسَنَ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ), commentators have offered several possibilities. What seems best of all is that the commands revealed by God are varied: some invite to obligations (wajibat), some to recommendations (mustahabbat), and some contain permission for permissible acts (mubahat). The meaning of "the best" is choosing the obligations and recommendations, considering their hierarchy.

Some have also considered it a reference to the Quran among the Heavenly Books, by the evidence of what appears in verse 23 of this same Surah Az-Zumar, which calls the Quran "the best of discourse" (ahsan al-hadith): (اللَّهُ نَزَّلَ أَحْسَنَ الْحَدِيثِ كِتاباً مُتَشابِهاً مَثانِيَ‌). Of course, these two interpretations do not contradict each other.

Key Points

1- The Way of Repentance is Open to All One of the major problems on the path of educational and spiritual development is the feeling of guilt due to past evil deeds, especially when these sins are heavy. This thought constantly manifests before the person: if he wants to change his path toward purity and piety and return to the way of God, how can he rid himself of the heavy responsibility of the past? This thought casts a shadow over his soul like a terrifying nightmare, and often prevents him from changing his life program and gravitating toward purity. It says to him: "What use is repenting?! The chain of your past deeds is like a collar of curse on your hands and feet; you have taken on the color of sin—a fixed and unchangeable color!" Those who deal with educational issues and repentant sinners have well-tested what we have said; they know what a great problem this is.

In Islamic culture, derived from the Holy Quran, this problem is solved. It considers repentance (tawbah) and turning (inabah), whenever accompanied by the proper conditions, as a decisive means for separating from the past and beginning a new life, and even a "second birth." Repeatedly in narrations regarding some sinners, we read: "He is like one born from his mother" (kaman waladat-hu ummuh). In this way, the Quran opens the doors of Divine grace to every human being in any condition and with any kind of burden of responsibility. A vivid example is the above verses, which, with various subtleties of expression, invite criminals and sinners toward God and promise them that they can completely detach themselves from their past life.

In a narration from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The one who repents from sin is like one who has no sin." [^1] The same meaning with an addition is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, who said: "The one who repents from sin is like one who has no sin, and the one who persists in sin while seeking forgiveness for it is like one who mocks." [^2]

But it is obvious that this return toward Divine mercy cannot be unconditional, for He is Wise and does nothing without calculation. If He has opened His arms of mercy to everyone and constantly calls them toward Himself, the existence of readiness in the servants is also necessary. On one hand, they must desire the return with their whole being and undergo an internal revolution and fundamental transformation. On the other hand, after the return, the foundations of their faith and beliefs, which were demolished by the storm of sin, must be reconstructed and renewed. And on the third hand, they must compensate for their spiritual inability and moral weakness through righteous deeds. Of course, the heavier the previous sins were, the more righteous deeds they must perform. This is exactly what the Quran has expressed in the three verses above under the titles of "turning" (inabah), "submission" (islam), and "following the best" (ittiba' al-ahsan).

2- The Heavy-Burdened Some commentators have mentioned "occasions of revelation" for the above verses, all of which are likely of the nature of "application" rather than "original revelation."

Among them is the story of "Wahshi," who committed the greatest crime on the field of Uhud and martyred the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله uncle, Hamza—that brave officer who made his life a shield for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله everywhere—in a cowardly manner. When Islam gained strength and Muslims were victorious everywhere, Wahshi wanted to embrace Islam but feared his Islam would not be accepted. Then the above verse was revealed, and he embraced Islam. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked him: "How did you kill my uncle Hamza?" Wahshi explained the incident. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wept bitterly, accepted his repentance, but said to him: "Hide your face from me, for I cannot bear to look at you." Wahshi then went to Sham and eventually died in the land of Khamr. Some asked if this verse was only about him or included all Muslims; he replied it includes everyone. [^1]

Another is the story of the "Grave-robber" (nabbash—one who dug up graves and took the shrouds of the dead), the summary of which is as follows: "A weeping youth came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, deeply distressed, saying: 'I fear the wrath of God.' He asked: 'Have you associated partners with God?' He said: 'No.' He asked: 'Have you shed innocent blood?' He replied: 'No.' He said: 'Allah forgives your sin, however great it may be.' He replied: 'My sin is greater than the heavens, the earth, the Throne (Arsh), and the Pedestal (Kursi).' He asked: 'Is your sin greater than God?!' He replied: 'No, God is greater than everything.' He said: 'Go (repent), for the Great God forgives the great sin.' Then he said: 'Tell me, what is your sin?' He replied: 'O Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, I am ashamed before your face to recount it.' He said: 'Tell me at last, what have you done?!' He replied: 'For seven years I used to dig up graves and take the shrouds of the dead, until one day while digging a grave, I came across the body of a girl from the Ansar. After I stripped her, the demon of the self was stirred within me...' (then he described the act of violation). When his speech reached this point, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became very upset and distressed, saying: 'Turn this transgressor out!' and turning toward him, added: 'How close you are to the Fire?!' The youth went out weeping bitterly, headed for the wilderness, and cried out: 'O God of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله! If You accept my repentance, inform Your Prophet of it, and if not, send a fire from heaven to burn me and save me from the punishment of the Hereafter.' It was here that the messenger of God's revelation descended upon the Prophet and recited the verse: (قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا ...)." [^1]

The recitation of this verse by Gabriel here might not be the first time (as an original revelation), but rather a repetition of a verse previously revealed to emphasize and draw more attention and to announce the acceptance of that sinful man's repentance. We repeat again that such individuals who carry a heavy burden of sin have a heavier responsibility in the stage of compensation through their righteous deeds.

Fakhr al-Razi has brought another occasion of revelation for the above verses. He narrates that some said: "These verses were revealed regarding the people of Mecca. They used to say: 'Muhammad thinks that whoever worships idols or stains his hands with human blood will never be forgiven, yet he tells us to embrace Islam. How can we embrace Islam when we have both worshipped idols and shed the blood of the innocent?!'" (The above verses were revealed and opened the doors of repentance to them). [^2]

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir al-Qurtubi, and Tafsir al-Safi under the verse discussed. [^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, vol. 1, p. 127 (under tawbah). [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, vol. 2, Chapter on Repentance, Hadith 10, p. 316. [^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, vol. 2, p. 637 (under wahsh); Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, vol. 27, p. 4; and Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, p. 493. [^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, vol. 9, p. 412 (under the verses discussed) with some abridgment. [^2]: Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr al-Razi, vol. 27, p. 4 (under the verses discussed).

Ayahs

أَن تَقُولَ نَفْسٌ يَـٰحَسْرَتَىٰ عَلَىٰ مَا فَرَّطتُ فِى جَنۢبِ ٱللَّهِ وَإِن كُنتُ لَمِنَ ٱلسَّـٰخِرِينَ (٥٦)
Lest anyone should say, ‘Alas for my negligence in the vicinage of Allah! Indeed I was among those who ridiculed.’ (56)
أَوْ تَقُولَ لَوْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هَدَىٰنِى لَكُنتُ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٥٧)
Or say, ‘Had Allah guided me I would have surely been among the Godwary!’ (57)
أَوْ تَقُولَ حِينَ تَرَى ٱلْعَذَابَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِى كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٨)
Or say, when he sights the punishment, ‘If only there were a second chance for me, I would be among the virtuous!’ (58)
بَلَىٰ قَدْ جَآءَتْكَ ءَايَـٰتِى فَكَذَّبْتَ بِهَا وَٱسْتَكْبَرْتَ وَكُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٩)
[They will be told,] ‘Yes, My signs certainly came to you, but you denied them and acted arrogantly and you were among the faithless.’ (59)

Commentary

On That Day, Regret is Futile

In the preceding verses, a firm command was given for repentance, reformation, and the compensation of past deeds. The verses under discussion follow that context, stating first: "These instructions were given lest anyone should say on the Day of Resurrection: 'Alas for me for the shortcomings I committed regarding the command of Allah! And indeed, I was among those who mocked [His signs and His messengers]!'" (أَنْ تَقُولَ نَفْسٌ يا حَسْرَتى‌ عَلى‌ ما فَرَّطْتُ فِي جَنْبِ اللَّهِ وَ إِنْ كُنْتُ لَمِنَ السَّاخِرِينَ‌) [^1]

"Ya Hasrata" was originally "Ya Hasrati" (the word hasrat is annexed to the first-person singular pronoun "my"). Hasrat refers to the grief and sorrow felt over things that have been lost, resulting in deep regret.

Al-Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: This word is derived from the root hasr (rhyming with habs), meaning to strip or set aside a garment. Since in cases of remorse and sorrow over the past, it is as if the veils of ignorance have been set aside, this expression is used.

Indeed, when a human being enters the arena of the Gathering and sees before his eyes the result of his neglect, negligence, wrongdoings, and taking serious matters as a joke, his cry of "Woe is my regret!" rises. A heavy sorrow coupled with profound remorse overshadows his heart, and he gives voice to this internal state through the aforementioned sentences.

Regarding what "Janb-Allah" (the side of Allah) means here, commentators have offered numerous possibilities.

The reality is that "Janb" literally means "side" or "flank," and is subsequently applied to anything situated beside something else, just as "Yamin" (right) and "Yasar" (left) refer to the right and left sides of the body, and then to anything located in those directions. Here, "Janb-Allah" refers to all matters pertaining to the Lord: His command, obedience to Him, proximity to Him, and the Heavenly Books revealed by Him—all are encompassed in its meaning.

Thus, the sinners express remorse, regret, grief, and sorrow for all the shortcomings they had toward Allah. They specifically point to the issue of mockery and ridicule regarding His signs and messengers, because the primary factor behind their neglect was this very indifference and treating these great truths as a joke due to ignorance, arrogance, and prejudice.

Then it adds: "And lest [the soul] should say: 'If only Allah had guided me, I would have been among the righteous'" (أَوْ تَقُولَ لَوْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ هَدانِي لَكُنْتُ مِنَ الْمُتَّقِينَ‌). It seems the soul says this when it is brought to the scales of reckoning; it sees a group heading toward Paradise with hands full of good deeds, and it wishes to be in their ranks and proceed with them toward Divine blessings.

It further adds: "Or lest it should say when it sees the Divine punishment: 'If only I had another chance [to return to the world], so that I might be among the doers of good!'" (أَوْ تَقُولَ حِينَ تَرَى الْعَذابَ لَوْ أَنَّ لِي كَرَّةً فَأَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ‌). This occurs when the soul is being led toward Hell, and its eyes fall upon the blazing fire and the sight of painful torment. It heaves a sigh from the heart and wishes it were permitted to return to the world to wash away past evils with righteous deeds and take its place among the doers of good.

Thus, each of these three statements by the criminals on the Day of Resurrection is expressed at a different station: 1. Upon entering the scene of the Gathering, it expresses regret. 2. Upon witnessing the reward of the righteous, it wishes for their fate. 3. Upon witnessing the Divine punishment, it wishes to return to the world to compensate for the past.

In response to these three statements, the Quran answers only the second one as follows: "Yes, My signs came to you, but you denied them, and you were arrogant, and you were among the disbelievers" (بَلى‌ قَدْ جاءَتْكَ آياتِي فَكَذَّبْتَ بِها وَ اسْتَكْبَرْتَ وَ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ‌) [^1]. This means: "As for your claim that 'if Divine guidance had come to me, I would have been among the righteous,' what is Divine guidance other than all these Heavenly Books, the messengers of Allah, and the signs of truth in the horizons and within yourselves? You saw and heard all these signs; what was your reaction to them? Denial, arrogance, and disbelief! Is it possible for Allah to punish someone without establishing the proof? Was there any difference between you and the guided ones in terms of Allah's educational programs?"

Therefore, you are the primary culprit, and you have brought this upon yourself—so let the blame be upon you! Among these three actions, "arrogance" is the root cause, followed by "denial of Divine signs," and the result is "disbelief and lack of faith."

But why does He not answer their first statement? Because it is a reality from which there is no escape; they must feel regret and be drowned in grief and sorrow.

As for the third statement regarding the request to return to the world, since it has been answered in several places in the Quran (including Surah Al-An'am, verse 28: "And if they were returned, they would certainly revert to what they were forbidden from; and indeed, they are liars," and also Surah Al-Mu'minun, verse 100), there was no need for repetition.

Furthermore, the answer given to their second statement can also serve as an indication of the answer to this question. For what is the purpose of returning to the world? Is it anything other than the establishment of proof? While Allah has already established the proof for them and left nothing lacking in this regard to be explained a second time. The realization and awakening that criminals experience upon seeing the punishment is a type of "forced awakening" which would not persist if they returned to a normal state. This is exactly like what the Quran describes regarding the polytheists when they are caught in the waves of the sea: they call upon Allah with sincerity, but when He delivers them to the shore, they forget everything: "And when they board a ship, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion. But when He delivers them to the land, at once they associate others with Him" (Al-Ankabut: 65).

Key Points

1. Neglect Regarding "Janb-Allah":

We mentioned that "Janb-Allah" in the verses under discussion has a broad meaning that includes any matter related to Allah. Thus, neglect in this area includes all types of neglect in obeying His command, following the Heavenly Books, and emulating the Prophets and the Awliya (Guardians).

For this reason, in numerous narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, we read that "Janb-Allah" is interpreted as the Imams. Among them, in a narration in Usul al-Kafi from Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of "يا حَسْرَتى‌ عَلى‌ ما فَرَّطْتُ فِي جَنْبِ اللَّهِ", it is stated: "Janb-Allah is Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him), and likewise those who come after him from the successors in the high station, until the matter reaches the last of them (who is Imam al-Mahdi, may our souls be sacrificed for him)." [^1] Also, in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "We are Janb-Allah." [^2] This same meaning is narrated in other reports from other Imams عليه السلام.

As we have said many times, these interpretations are of the nature of identifying the clearest examples. For it is certain that following the school of the Imams is following the Prophet and obeying the command of Allah, as they do not speak of their own accord.

In another hadith, the clearest example of those who will feel regret on the Day of Resurrection is identified as "scholars without action." In the book Al-Mahasin, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام: "Indeed, the people with the greatest regret on the Day of Resurrection are those who described justice but then acted contrary to it, and that is the word of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'Lest a soul should say: Alas for me for what I neglected regarding Janb-Allah.'" [^1]

2. At the Threshold of Death or on the Day of Resurrection?

Are these three dialogues that the criminals have after witnessing the Divine punishment related to the "punishment of eradication" at the end of their lives? Or do they relate to the time of entering the scene of the Resurrection?

The second meaning seems more correct, even though the verses before it relate to the punishment of eradication and the verse after it relates to the Resurrection. A witness to this is verse 31 of Surah Al-An'am, which says: "Those who denied the meeting with Allah have certainly lost, until when the Hour comes upon them unexpectedly, they say: 'Alas for us for what we neglected regarding it!'"

In the aforementioned narrations, we also had evidence for this meaning.

[^1]: The beginning of this verse has an omitted part that links it to the previous verses, and it is implied as "Lest a soul should say..." or "In caution lest a soul should say...". In the second case, it would be the "object of purpose" for "turn [to Him], submit, and follow." The word an in the phrase "وَ إِنْ كُنْتُ لَمِنَ السَّاخِرِينَ" is the lightened form of the heavy anna, and it was originally "Indeed, I was among the mockers."

[^1]: Although the speaker in the previous verses was "soul" (nafs), which is feminine, and frequently in Quranic verses its attributes and verbs appear in the feminine form, in the verse under discussion, the pronoun in kadhdhabta (you denied) and what follows it appears in the masculine form. This is because the intended meaning here is "human being" (insan) or similar terms. Some have also said that "nafs" can be applied to both masculine and feminine.

[^1] and [^2]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 495. [^1]: Ibid, p. 496.

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ تَرَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ وُجُوهُهُم مُّسْوَدَّةٌ ۚ أَلَيْسَ فِى جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوًى لِّلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٦٠)
On the Day of Resurrection you will see those who attributed lies to Allah with their faces blackened. Is not the [final] abode of the arrogant in hell? (60)
وَيُنَجِّى ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ بِمَفَازَتِهِمْ لَا يَمَسُّهُمُ ٱلسُّوٓءُ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦١)
Allah will deliver those who were Godwary with their salvation. No ill shall touch them, nor will they grieve. (61)
ٱللَّهُ خَـٰلِقُ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ وَكِيلٌ (٦٢)
Allah is creator of all things, and He watches over all things. (62)
لَّهُۥ مَقَالِيدُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (٦٣)
To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth, and those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah—it is they who are the losers. (63)
قُلْ أَفَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ تَأْمُرُوٓنِّىٓ أَعْبُدُ أَيُّهَا ٱلْجَـٰهِلُونَ (٦٤)
Say, ‘Will you, then, bid me to worship other than Allah, O you ignorant ones?!’ (64)

Commentary

Allah is the Creator and Protector of All Things

Since the previous verses spoke of the mendacious and arrogant polytheists who will regret their actions on the Day of Resurrection and request to return to this world—a futile and unacceptable request—the verses under discussion continue this discourse by stating: "And on the Day of Resurrection you will see those who lied about Allah with their faces blackened!" (وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ تَرَى الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ وُجُوهُهُمْ مُسْوَدَّةٌ).

It then adds: "Is there not in Hell a residence for the arrogant?" (أَ لَيْسَ فِي جَهَنَّمَ مَثْوىً لِلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ).

Although the concept of "كَذَبُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ" (they lied about Allah) is broad and extensive, in the context of this verse, it primarily refers to attributing partners to Allah and claiming He has children, whether from the angels or Prophet Jesus عليه السلام and the like.

Similarly, the term "مستكبر" (arrogant), though applied to all who are self-important, refers here mainly to those who showed arrogance against the invitation of the Prophets to the true religion and refused to accept their call.

The blackening of the faces of liars on the Day of Resurrection is a sign of their humiliation, disgrace, and scandal. As we know, the arena of Resurrection is the place where hidden secrets emerge and human actions and thoughts are personified. Those who had black and dark hearts in this world, and whose actions were as dark as their thoughts, will find this internal state transferred to their exterior, and their faces will be dark and black.

In other words: on the Day of Resurrection, the internal and external become one, and faces take on the color of hearts. Those who have a dark heart will have a dark face, and those whose hearts are luminous will have faces that are likewise.

As we read in verses 106 and 107 of Surah Al-Imran: يَوْمَ تَبْيَضُّ وُجُوهٌ وَ تَسْوَدُّ وُجُوهٌ فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ اسْوَدَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ أَ كَفَرْتُمْ بَعْدَ إِيمانِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا الْعَذابَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ- وَ أَمَّا الَّذِينَ ابْيَضَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فَفِي رَحْمَتِ اللَّهِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ: "On the Day when some faces will turn white and some faces will turn black. As for those whose faces turn black, [it will be said], 'Did you disbelieve after your belief? Then taste the punishment for what you used to reject.' But as for those whose faces turn white, they will be within the mercy of Allah. They will abide therein eternally."

It is noteworthy that some narrations cited from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام indicate that lying against Allah—which causes the blackening of faces on the Day of Resurrection—has a broad meaning that includes the unjust claim of Imamate and leadership. As Saduq narrates in the book Al-I'tiqadat from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, when he was asked about the interpretation of this verse, he said:

"من زعم انه امام و ليس بامام" (It refers to one who claims to be an Imam while he is not). It was asked: "Even if he is a descendant of Ali عليه السلام and the children of Fatimah عليها السلام?" He replied: "Even if he is a descendant of Ali عليه السلام and the children of Fatimah عليها السلام." [^1] This is, in fact, the mention of a clear instance, for claiming divine Imamate and leadership without reality is one of the most obvious examples of lying against Allah.

Likewise, those who attribute lies to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or the rightful Imam are, in reality, lying against Allah, because they do not speak of their own accord.

Thus, in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever narrates a hadith from us, we will question him about it one day. If he spoke the truth about us, he has spoken the truth about Allah and His Messenger. And if he lied about us, he has lied about Allah and His Messenger, for when we narrate, we do not say 'so-and-so said' or 'so-and-so said'; we only say 'Allah said' and 'His Messenger صلى الله عليه وآله said.' Then he recited this verse: 'وَ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ تَرَى الَّذِينَ كَذَبُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ وُجُوهُهُمْ مُسْوَدَّةٌ...'" [^1]

This hadith clearly shows that the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام said nothing of their own, and all the authentic and reliable hadiths narrated from them trace back to the hadiths of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This is a point worthy of attention for all Islamic scholars; therefore, even those who have not accepted their Imamate should accept their words as narrations from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Consistent with this, another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام is narrated in al-Kafi stating: "The hadith of any one of us Imams is the hadith of the other, and our hadith is the hadith of the Messenger of Allah" (al-Kafi, Vol. 1, Chapter on the Narration of Books and Hadith, Hadith 14).

It is also worth noting that it is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran that the primary source of disbelief is arrogance, as we read regarding Satan:

أَبى‌ وَ اسْتَكْبَرَ وَ كانَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ: "He refused and was arrogant and became of the disbelievers" (Al-Baqarah, Verse 34).

For this reason, the place of the arrogant can be nowhere but the blazing fire of Hell. Even in a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"In Hell, there is a valley for the arrogant called Saqar. It complained to Allah عز و جل about the intensity of its heat and asked Him to allow it to take a breath. It was permitted, so it took a breath and scorched Hell!" [^1]

In the next verse, the discussion turns to the opposite of this group, namely the group of the pious and their salvation on the Day of Resurrection. It says: "And Allah will deliver those who feared Him to their place of success" (وَ يُنَجِّي اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا بِمَفازَتِهِمْ) ^2. Then He explains this success and victory with these two short and meaningful phrases:

"No evil shall touch them, nor shall they grieve" (لا يَمَسُّهُمُ السُّوءُ وَ لا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ).

They live in a world where nothing exists except goodness, purity, joy, and delight. This brief expression, in reality, encompasses all divine blessings.

The following verse returns once again to the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the struggle against polytheism, continuing the dialogues held with the polytheists.

[^1]: I'tiqadat al-Imamiyyah as cited in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 496. The same meaning is narrated from Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim and the book al-Kafi (refer to al-Kafi, Vol. 1, "Chapter on whoever claims Imamate while not being worthy of it," Hadiths 1 and 3).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed.

[^1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 496. The same meaning appears in Tafsir al-Safi under the verses discussed.

He says: "Allah is the Creator of all things, and He is the Guardian and Overseer over all things" (اللَّهُ خالِقُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَ هُوَ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ وَكِيلٌ‌).

The first clause refers to the "Monotheism of Creation" (Tawhid al-Khaliqiyyah), and the second clause refers to the "Monotheism of Lordship" (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah).

However, the issue of the Monotheism of Creation is something that even the polytheists often confessed to, as we read in verse 38 of this same Surah: "If you ask them who created the heavens and the earth, they will surely say: Allah."

But they fell into deviation regarding the Monotheism of Lordship; sometimes they considered idols to be the protectors, guardians, and managers of their affairs, and they would seek refuge in them during hardships. By stating the above, the Quran actually points to the truth that the management of the world's affairs and its preservation and maintenance are in the hands of the One who created it; therefore, one must seek refuge in Him at all times.

"Ibn Manzur" in "Lisan al-Arab" mentions several meanings for "Wakil," including: guarantor (kafil), protector (hafiz), and one who stands to manage the affairs of something.

In this way, it is proven that idols are neither the source of benefit nor harm; they neither untie a knot nor is any knot tied by their hands. They are weak and helpless beings from whom nothing can be achieved.

A group of followers of the school of Determinism (Jabr) have argued using "Allah is the Creator of all things" for their deviant belief, saying: "Our actions are also included in the concept of the verse; therefore, Allah is their Creator, even though the place of their manifestation is our bodily members!" Their great mistake lies here: they have been unable to understand that Allah's creativity regarding our actions has no contradiction with our choice and freedom of will, because these two attributions are in a vertical (longitudinal) relationship, not a horizontal one.

To explain: our actions have a relationship to God and a relationship to us. On one hand, nothing in the entire universe is outside the sphere of Allah's power, and from this perspective, our actions are His creation, because He has given us the power, intellect, choice, tools of work, and freedom of action. From this perspective, our action can be attributed to Him; He willed that we be free and perform voluntary actions, and He placed all the means at our disposal.

But at the same time, we are free and independent in our actions, and from this perspective, our deeds are attributed to us, and we are responsible for them.

If someone says we are the creators of our own actions and Allah has no involvement in them, he is a polytheist, because he believes in two creators: a Great Creator and a small creator. And if he says the creator of our actions is God and we have no involvement, he is deviant, because he has denied the wisdom and justice of God. How can the actions be His while we are held responsible for them? In that case, punishment, reward, reckoning, the Resurrection, obligation, and responsibility would have no meaning.

Therefore, the correct Islamic belief, which is clearly derived from the synthesis of Quranic verses, is that all our actions are attributed both to Him and to ourselves, and these two attributions do not contradict each other because they are two vertical relationships, not horizontal ones (reflect deeply).

The next verse completes the monotheistic discussion of the previous verse by mentioning Allah's Monotheism of Ownership, saying: "To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth" (لَهُ مَقالِيدُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ‌).

"Maqalid," according to most lexicographers, is the plural of "Maqlid" (although Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf says this word has no singular of its own kind), and both "Maqlid" and "Iqlid" mean "key." According to "Lisan al-Arab" and some others, its origin is taken from the Persian "Kilid" (key), and it is used in Arabic with the same meaning. Therefore, "Maqalid al-Samawati wal-Ard" means the keys of the heavens and the earth. [1] This expression is usually a metaphor for ownership or authority over something, just as we say: "The key to this matter is in the hands of so-and-so." Thus, the above verse can refer to both Allah's Monotheism of Ownership and His Monotheism of Management, Lordship, and Sovereignty over the universe.

For this reason, immediately after this sentence, it draws this conclusion: "And those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah, it is they who are the losers" (وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللَّهِ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ‌).

This is because they have abandoned the primary source and true fountainhead of all goodness and blessings and have wandered into dead ends. They have turned away from the One in whose hand are all the keys of heaven and earth and have gone toward helpless beings who can absolutely do nothing.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, it is mentioned that he asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله about the interpretation of "Maqalid." He replied:

"O Ali! You have asked about the great keys. It is that you say ten times in the morning and ten times in the evening: 'There is no god but Allah, and Allah is the Greatest, and Glory be to Allah, and Praise be to Allah, and I seek forgiveness from Allah, and there is no power except by Allah; (He is) the First and the Last, the Manifest and the Hidden; to Him belongs the kingdom and to Him belongs the praise; (He gives life and causes death); in His hand is all good, and He is over all things Powerful.'"

[1] According to some Persian lexicographers: "Kilid," its Arabized forms are "Iqlid," "Iklil," and "Miqlad," is a tool with which a lock is opened and closed (Burhan-e Qati' - footnotes).

"...until the end of the Hadith."

Then he added: "Whoever repeats these words ten times every morning and evening, Allah gives him six rewards, one of which is that Allah protects him from Satan and the army of Satan so that they have no authority over him." [1] It goes without saying that merely uttering these words as a lip service is not enough for all this reward; rather, faith in the content and embodying its character is also necessary.

This Hadith may be a subtle reference to the Beautiful Names of Allah (Asma al-Husna), which are the source of His sovereignty and ownership over the universe (reflect deeply).

From the sum of what was mentioned regarding the branches of Monotheism in the previous verses, it can be well concluded that Monotheism in Worship is an undeniable reality, to the extent that no wise and rational person should allow themselves to prostrate before idols. Therefore, following that, it says in a decisive and stern tone: "Say: 'Is it other than Allah that you order me to worship, O ignorant ones?'" (قُلْ أَ فَغَيْرَ اللَّهِ تَأْمُرُونِّي أَعْبُدُ أَيُّهَا الْجاهِلُونَ‌).

This statement takes on a deeper meaning especially considering that the disbelievers and polytheists sometimes invited the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to respect and worship their gods, or at least to refrain from criticizing and finding fault with the idols. It explicitly declares that the issue of Monotheism and the negation of polytheism is not something that can be bargained or compromised upon; polytheism in all its forms must be crushed and erased from the face of the world!

[1] "Tafsir al-Qurtubi," Vol. 8, p. 5719, and "Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi," Vol. 9, p. 417, under the verses in question (summarized).

The concept of the verse is that idolaters are generally ignorant people; not only are they ignorant of their Lord, but they have also failed to recognize and have trampled upon their own high human status.

The use of "order" and "command" in the above verse is also meaningful, showing that they invited the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to idol worship with a commanding tone and without mentioning any reason or logic! This stance is not surprising from ignorant and foolish individuals.

Is it not ignorance and folly for a human to abandon all these signs of God in the universe—which testify to His knowledge, power, management, and wisdom—and cling to worthless beings that are the source of no effect and the origin of no benefit?

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكَ لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (٦٥)
Certainly it has been revealed to you and to those [who have been] before you: ‘If you ascribe a partner to Allah your works shall fail and you shall surely be among the losers. (65)
بَلِ ٱللَّهَ فَٱعْبُدْ وَكُن مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٦٦)
No, worship Allah, and be among the grateful!’ (66)
وَمَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦ وَٱلْأَرْضُ جَمِيعًا قَبْضَتُهُۥ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ مَطْوِيَّـٰتٌۢ بِيَمِينِهِۦ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ وَتَعَـٰلَىٰ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٧)
They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him, yet the entire earth will be in His fist on the Day of Resurrection, and the heavens, scrolled, in His right hand. Immaculate is He and exalted above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (67)

Commentary

If You Associate Partners with Allah, Your Deeds Will Go to Waste!

These verses continue to pursue the matters related to polytheism (shirk) and monotheism (tawhid) that were discussed in the preceding verses.

In the first verse, the danger of polytheism is recounted with a decisive tone, stating: "And it was already revealed to you and to those before you that if you should associate [anything with Allah], your work would surely become worthless, and you would surely be among the losers" (وَلَقَدْ أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ وَلَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ).

Thus, polytheism has two dangerous consequences, even for the Prophets of God, if—by an impossible hypothetical—they were to become polytheists.

First is the issue of the "nullification of deeds" (habt al-a'mal), and second is falling into the ruin and loss of life.

As for "nullification of deeds," it means the erasure of the effects and rewards of an action due to polytheism, because the condition for the acceptance of deeds is belief in the principle of monotheism, and without it, no deed is accepted.

Polytheism is a consuming fire that burns the tree of human deeds.

Polytheism is a lightning bolt that sets fire to the entire harvest of one's life.

And polytheism is like a storm that disintegrates human deeds and carries them away, as we read in Surah Ibrahim, verse 18: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ أَعْمالُهُمْ كَرَمادٍ اشْتَدَّتْ بِهِ الرِّيحُ فِي يَوْمٍ عاصِفٍ لا يَقْدِرُونَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا عَلى‌ شَيْ‌ءٍ ذلِكَ هُوَ الضَّلالُ الْبَعِيدُ: "The example of those who disbelieve in their Lord is [that] their deeds are like ashes which the wind blows forcefully on a stormy day; they are unable [to keep] from what they earned a [single] thing. That is the extreme error."

Hence, it is narrated in a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"Indeed, Allah the Almighty will call every creation to account except for the one who associated partners with Allah; for he will not be called to account and will be ordered to the Fire." [1]

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 497.

As for them becoming losers, it is because they have lost their greatest assets—intellect, reason, and precious life—in this great marketplace of worldly trade and "purchased no commodity but regret and sorrow!" Here, a question arises: Is it even possible for the great Prophets of God to take the path of polytheism, such that the above verse addresses them with this tone?

The answer to this question is clear: The Prophets will never become polytheists, even though they possess the power and free will to do so. Being infallible (ma'sum) does not mean the deprivation of power and choice; rather, the high level of their knowledge and their direct, continuous connection with the Source of Revelation prevents them from allowing even a momentary thought of polytheism to enter their minds. Would an intelligent and skilled physician, who is well aware of the effects of a highly dangerous and lethal toxic substance, ever intentionally contaminate himself with it while in a sound state of mind?

The goal is to warn everyone of the gravity of the danger of polytheism, so people realize that when Allah speaks to His great Prophets in this manner, the duty of others is clear. In other words, this is like the famous Arabic proverb: "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor!" (meaning: I am addressing one person to make sure the bystander hears).

This same meaning is narrated in a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام) when Ma'mun questioned him about certain verses; the Imam said: "The intent of such verses is the Ummah (the community), even though the addressee is the Messenger of Allah." [1]

In the next verse, to emphasize further, He adds: "Rather, worship only Allah and be among the grateful" (بَلِ اللَّهَ فَاعْبُدْ وَكُنْ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ). [2]

[1]: Same source. [2]: The "fa" in "fa'bud" may be redundant (za'idah), used in such cases for emphasis. Some have also said the sentence is the result of an omitted conditional clause, as if it were: "If you are a worshiper, then worship Allah," then the condition was omitted and the object was placed first.

Placing "Allah" first is for "restriction" (hasr), meaning your object of worship must exclusively be the Pure Essence of Allah. Following this, He commands gratitude, because gratitude for the blessings in which man is immersed is always a ladder toward the "Recognition of Allah" (Ma'rifat Allah) and the negation of all forms of polytheism. Gratitude for blessings is innate to every human being, and for gratitude, one must first recognize the Bestower of blessings; it is here that the path of gratitude leads to the path of monotheism, and idols—which are the source of no blessing—are set aside.

In the final verse under discussion, He turns to another expression to negate polytheism, mentioning the root cause of their deviation: "They have not appraised Allah with His true appraisal" (وَمَا قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ). And for this reason, they degraded His Holy Name to the point of placing Him on the same level as idols.

Yes, the source of polytheism is the lack of correct knowledge regarding Allah. One who knows that: firstly, He is a Being infinite and unlimited in every respect; secondly, the creation of all beings is from Him and they are dependent on the grace of His existence every moment even for their survival; thirdly, the management of the universe, the untying of all knots of difficulty, and all sustenance are in His powerful hand—and even if intercession occurs, it will be by His permission and command—it makes no sense for such a person to turn toward another.

In fact, for such a Being with these attributes, duality is impossible, because two beings infinite in all respects are intellectually impossible (reflect on this).

Then, to describe His greatness and power, He uses two beautiful metaphorical expressions:

He says: "The earth entirely will be [within] His grip on the Day of Resurrection, and the heavens will be folded in His right hand" (وَالْأَرْضُ جَمِيعاً قَبْضَتُهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَالسَّماواتُ مَطْوِيَّاتٌ بِيَمِينِهِ).

"Qabdah" (grip) refers to something held in the fist, and it is usually a metaphor for absolute power and complete authority over something, just as we say in everyday speech, "Such a city is in my hand" or "Such a property is in my grip and fist."

"Matwiyyat" is from the root "tayy," meaning "to fold up," which is sometimes a metaphor for the passing of life or crossing over something.

The above expression regarding the heavens appears more clearly in verse 104 of Surah al-Anbiya: يَوْمَ نَطْوِي السَّماءَ كَطَيِّ السِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ: "The Day when We will fold the heaven like the folding of a [written] sheet for the records."

One who has folded a scroll and held it in their right hand has the most complete mastery over it. Specifically, the choice of "yamin" (right hand) is because most people perform important tasks with the right hand and feel more strength and power in it.

In short: all these similes and expressions are metaphors for the absolute authority of the Lord over the universe in the next world, so that everyone knows that in the realm of Resurrection, the key to salvation and the solution to problems is in the hand of Allah's power, so they do not go after idols and other deities under the pretext of intercession and the like. Is not the earth and heaven in the grip of His power in this world in the same way?

Why does He speak of the Hereafter?

The answer is that on that day, the power of Allah is more manifest than at any other time, reaching the stage of final appearance and disclosure; everyone will clearly realize that everything belongs to Him and is under His control.

Furthermore, some might go after other than Allah under the pretext of salvation in the Resurrection, just as Christians bring up the subject of "salvation" regarding the worship of Jesus Christ (عليه السلام). Therefore, it is appropriate to speak of Allah's power in the Resurrection.

From what we have said, it is clearly evident that these expressions are all metaphorical. Due to the limitation of our words in daily life, we are forced to pour those lofty meanings into the mold of these small words, and there is no place for anyone to assume the anthropomorphism (tajsim) of the Lord from these, unless they are very naive, narrow-minded, and shallow-thinking. What can be done?

We do not have words at our disposal that are worthy of expressing the station of the Lord's greatness; we must use these same words by utilizing metaphorical meanings which have a vast and expansive scope.

In any case, after the above statements, at the end of the verse, He says in a concise and eloquent conclusion: "Exalted is He and high above what they associate with Him" (سُبْحانَهُ وَتَعالى‌ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ).

If humans did not judge His Pure Essence by the small scales of their own thoughts, they would never have fallen into the deviations of polytheism and idol-worship.

Key Points

1- The Issue of "Nullification of Deeds" (Habt al-A'mal)

Is it truly possible for a person's good deeds to be nullified and destroyed because of their bad deeds? Does this issue not conflict with Allah's justice on one hand, and with the apparent meaning of verses stating that if a human performs an atom's weight of good or evil, they shall see it?

There is an extensive discussion here, both from the perspective of intellectual and traditional proofs, part of which we discussed in Volume 2, page 70 (under verse 217 of Surah al-Baqarah), and we will discuss it further under other appropriate verses, God willing. What is necessary to mention here, as raised in the verses under discussion, is that if someone doubts "nullification of deeds" in the face of sins, they will have no doubt regarding "polytheism" and its effect on nullifying deeds. This is because many verses of the Holy Quran, some of which were mentioned above, are explicit that "dying upon faith" (muwafat 'ala al-iman) is a condition for the acceptance of deeds, and without it, no action is accepted.

The heart of a polytheist is like salt-marsh land; even if all the seeds of flowers are scattered upon it and life-giving rain falls on it, it has no capacity to nurture a flower, and nothing but weeds will grow from it.

2- Have the Believers Recognized Allah?

In the above verses, we read that the polytheists did not recognize Allah as He deserves, for if they had recognized Him, they would not have followed the path of polytheism. The implication of this statement is that the monotheistic believers have recognized Him as He deserves.

Now the question arises: how is this statement compatible with the famous hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) who says:

"We have not recognized You as is the right of Your recognition, and we have not worshiped You as is the right of Your worship!"

In response, it must be said: Recognition (ma'rifah) has stages. One is the highest stage of recognition, which is perceiving the core of Allah's Essence (kunh al-dhat). This is not possible for anyone, and none but His Pure Essence is aware of the core of His Essence; the famous Prophetic hadith refers to this very meaning.

However, there are stages lower than this that are within human capacity, which are the stage of general recognition of His attributes and the detailed recognition of His actions. This stage is possible for humans, and the command for "Recognition of Allah" refers to this very stage. The verse under discussion speaks of this stage, in which the polytheists are crippled and left behind.

Ayah

وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرَىٰ فَإِذَا هُمْ قِيَامٌ يَنظُرُونَ (٦٨)
And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens will swoon and whoever is on the earth, except whomever Allah wishes. Then it will be blown a second time, behold, they will rise up, looking on! (68)

Commentary

The "Trumpet Blast" and the Universal Death and Life of Servants

In the final verses of the previous discussion, the Resurrection was mentioned; the verse under discussion pursues this same issue by mentioning many of its characteristics. It begins first with the end of the world, stating: "And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will fall dead except whom Allah wills (وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ)."

"Then it will be blown again, and at once they will be standing, looking on [in anticipation of their account, reward, and destiny]!" (ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرَىٰ فَإِذَا هُمْ قِيَامٌ يَنْظُرُونَ).

From this verse, it is clearly understood that at the end of the world and the beginning of the Resurrection, two sudden events occur: in the first event, all living beings die instantly, and in the second event, which takes place after an interval, all humans are suddenly brought to life, stand up, and await their reckoning.

The Holy Quran refers to these two events as the "Blowing of the Trumpet" (Nafkh-i Sur), which is a beautiful metaphorical expression for sudden and simultaneous events. This is because Nafkh means "to blow" and Sur means a "trumpet" or a hollow horn that was typically sounded to signal the movement or the halting of a caravan or an army. Of course, the tones of these two were different; the "stop" trumpet would halt the caravan in its tracks, and the "move" trumpet announced the start of the caravan's journey.

This expression also signifies the ease of the matter, showing that the Great Lord, with a single command as simple as blowing into a trumpet, causes the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth to die, and with another command—similar to the "trumpet of departure and movement"—He brings them all back to life.

We have repeatedly stated that our words, which are coined for our limited daily lives, are too inadequate to precisely describe the realities pertaining to the metaphysical world or the end of this world and the beginning of the next. For this reason, broader and more expansive meanings must be derived from ordinary words, while paying attention to the existing contextual clues.

To clarify: in the Holy Quran, various expressions are used for the event of the end of the world and the beginning of the next:

In numerous verses (more than ten instances), the "Blowing of the Trumpet" is mentioned. [^1] In one instance, it is referred to as "a peck on the Naqur" (Naqr fi al-Naqur), which also means blowing into a trumpet or something similar: "فَإِذَا نُقِرَ فِي النَّاقُورِ فَذَٰلِكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَوْمٌ عَسِيرٌ" (Al-Muddaththir: 8-9).

[^1]: Instances in the Quran where the phrase Nafkh-i Sur appears are as follows: Surah Al-Kahf verse 99, Al-Mu'minun 101, Ya-Sin 51, Az-Zumar 68, Qaf 20, Al-Haqqah 13, Al-An'am 73, Ta-Ha 102, An-Naml 87, An-Naba' 18.

In some instances, the term Qari'ah is seen, meaning a "striking calamity" (Surah Al-Qari'ah, verses 1, 2, and 3).

And finally, in others, the term Sayhah is used, which means a "mighty blast" or "shout," such as verse 49 of Surah Ya-Sin: "مَا يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً تَأْخُذُهُمْ وَهُمْ يَخِصِّمُونَ." This verse speaks of the blast at the end of the world that catches people off guard. And verse 53 of Surah Ya-Sin: "إِنْ كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ." However, this verse speaks of the blast of Resurrection, following which all people are brought to life and presented before the Divine Court of Justice.

From the sum of these verses, it is understood that at the end of the world, a mighty blast causes the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth to die; this is the "Blast of Death."

And at the beginning of the Resurrection, with a mighty blast and shout, everyone is brought to life and stands up; this is the "Shout of Life."

But what exactly are these two shouts? What effect is in the first blast, and what impact is in the second? No one knows but Allah. Therefore, in some narrations describing the "Trumpet" (Sur) that Israfil blows at the end of the world, it is stated: "The Trumpet has one head and two ends, and the distance between the tip of each head to the other is like the distance between the heaven and the earth!" Then, in the continuation of the same narration, we read: "When he blows into the end facing the earth, no living creature remains on earth, and when he blows into the end facing the heaven, all the inhabitants of the heavens die. Then Allah commands death for Israfil, saying 'Die,' and he dies too!" [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 502.

In any case, most commentators have interpreted Nafkh-i Sur as "blowing into a trumpet," which we said are subtle metaphors regarding the nature of the end of the world and the start of Resurrection. However, a few commentators have considered Sur to be the plural of Surat (image/form). Accordingly, they have interpreted Nafkh-i Sur as blowing into the "form," like blowing the spirit into the human body. According to this interpretation, once it is blown into human forms and they all die, and once it is blown and they all gain life. [^2] This interpretation, besides being inconsistent with the texts of the narrations, does not align with the verse itself, because the singular masculine pronoun in the phrase "ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرَىٰ" (then it was blown into it again) refers back to it. If it had a plural meaning, a singular feminine pronoun should have referred back to it, saying Nafkh fiha.

Furthermore, blowing into a form is appropriate for the revival of the dead (as mentioned regarding the miracles of the Messiah), but this expression is not used for the seizing of the soul.

Key Points

1- Does the Blowing of the Trumpet occur twice or more?

The famous view among Islamic scholars is twice, and the appearance of the verse under discussion also suggests this. Synthesizing other verses of the Quran also informs of two "blasts." However, some have considered the number to be three or even four blasts.

In this arrangement, the first blast is called the blast of "Terror" (Faz'a).

[^2]: Note that Sur (rhyming with Nur) and Suwar (rhyming with Zuhal) are both plurals of Surat.

This expression is taken from verse 87 of Surah An-Naml: "وَيَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَنْ فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ": "And the Day the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will be terrified."

They consider the second and third blasts to be the blasts of "Death" and "Life," which are mentioned in the verses under discussion and other verses of the Quran. One is called the blast of Sa'q (Sa'q means both fainting and dying) and the other is the blast of Qiyam (Rising). Those who have suggested the possibility of a fourth blast apparently take it from verse 53 of Surah Ya-Sin, which says after the blast of life: "إِنْ كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ جَمِيعٌ لَدَيْنَا مُحْضَرُونَ": "It will be but one shout, and immediately they will all be brought before Us."

And this is the blast of "Gathering and Presence."

However, the truth is that there are no more than two blasts. The issue of general terror and panic is, in fact, a prelude to the death of the world's inhabitants, which occurs following the first blast or the first shout. Similarly, the blast of gathering is a continuation of the same blast of life. Thus, there will be no more than two blasts: the "Blast of Death" and the "Blast of Life."

Another witness to this statement is verses 6 and 7 of Surah An-Nazi'at, where it says: "يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الرَّاجِفَةُ تَتْبَعُهَا الرَّادِفَةُ": "On the Day the blast [that causes a quaking] quakes, followed by the subsequent [blast]."

2- What is the Trumpet of Israfil?

How do its sound waves encompass the entire world? This is while we know that sound waves move slowly and do not exceed 340 meters per second, while the movement of light is more than a million times faster, reaching 300,000 kilometers per second.

It must be said that regarding this subject, like many issues related to the Resurrection, we only have general knowledge ('ilm-i ijmali), and its details—as we said—are not clear to us.

A careful look at the narrations found in Islamic sources regarding the interpretation of the "Trumpet" also shows that, contrary to some people's imagination, the "Trumpet" is not an ordinary horn.

In a narration from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn Zayn al-Abidin عليه السلام, it is stated: "The Trumpet is a great horn that has one head and two ends, and between the lower end which is toward the earth to the upper end which is toward the heaven is like the distance from the depths of the earths to above the seventh heaven; in it are holes equal to the number of the souls of the creatures!" [^1]

In another hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The Trumpet is a horn of light in which there are holes according to the number of the souls of the servants!" [^2]

The mention of "light" here also answers the second question mentioned above, clarifying that this mighty shout is not of the nature of our ordinary sound waves. It is a shout superior and higher, with waves exceptionally faster than light waves, which traverse the expanse of the earth and heaven in a short interval. The first time it is death-dealing, and the second time it is life-giving and revitalizing!

[^1]: La'ali al-Akhbar, p. 453. [^2]: 'Ilm al-Yaqin, p. 892.

The question of how sound can be so death-dealing might have been surprising to some in the past, but today it is not surprising to us. We have often heard that a blast wave can deafen ears, disintegrate bodies, and even destroy houses, lifting humans from their places and throwing them to distant locations. It is frequently seen that the rapid movement of an aircraft—the so-called breaking of the sound barrier—creates such a terrifying sound and destructive waves that it shatters the windows of buildings over a wide radius.

If small examples of sound waves created by humans produce such effects, what effects will that mighty Divine Shout, that great universal explosion, bring about?! For this reason, it is not surprising that there are also waves that, in contrast, are shaking, awakening, and revitalizing, even if imagining it today is not possible for us. However, we have at least seen the awakening of sleeping individuals with a shout or the reviving of unconscious humans with intense shocks. Again, we repeat: with our limited knowledge, we only see a shadow of these matters from afar.

3- Who are the ones exempted?

As we saw in the verse under discussion, it says: all the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth die, then it exempts a group and says: "إِلَّا مَنْ شَاءَ اللَّهُ" (except whom Allah wills). There is discussion among commentators as to who these individuals are.

A group believes they are a collection of the great angels of Allah, such as Jibra'il, Mika'il, Israfil, and 'Izra'il.

A narration also points to this meaning. [^1] Some have added the bearers of Allah's Throne to them (as mentioned in another narration). [^2] Others have considered the souls of the martyrs—who, by the decree of the Quranic verses "أَحْيَاءٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ" are alive and receiving provision near their Lord—to be exempted.

A narration also points to this meaning. [^3] Of course, these narrations are not contradictory. However, in any case, it is clearly understood from the end of some of these same narrations that this remaining group will also eventually die, such that in the entire universe, there will be no living being except Allah, the "Living who does not die."

As for how death is for the angels or the souls of the martyrs, prophets, and saints? It is possible that death for them means the severing of the soul's connection from the imaginal form (qalib-i mithali), or the cessation of the continuous activity of the souls.

4- Are both blasts sudden?

It is clearly understood from the verses of the Holy Quran that both blasts occur suddenly. However, the first blast is so unexpected that a large group of people will be busy with business, disputes, and arguments over property and buying and selling when the first shout occurs, and they all die on the spot, as we read in verse 29 of Surah Ya-Sin: "إِنْ كَانَتْ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَاحِدَةً فَإِذَا هُمْ خَامِدُونَ."

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. [^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 329. [^3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 503 (Hadith 119).

Regarding the "second shout," the expressions in the Quranic verses, including the verse under discussion (فَإِذَا هُمْ قِيَامٌ يَنْظُرُونَ), show that it also occurs suddenly.

5- What is the interval between the two blasts?

Nothing is understood from the verses of the Holy Quran in this regard; only the expression "ثُمَّ" (then) indicates that an interval exists. However, in some Islamic narrations, this interval is mentioned as forty years, [^1] and it is not clear what the measure of these years is—ordinary years or years and days like the years and days of Resurrection?

In any case, reflecting on the Blowing of the Trumpet and the end of this world, as well as the second blowing and the beginning of the next world—considering the hints in the Holy Quran and the greater detail found in Islamic narrations—provides a profound educational lesson for humans. Specifically, it clarifies the reality that at every moment and in every state, one must be ready to welcome such a great and terrifying event, for no specific date has been stated for it, and its occurrence is possible at any time. Furthermore, it begins without any prelude. Therefore, at the end of one of the hadiths related to the Blowing of the Trumpet that we read above, the narrator relates that when the talk reached this point: "I saw Ali ibn al-Husayn (ع) weeping intensely at that moment." The narrator says: "I saw Imam Sajjad (ع) in this state, weeping severely, deeply concerned about the issue of the end of the world, the Resurrection, and the presence of people for reckoning before Allah." [^2]

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 503, Hadith 119. [^2]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

وَأَشْرَقَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّهَا وَوُضِعَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ وَجِا۟ىٓءَ بِٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ وَٱلشُّهَدَآءِ وَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ (٦٩)
The earth will glow with the light of her Lord, and the Book will be set up, and the prophets and the martyrs will be brought, and judgment will be made between them with justice, and they will not be wronged. (69)
وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٧٠)
Every soul will be recompensed fully for what it has done, and He is best aware of what they do. (70)

Commentary

The Day the Earth Shines with the Light of God

In these verses, the discussions regarding the Resurrection that began in the preceding verses continue.

In these two verses, there are seven statements, each of which mentions a point concerning the Hereafter that complements the others or provides a reason for them, possessing a specific internal cohesion.

First, He says: "And the earth will shine with the light of its Lord" (وَ أَشْرَقَتِ الْأَرْضُ بِنُورِ رَبِّها).

Regarding what is meant by this "shining" (ishraq) and illumination by Divine light, various interpretations have been offered, the most important of which are the following three:

  1. A group has said that the "light of the Lord" refers to truth and justice, with which Allah will illuminate the face of the earth on that day.

The late Allamah Majlisi says in Bihar al-Anwar: "That is, the earth shines with the justice of its Lord on the Day of Resurrection, for the light of the earth is through justice." [^1] Others have taken the famous Prophetic Hadith as evidence for this meaning, which states:

"Injustice (dhulm) will manifest as darkness (dhulumat) on the Day of Resurrection." [^2]

Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf has also chosen this meaning, stating: "On that day, the earth will be illuminated by the establishment of justice and the spreading of equity in the accounting and evaluation of good and evil deeds."

  1. Others believe that this refers to a light other than the light of the sun and the moon, which Allah creates specifically for that day.

  2. The distinguished commentator, the author of Al-Mizan, says: What is meant by the earth shining with the light of its Lord—which is among the characteristics of the Day of Resurrection—is the uncovering of the veil (inkishaf al-ghita), the removal of curtains and barriers, and the manifestation of the realities of things and human actions, whether good or evil, obedience or disobedience, truth or falsehood. He then argues for this meaning using verse 22 of Surah Qaf: "You were in heedlessness of this; so We have removed from you your veil, and your sight today is sharp!" (لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هذا فَكَشَفْنا عَنْكَ غِطاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ). It is true that this Divine illumination encompasses everything on that day, but—

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 6, p. 321. [^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani and Ruh al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

—the specific mention of the earth is because the primary goal is to describe the state of the people on the earth on that day.

Of course, these interpretations do not contradict one another and can be reconciled, although the first and third interpretations seem more appropriate.

Undoubtedly, this verse pertains to the Resurrection. If we see that in some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام it has been interpreted as the uprising of Imam al-Mahdi عليه السلام, it is in fact a type of application and comparison. It is an emphasis on the fact that at the time of Mahdi's عليه السلام uprising, the world will become a sample of the scene of the Resurrection, and justice and equity will prevail on earth through that rightful Imam, the successor of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the representative of the Lord, to the extent that the nature of this world permits.

Mufaddal ibn Umar narrates from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"When our Qa'im rises, the earth will shine with the light of its Lord, the servants will become independent of the light of the sun, and darkness will vanish!" [^1]

In the second statement of this verse, the talk is about the record of deeds; it says: "On that day, the records of deeds will be placed [before them], and they will be examined" (وَ وُضِعَ الْكِتابُ). These are records in which all human actions, small and large, are gathered, and as the Quran says in verse 49 of Surah al-Kahf: "It leaves out nothing small or large but has accounted for it" (لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها).
