In the next statement, which speaks of witnesses, it adds: "The prophets and the witnesses—

[^1]: Irshad al-Mufid, according to Tafsir al-Safi and Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verses being discussed. The late Allamah Majlisi has cited the same meaning in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 52, p. 330, with a slight variation.

—will be brought forth on that day" (وَ جِي‌ءَ بِالنَّبِيِّينَ وَ الشُّهَداءِ).

The prophets are summoned to speak about the delivery of their message to the criminals, just as we read in verse 6 of Surah al-A'raf: "We shall certainly question those to whom [the message] was sent, and We shall certainly question the messengers" (وَ لَنَسْئَلَنَّ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

And the "witnesses" are brought to testify in that court of justice. It is true that Allah is aware of everything, but for the emphasis of the levels of justice, the presence of witnesses is necessary.

Who are these witnesses? There is discussion among commentators:

Some have considered them to be the virtuous, the pure, and the just of the nations, who testify both to the delivery of the prophets' messages and to the actions of the people who lived in their era, with the "Infallible Imams" at their forefront.

Others have interpreted them as the angels who are witnesses over human actions, taking verse 21 of Surah Qaf as evidence, which says: "And every soul will come, accompanied by a driver and a witness" (وَ جاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَ شَهِيدٌ).

Some have also interpreted them as the limbs of the body, and the place and time of obedience or disobedience, which are among the witnesses of the Day of Resurrection.

However, it appears that "witnesses" (shuhada) has a broad meaning, and each commentator has pointed to a part of it.

Some have suggested that it specifically refers to "martyrs in the way of Allah," but this seems unlikely, as the context is about witnesses in the Divine court of justice, not "martyrs for the truth," although they too may be in the rank of witnesses.

The fourth statement says: "Judgment will be passed between them with truth" (وَ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْحَقِ).

And in the fifth statement, it adds: "And they will not be wronged" (وَ هُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ).

It is obvious that when the Judge is Allah, and the earth is illuminated by the light of His justice, and the record of deeds—which precisely reflects human actions—is presented, and the prophets and witnesses of justice are present, judgment will be passed only with truth, and in such a court, oppression and injustice have no meaning.

The sixth statement in the following verse completes this point, saying: "And every soul will be paid in full for what it has done" (وَ وُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ).

Not just the reward or punishment for their deeds, but their very deeds will be given to them! And what reward or punishment is greater than for a person's action to be delivered to them in full (note that "wuffiyat" means to pay or fulfill completely) and to become their companion and associate forever.

Who can execute these programs of justice precisely? One whose knowledge encompasses everything. Therefore, in the seventh and final statement, He says: "He is most knowing of what they do" (وَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِما يَفْعَلُونَ).

There is not even a need for witnesses, as He is more knowing than all witnesses; however, His grace and justice necessitate that He summon the witnesses. Yes, such is the scene of the Resurrection for which we must all prepare.

Ayahs

وَسِيقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ زُمَرًا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوهَا فُتِحَتْ أَبْوَٰبُهَا وَقَالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَآ أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّنكُمْ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّكُمْ وَيُنذِرُونَكُمْ لِقَآءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَـٰذَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ بَلَىٰ وَلَـٰكِنْ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ ٱلْعَذَابِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧١)
The faithless will be driven to hell in throngs. When they reach it and its gates are opened, its keepers will say to them, ‘Did there not come to you [any] apostles from among yourselves, reciting to you the signs of your Lord and warning you of the encounter of this day of yours?’ They will say, ‘Yes, but the word of punishment became due against the faithless.’ (71)
قِيلَ ٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٧٢)
It will be said, ‘Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the [ultimate] abode of the arrogant.’ (72)

Commentary

Those Who Enter Hell in Groups

These verses continue the discussion regarding the Resurrection, detailing what was mentioned briefly in the preceding verses concerning the reward and punishment of the believers and the disbelievers.

It begins first with the people of Hell, stating: "And those who disbelieved will be driven to Hell in groups" (وَ سِيقَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا إِلى‌ جَهَنَّمَ زُمَراً).

Who are those who drive them? They are the angels of punishment commissioned to lead them to the gates of Hell. A similar expression appears in verse 21 of Surah Qaf:

وَ جاءَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَعَها سائِقٌ وَ شَهِيدٌ: "And every soul will come, accompanied by a driver and a witness."

The use of the term zumar (groups), which refers to small bands, indicates that they are driven toward Hell in small, scattered groups.

Siqa is derived from the root sawq, meaning to urge or drive forward.

The verse then adds: This process continues until they reach Hell, at which point the gates of Hell are opened, and the keepers of Hell say to them reproachfully: "Did there not come to you messengers from among yourselves, reciting to you the verses of your Lord and warning you of the meeting of this Day of yours?" (حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها فُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها وَ قالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها أَ لَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِنْكُمْ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْكُمْ آياتِ رَبِّكُمْ وَ يُنْذِرُونَكُمْ لِقاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هذا) [^1]. It is clearly understood from this expression that the gates of Hell are closed before their arrival; just like the gates of prisons, as they approach, the gates are suddenly flung open. This sudden sight causes them even greater terror. However, before anything else, they are subjected to a barrage of reproach from the keepers of Hell.

[^1]: Khazanah is the plural of khazin, derived from the root khazn (rhyming with jazm), meaning to preserve or guard something. Khazin refers to a protector or guardian.

They tell them that all the means of guidance were provided for them.

Messengers of your own kind came to you with the verses of your Lord, providing continuous and successive warnings and dangers, and persistently reciting the verses [^1]. Despite this, how did this wretchedness overtake you? Truly, this dialogue with the keepers of Hell is among the most painful punishments they encounter upon entering Hell (instead of the welcome received by the people of Paradise).

In any case, they respond with a short and sorrowful sentence, "They will say: 'Yes, [they came and recited the divine verses to us and warned us sufficiently], but the word of punishment has come into effect against the disbelievers'" and His punishment has overtaken us (قالُوا بَلى‌ وَ لكِنْ حَقَّتْ كَلِمَةُ الْعَذابِ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ‌).

A group of great commentators believe "the word of punishment" (kalimat al-adhab) refers to the statement made by the Lord at the time of Adam's descent to Earth, or when Satan decided to seduce the children of Adam. As we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 39, when Adam descended to Earth, Allah said: وَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ‌: "And those who disbelieve and deny Our signs - those will be companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally."

And when Satan said he would seduce them all—except Your sincere servants—Allah said: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنَ الْجِنَّةِ وَ النَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ‌: "I will surely fill Hell with jinn and men all together!" (As-Sajdah, 13).

In this way, they confess that they chose the path of denying the prophets and rejecting the divine verses,

[^1]: Yatluna (reciting) and yundhiruna (warning) are both in the present continuous tense (mudari'), indicating persistence and continuity.

and naturally, they could have no better fate than this.

It is also possible that "the word of punishment has come into effect" refers to what is mentioned in Surah Ya-Sin, verse 7: لَقَدْ حَقَّ الْقَوْلُ عَلى‌ أَكْثَرِهِمْ فَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ‌: "The word has already come into effect upon most of them, so they do not believe." This refers to the fact that sometimes, due to excessive sin, enmity, stubbornness, and prejudice against the truth, a person reaches a point where their heart is sealed, and no path of return remains for them; thus, the decree of divine punishment becomes certain for them.

However, in any case, all of this stems from the person's own actions, and there is no room for anyone to imagine predestination or a lack of free will from this sentence.

This brief dialogue at the threshold of Hell concludes: "It will be said, 'Enter the gates of Hell to abide eternally therein, and wretched is the residence of the arrogant'" (قِيلَ ادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها فَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ‌). The gates of Hell, as previously mentioned, may refer to gates categorized according to human deeds, where each group is taken to Hell in accordance with their actions, just as the gates of Paradise are. Hence, one of its gates is named "The Gate of the Mujahideen" (Bab al-Mujahidin), and it is mentioned in the words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

Inna al-jihada babun min abwab al-jannah

"Indeed, Jihad is one of the gates of Paradise" [^1]. It is interesting that the angels of punishment, out of all the vile human traits that lead one to Hell, emphasize the issue of "arrogance" (takabbur), indicating that the primary source

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 27.

of disbelief, deviation, and sin is, above all, pride, vanity, and the refusal to submit to the truth. Yes, it is pride that casts thick veils over a person's eyes and deprives them of seeing the radiant face of truth. For this reason, we read in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq and Imam al-Baqir عليهما السلام:

La yadkhulu al-jannata man fi qalbihi mithqalu dharratin min kibrin

"He who has an atom's weight of arrogance in his heart shall not enter Paradise!" [^1]

[^1]: Al-Kafi, Volume 2, Chapter on Arrogance (Al-Kibr), Hadith 6.

Ayahs

وَسِيقَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْجَنَّةِ زُمَرًا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُوهَا وَفُتِحَتْ أَبْوَٰبُهَا وَقَالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَا سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمْ طِبْتُمْ فَٱدْخُلُوهَا خَـٰلِدِينَ (٧٣)
Those who are wary of their Lord will be led to paradise in throngs. When they reach it and its gates are opened, its keepers will say to them, ‘Peace be to you! You are welcome! Enter it to remain [forever].’ (73)
وَقَالُوا۟ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِى صَدَقَنَا وَعْدَهُۥ وَأَوْرَثَنَا ٱلْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ ٱلْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشَآءُ ۖ فَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (٧٤)
They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us inheritors of the earth, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish!’ How excellent is the reward of the workers [of righteousness]! (74)
وَتَرَى ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ حَآفِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ ٱلْعَرْشِ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ ۖ وَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ وَقِيلَ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧٥)
And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne, celebrating the praise of their Lord, and judgment will be made between them with justice, and it will be said, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds!’ (75)

Commentary

And This Group Enters Paradise in Ranks

These verses, which are the final verses of Surah al-Zumar, continue the discussions regarding the Resurrection. Since the preceding verses spoke of how the disbelievers enter Hell, here the discourse turns to how the pious believers enter Paradise, so that through the principle of contrast, the matters become clearer and more evident.

First, it says: "And those who feared their Lord will be driven to Paradise in groups" (وَ سِيقَ الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَى الْجَنَّةِ زُمَراً).

The use of the term "siqa" (derived from the root sawq, meaning to drive or urge forward) here raises a question and has attracted the attention of many commentators. This is because this expression is typically used in cases where an action is performed without inner longing or desire. While this expression is appropriate for the people of Hell, why is it used for the people of Paradise who go toward it with intense longing?

Some have considered this expression to be because many of the inhabitants of Paradise are waiting for their friends.

Others believe it is because the longing to meet the Lord has so captivated the pious that they pay no attention to anything else—even Paradise—[until they are guided toward it].

Some have also said that their mounts are driven swiftly toward Paradise.

While these interpretations are good and not mutually exclusive, there is another point here that may be the primary secret behind this expression: no matter how much the pious are in love with Paradise, Paradise and the angels of mercy are even more eager for their arrival. Just as a host is sometimes so eager to see his guest that he brings him toward himself with a speed greater than the guest’s own pace, the angels of mercy also escort them toward Paradise.

In any case, here too, "zumar," which means small groups, indicates that the inhabitants of Paradise go toward it in various groups that represent the hierarchy of their spiritual ranks.

"Until, when they reach it while its gates have been opened [beforehand], and its keepers—those angels of mercy—say to them: 'Peace be upon you! You have become pure; so enter it to abide eternally!'" (حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُها وَ فُتِحَتْ أَبْوابُها وَ قالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ طِبْتُمْ فَادْخُلُوها خالِدِينَ) [1]. It is interesting that regarding the people of Hell, it says when they reach it, its gates are opened, but regarding the people of Paradise, it says its gates have been opened beforehand. This is an allusion to the special respect and honor accorded to them, just like a welcoming host who opens the doors of his house before the guest arrives and stands by the door waiting for him; the angels of Divine mercy are in the same state.

In the previous verses regarding the people of Hell, we read that the first words of the angels of punishment are a harsh rebuke and reproach: why, despite having the means of guidance, did they end up in this state?! But regarding the people of Paradise, the first words are "peace, greetings, respect, and honor."

[1]: There is a discussion among commentators regarding what the result (jaza) of the conditional clause (إِذا جاؤُها) is. The most appropriate view is that the result is the phrase "قالَ لَهُمْ خَزَنَتُها" (its keepers said to them), and the "waw" (and) is extra (za'idah). It has also been suggested that the result is a deleted phrase, implied as "Peace from Allah be upon you," or that the deletion of the result indicates that the matter is so vast and sublime that it cannot be described. Some have also considered "fatummat" as the result and the "waw" as extra.

And then comes the invitation to enter the eternal Paradise!

The word "tibtum" is derived from the root tayyib, meaning purity. Since it follows the greeting of peace, it is appropriate for it to have a "performative" (insha'i) meaning, i.e., "May you be pure and remain happy and joyful," or in other words, "May these pure blessings be pleasant for you, O pure-natured and pure-hearted ones."

However, many commentators have interpreted this sentence as "informative" (khabari) and said the angels tell them: "You have been purified from every pollution and filth; through faith and righteous deeds, your hearts and souls have become pure, and you have been cleansed of sins and transgressions." Some have even narrated a tradition that at the gate of Paradise, there is a tree from whose base two springs of crystal-clear water gush forth. The believers drink from one spring and their inner selves become pure, and they wash themselves in the other spring and their outer selves become pure. It is then that the keepers of Paradise say to them: "سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ طِبْتُمْ فَادْخُلُوها خالِدِينَ" [1]. It is noteworthy that the term "khulud" (eternity) is used for both the people of Hell and the people of Paradise, so that the first group knows there is no way of escape, and the second group feels no anxiety regarding the cessation of Divine blessings.

In the next verse, He quotes four short and meaningful sentences from them that reflect the ultimate happiness and satisfaction of the inhabitants of Paradise: "And they will say: 'Praise be to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us'" (وَ قالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنا وَعْدَهُ).

In the following sentences, they add: "And He made us inherit the land [of Paradise]" (وَ أَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ).

The meaning of "land" here is the land of Paradise, and the use of the term "inheritance" (irth) is because all these blessings have been given to them in exchange for a small amount of effort; and we know that an inheritance is something for which a person usually has not toiled. Or it is from the perspective that every human has a place in Paradise and a place in Hell; if one becomes an inhabitant of Hell due to their deeds, their place in Paradise is given to others, and if one becomes an inhabitant of Paradise, their place in Hell remains for others. Or it is because they can use it with ultimate freedom, just like an inheritance which a person is completely free to use.

This sentence is, in fact, the objective realization of that Divine promise mentioned in verse 63 of Surah Maryam: "تِلْكَ الْجَنَّةُ الَّتِي نُورِثُ مِنْ عِبادِنا مَنْ كانَ تَقِيًّا" (That is the Paradise which We give as an inheritance to those of Our servants who were pious).

In the third sentence, they express their complete freedom in utilizing the vast Paradise of the Lord as such: "We may settle in Paradise wherever we wish" (نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشاءُ).

It is understood from various verses of the Quran that Paradise is composed of many gardens, hence the Quranic expression "جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ" (Gardens of Eternity) (al-Tawbah: 72). The inhabitants of Paradise settle in them according to their spiritual hierarchy and ranks. Therefore, their freedom is within those vast gardens of Paradise that are at their disposal, not in higher ranks for which they do not see themselves worthy and, fundamentally, never even make such a request.

Finally, in the last sentence, they say: "How excellent and wonderful is the reward of those who work [according to the Lord's commands]" (فَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ الْعامِلِينَ).

This is an indication that these vast bounties are given for a "price" (baha), not for an "excuse" (bahaneh); faith and righteous deeds are necessary so that in their light, such worthiness is attained.

Is this sentence also the speech of the inhabitants of Paradise, or the word of the Lord following their speech?

Commentators have suggested both possibilities, but the first meaning—its connection to the speech of the inhabitants of Paradise—is more harmonious with the other sentences of the verse.

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, which is the final verse of Surah al-Zumar, addressing the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it says: "And you will see the angels surrounding the Throne of God, circling it and exalting the praise of their Lord" (وَ تَرَى الْمَلائِكَةَ حَافِّينَ مِنْ حَوْلِ الْعَرْشِ).

The reference to the state of the angels around the Throne of Allah is either to express their readiness to execute Divine commands, or it refers to a highly valuable inner witnessed state that is attained for the elite and those near to Allah on that day. Although these three meanings are not mutually exclusive, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

Therefore, it says following that: "And judgment will be passed between them in truth" (وَ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْحَقِ).

And since these matters are signs of the Lordship of the Creator and evidence of the worthiness of His Pure Essence for every kind of praise and thanks, in the final sentence it adds: "And it will be said: 'Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds'" (وَ قِيلَ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

Is the speaker of these words the angels? Or the inhabitants of Paradise and the pious? Or all of them? The latter meaning seems more appropriate, because the praise and thanks of Allah is the program of all possessors of intellect and thought and all the elite and those near [to Him], and the use of the passive verb "qila" (it was said) also supports this meaning.

O Allah! We too join our voices with all the angels and Your obedient servants, and we thank You for all these blessings You have bestowed upon us, especially for this great blessing of granting us the success to journey through the verses of Your Glorious Quran, and we say: "Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds."

O Lord! We beseech You by Your Great Prophet, the bearers of Your Throne, and all those near to Your threshold, do not separate us from them in this world or the next.

O Lord! Place us among those who, in the light of piety and action, enter Your sublime Paradise in groups and are met with the peace and greetings of Your angels. Amen, O Lord of the worlds.

End of Surah al-Zumar and end of Volume 19 of Tafsir Nemouneh. 3rd of Dhu al-Hijjah 1404 (August 28, 1984).
